# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions below are modeled on **KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Examination (ESE)** patterns: Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks with internal choice), mapped to **Course Outcomes CO1–CO3** and **Bloom's Levels L1–L4 (Remember, Understand, Apply, Analyze)**.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]**
**CO1, Bloom L1 (Remember):** List **any four** ways in which prototypes are used in interactive systems design.

**Model Answer:**

Prototypes are used in interactive systems design for the following purposes:

1. **Communication** — to convey design ideas to clients, users, developers, and management in a concrete, shared form.
2. **Design Verification** — to systematically check that the design conforms to requirements, usability heuristics, and accessibility standards.
3. **Requirements Analysis and Refinement** — to probe and clarify what users actually need through tangible artifacts.
4. **Learning and Exploration** — to investigate the technical and design feasibility of an approach before committing to a full build.

*(Any four of the above earn full marks. Mentioning *validation* or *user testing* alongside is acceptable as a fifth.)*

---

### Question A2

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]**
**CO2, Bloom L2 (Understand):** Differentiate between **verification** and **validation** in the context of design evaluation.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Verification | Validation |
|--------|--------------|------------|
| **Question answered** | Are we building the design **right**? | Are we building the **right** design? |
| **Focus** | Internal — conformity to specification, heuristics, standards | External — meeting real user needs in real contexts |
| **Method** | Inspection: heuristic evaluation, cognitive walkthrough, standards check | Empirical: user testing, field studies, A/B testing |
| **Who is involved** | Experts / inspectors | Real users |
| **When** | After design, before coding | Throughout, especially after iteration |
| **Example** | Checking that all error states have recovery actions per spec | Watching 5 patients book an appointment to see if the flow actually works |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2, Q1(a)]**
**CO2, CO3, Bloom L3–L4 (Apply, Analyze):**

**(a)** *[7 marks]* Explain in detail **Nielsen's 10 usability heuristics** and describe how each can be used to perform a **heuristic evaluation** of a prototype for an interactive mobile application.

**(b)** *[7 marks]* A startup is developing a **food-delivery app** for the Indian market. The team has only **5 days** before pitching to investors and has a **medium-fidelity Figma prototype** of the order-flow (browse → cart → checkout → payment confirmation). Apply the **guidelines for building prototypes for communication** to recommend: (i) the **right level of fidelity** for the demo, (ii) the **key screens** to include, and (iii) a **walkthrough script** outline.

#### Model Solution — (a) [7 marks]

**Nielsen's 10 Usability Heuristics** (introduced by Jakob Nielsen, 1994, foundational to KTU's HCI syllabus):

1. **Visibility of system status** — The system should always keep users informed about what is going on, through appropriate feedback within reasonable time.
2. **Match between system and the real world** — Speak the users' language; follow real-world conventions; make information appear in a natural and logical order.
3. **User control and freedom** — Users often choose system functions by mistake and need a clearly marked "emergency exit" to leave the unwanted state without having to go through an extended dialogue.
4. **Consistency and standards** — Users should not have to wonder whether different words, situations, or actions mean the same thing; follow platform conventions.
5. **Error prevention** — Even better than good error messages is a careful design that prevents a problem from occurring in the first place.
6. **Recognition rather than recall** — Minimize the user's memory load by making objects, actions, and options visible.
7. **Flexibility and efficiency of use** — Accelerators (often unseen by the novice user) may often speed up the interaction for the expert user.
8. **Aesthetic and minimalist design** — Interfaces should not contain information that is irrelevant or rarely needed; every extra unit of information competes with relevant units.
9. **Help users recognize, diagnose, and recover from errors** — Error messages should be expressed in plain language, precisely indicate the problem, and constructively suggest a solution.
10. **Help and documentation** — Even though it is better if the system can be used without documentation, help and documentation may be necessary; such information should be easy to search, focused on the user's task, and list concrete steps.

**Heuristic Evaluation Process Applied to a Mobile App Prototype:**

- **Step 1:** Recruit 3–5 usability experts. Brief them on the app's purpose, target users, and the 10 heuristics.
- **Step 2:** Each evaluator independently walks through the prototype, attempting representative tasks (e.g., "Sign up," "Place an order").
- **Step 3:** For each issue found, the evaluator records the violated heuristic, the screen/element, a one-sentence description, and a severity rating (0–4 per Nielsen's scale).
- **Step 4:** Aggregator merges duplicate issues, sorts by severity, and produces a master list.
- **Step 5:** Design team fixes severity 3 and 4 issues first, then iterates.
- **Output:** A **Heuristic Evaluation Report** that the team can act on *before* user testing or developer handoff.

#### Model Solution — (b) [7 marks]

**Context:** A 5-day, investor-pitch demo of a food-delivery app. Audience: investors (non-technical, vision-focused, time-poor). Current artifact: a medium-fidelity Figma prototype of the order flow.

**Applying the communication-prototype guidelines:**

**(i) Recommended fidelity — High fidelity, but only for the order flow.**

- The audience is **investors**, whose primary concerns are *brand impression* and *concept clarity*.
- Time is **tight (5 days)**, so polishing every screen is infeasible.
- **Rule of thumb (KTU):** Use the *lowest* fidelity that will inform the audience. Here, the audience's information need is **vision and wow-factor**, which medium-fidelity Figma cannot deliver.
- **Recommendation:** Upgrade the **order-flow screens only** to high fidelity (app branding, realistic food images, polished typography, fully clickable), while keeping auxiliary screens (settings, profile) at low fidelity or as gray-boxed placeholders.

**(ii) Key screens to include (4–5 total):**

1. **Splash + Home** — restaurant list with images, ratings, and categories.
2. **Restaurant Menu** — dish list with prices, "Add to cart" CTAs.
3. **Cart** — item list, subtotal, delivery address selector, "Proceed to checkout" button.
4. **Checkout + Payment** — address, payment method (UPI / Card / COD), confirm order.
5. **Order Confirmation + Tracking** — animated success screen with ETA.

**(iii) Walkthrough script outline (5 minutes):**

| Time | Step | What to Show | What to Say |
|------|------|--------------|-------------|
| 0:00 | Hook | Logo, tagline | "Meet *BiteBuddy* — food from your favorite local restaurants in 30 minutes." |
| 0:30 | Persona + Context | Slide with persona *Rohit, 26, working professional* | "Rohit has a 30-minute lunch break and is hungry. Let's watch him order." |
| 1:00 | Browse | Open home, scroll, tap a restaurant | "He browses top-rated restaurants near his office." |
| 1:30 | Order | Open menu, add 2 items to cart | "He picks his usual — butter chicken and a naan." |
| 2:30 | Checkout | Cart → address → UPI payment | "One-tap UPI payment — no card details, no friction." |
| 3:30 | Confirmation + Tracking | Animated success + map | "Order confirmed. Live tracking begins in 30 seconds." |
| 4:30 | Close | Recap screen | "Time to order: under 60 seconds. Time to deliver: under 30 minutes. That's *BiteBuddy*." |
| 5:00 | Questions | Logo slide | Pause for investor questions. |

**Key communication-prototype principles applied:**
- **Storytelling:** Persona-based scenario, not screen-by-screen demo.
- **Right fidelity:** High only on the journey that matters.
- **Time-bounded:** 5 minutes = investors stay engaged.
- **Invite questions:** Ends with open Q&A, not "Any thoughts?"
- **Annotate the *why*:** Script explains *outcomes*, not just *clicks*.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2, Q1(b)]**
**CO1, CO3, Bloom L3–L4 (Apply, Analyze):**

**(a)** *[7 marks]* Compare and contrast the following **prototype inspection methods** in the context of design verification: **heuristic evaluation**, **cognitive walkthrough**, **pluralistic walkthrough**, and **standards inspection**. For each method, state the **purpose**, **conductors**, **process**, and **typical output**.

**(b)** *[7 marks]* A healthcare-tech team has built a **medium-fidelity prototype** of a **telemedicine consultation app** for elderly patients (ages 65+). They wish to perform a **design verification** before the November 2024 launch. Recommend the **most suitable inspection method(s)**, justify your choice with at least **three reasons**, and outline a **step-by-step verification plan** for the team.

#### Model Solution — (a) [7 marks]

**Comparison of Four Prototype Inspection Methods for Design Verification**

| Method | Purpose | Conductors | Process | Typical Output |
|--------|---------|------------|---------|----------------|
| **Heuristic Evaluation** | Identify broad usability issues by comparing prototype against established heuristics | 3–5 HCI/UX experts (independent) | Each evaluator independently walks through the prototype using the 10 heuristics; logs issues with severity | Master issues list, sorted by severity; heuristic evaluation report |
| **Cognitive Walkthrough** | Evaluate learnability for first-time use by simulating user cognition step-by-step | 1+ HCI expert with task knowledge; sometimes a user rep | For each task, evaluator asks 4 questions at each step (will user try? notice? associate? see progress?) | Per-task success/failure stories; learnability issues |
| **Pluralistic Walkthrough** | Bring together multiple perspectives to surface issues early and build shared understanding | Users, developers, HCI experts, business stakeholders | Group walks through the prototype together, voicing reactions at each step | Group-discussed issues, often captured live on a whiteboard |
| **Standards Inspection** | Check compliance with published standards (e.g., WCAG 2.2 for accessibility, ISO 9241) | Domain experts (e.g., accessibility specialists) | Systematic checklist-based review against the chosen standard's criteria | Compliance gap list with severity and remediation guidance |

**Key contrasts:**

- **Heuristic evaluation** is **broad and fast** but subjective to expert opinion.
- **Cognitive walkthrough** is **task-focused and structured** but slow per task.
- **Pluralistic walkthrough** is **socially rich** but vulnerable to social-desirability bias and dominant voices.
- **Standards inspection** is **objective and benchmarked** but only catches the class of issues the standard covers (e.g., accessibility, not content clarity).

#### Model Solution — (b) [7 marks]

**Context:** Telemedicine app for elderly patients (65+). Medium-fidelity prototype. Design verification needed before Nov 2024 launch.

**Recommended inspection method(s):**

**Primary: Heuristic Evaluation (modified for age-related heuristics).**
**Secondary: Cognitive Walkthrough for first-time-use tasks.**
**Tertiary: Standards Inspection against WCAG 2.2 (accessibility).**

**Justification (at least 3 reasons):**

1. **Elderly users often have age-related usability needs** (vision decline, motor control, lower digital literacy) that align with classic heuristics (visibility, error prevention, recognition over recall) and with **age-specific extensions** like *senior-friendly heuristics* (Fisk et al.). Heuristic evaluation by experts familiar with these is the most efficient broad catch.

2. **First-time-use learnability is critical for this audience.** A 70-year-old may not return to the app after a confusing first session. **Cognitive walkthrough** simulates the *first-time* user step-by-step, surfacing learnability issues that heuristic evaluation might miss.

3. **Accessibility is a legal and ethical requirement** for healthcare apps. **Standards inspection against WCAG 2.2** is necessary to verify that the prototype meets AA-level contrast, keyboard navigation, and screen-reader labeling. This is non-negotiable for launch.

4. **Validation (user testing) is *also* needed, but the question is about verification** — so the recommended methods are *inspection-based*, not user-based.

**Step-by-step verification plan:**

| Step | Activity | Output |
|------|----------|--------|
| 1 | Recruit 4 HCI experts, including 1 specialist in age-related design | Evaluation team formed |
| 2 | Brief experts on app purpose, target users (65+, rural/urban mix, varying tech literacy), and core tasks (book appointment, join video call, view prescription) | Brief document |
| 3 | Run **independent heuristic evaluation** (60–90 min each) using Nielsen's 10 + age-specific heuristics | Per-evaluator issue list |
| 4 | **Aggregate** issues, deduplicate, sort by severity | Master issues list |
| 5 | Run **cognitive walkthrough** on the top 3 first-time-use tasks: sign-up, book appointment, join call | Success/failure stories per task |
| 6 | Run **WCAG 2.2 standards inspection** (contrast, font sizes ≥16pt, tap targets ≥44×44px, alt-text, ARIA labels) | Accessibility gap list |
| 7 | Combine all three outputs into a **Verification Report**, prioritized by severity | Single source of truth |
| 8 | **Design team fixes** all severity 3 and 4 issues; re-verify | Revised prototype |
| 9 | **Re-verify** with the same methods to confirm fixes and catch regressions | Re-verification report |
| 10 | **Proceed to user testing (validation)** with 5–8 elderly users for a final reality check | User test findings |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways students LOSE marks on Module 2 questions:**
>
> 1. **Conflating verification and validation.** Examiners explicitly test this. Memorize the "V-V-Spec" vs. "V-V-User" mnemonic.
> 2. **Listing heuristics without explaining their application.** Don't just *name* the 10 heuristics — *apply* them to a specific prototype scenario.
> 3. **Vague severity claims.** "It's a major issue" earns 0 marks. "Severity 3, violating Heuristic 5 (Error Prevention), at the password-strength field" earns full marks.
> 4. **Wrong audience-fidelity pairing.** Pitching to a CEO with a paper sketch, or testing a high-fidelity video with blind users — examiners deduct heavily for this.
> 5. **Confusing throw-away vs. evolutionary prototypes** with low vs. high fidelity. They are orthogonal concepts.
> 6. **Skipping the iteration step.** A verification plan that ends at "report produced" is incomplete — examiners want "report produced → fixes applied → re-verify."
> 7. **Forgetting Nielsen's severity scale values (0–4).** KTU expects the exact integers; don't write "high/medium/low" instead.
> 8. **In communication-prototype answers, forgetting the storyboard / persona.** A "walkthrough" without a *persona* and *scenario* is just a feature demo, not a communication prototype.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 2**

**Core Concepts**
- ☐ Prototype = concrete, experienceable artifact used to *answer design questions*, not to ship a product.
- ☐ Four canonical uses: **Communication, Verification, Requirements Refinement, Exploration**.
- ☐ Verification = "build design *right*" (vs. spec). Validation = "build *right* design" (vs. user).

**Communication Prototypes**
- ☐ **Audience first**, fidelity second.
- ☐ **Lowest fidelity that informs the audience** — the KTU rule.
- ☐ Wrap demos in a **persona + scenario** (storyboard).
- ☐ **Limit scope** — prototype the 2–3 key journeys only.
- ☐ **Annotate** interactions and edge cases.
- ☐ **Invite critique**, not approval.

**Design Verification**
- ☐ **Inspection methods** (no users): Heuristic Eval, Cognitive Walkthrough, Pluralistic Walkthrough, Standards Inspection, Feature Inspection.
- ☐ **Heuristic Evaluation** = 3–5 experts, independent, Nielsen's 10 heuristics, severity 0–4.
- ☐ **Cognitive Walkthrough** = 4 questions per step (try / notice / associate / see progress).
- ☐ **Nielsen's severity scale:** 0 (none), 1 (cosmetic), 2 (minor), 3 (major), 4 (catastrophe).
- ☐ **Fix severity 3 and 4 first.**
- ☐ **Iterate** — verify → fix → re-verify.
- ☐ **Standards inspection** (e.g., WCAG 2.2) is mandatory for accessibility.

**Frameworks & Mnemonics**
- ☐ **V-V-Spec** = Verification. **V-V-User** = Validation.
- ☐ **Gould & Lewis's 3 rules:** Early user focus, empirical measurement, iterative design.
- ☐ **Fix-cost curve:** ~100× more expensive to fix post-release.
- ☐ **Nielsen's 3-5 evaluators rule** for heuristic evaluation.
- ☐ **Throw-away vs. Evolutionary prototypes** are orthogonal to low/high fidelity.

**Exam Pattern Awareness**
- ☐ Part A (3 marks): Definitions, short comparisons, lists of 4–6 items.
- ☐ Part B (14 marks): Process explanations with 7+7 sub-parts, often comparing methods or applying guidelines to a scenario.
- ☐ Always **name the heuristic + state the severity + describe the location** in verification answers.
- ☐ Always **end verification plans with re-verification**, not just "report produced."

**One-line high-yield summary:**
> *Prototypes are inquiry tools. Communication prototypes convey the design; verification prototypes confirm it. Pick the lowest fidelity that informs the audience, inspect with the cheapest method that catches the issue, and iterate until severity 3 and 4 issues are gone.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Technical Performance Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT748 - PROTOTYPING INTERACTIVE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Technical Performance Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Technical Performance Testing** in the context of prototyping interactive systems is a systematic, quantitative, and qualitative evaluation methodology that measures, validates, and verifies whether a prototype meets its predetermined non-functional technical requirements. It focuses on attributes such as **responsiveness, speed, scalability, stability, reliability, and resource efficiency** of a prototype under defined workload conditions.

In the KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework, technical performance testing is mapped to higher-order Bloom's Taxonomy levels — specifically **Analyze (L4), Evaluate (L5), and Create (L6)** — because it requires students to *measure, compare, and judge* the prototype against established benchmarks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Exam Ready):**
> *"Technical Performance Testing is the controlled execution of a prototype under simulated workload conditions to quantify system behavior across critical engineering metrics — including **response time, throughput, latency, frame rate (FPS), CPU utilization, memory footprint**, and **battery consumption** — in order to validate the prototype's fitness for the intended user environment."*

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "New Car Test Drive"

Imagine you have just manufactured a brand-new car (your **prototype**). Before selling it to customers, you wouldn't just check whether the steering wheel turns. You would:

- **Test the acceleration** → analogous to *Response Time / Latency Testing*.
- **Test the top speed** → analogous to *Throughput Testing*.
- **Drive it up a steep hill with 5 passengers and luggage** → analogous to *Load Testing*.
- **Drive it non-stop for 24 hours in desert heat** → analogous to *Stress / Soak Testing*.
- **Check the fuel efficiency on highways and city roads** → analogous to *Resource Utilization Testing*.
- **Verify it works the same on Day 1 and Day 365** → analogous to *Reliability / Stability Testing*.

In the same way, **Technical Performance Testing** of an interactive prototype validates whether the system *feels fast, remains stable, scales gracefully, and consumes resources responsibly* — exactly the qualities a user subconsciously judges within the first few seconds of interaction.

### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Industry-Standard Values)

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Metrics Every KTU Student Must Memorize (Bolded for Quick Recall):**
> - **Response Time (RT):** ≤ **100 ms** — perceived as instantaneous by users (Nielsen Norman Group threshold).
> - **Frame Rate (FPS):** ≥ **60 FPS** for smooth interaction; ≤ **24 FPS** feels sluggish.
> - **Time to First Byte (TTFB):** ≤ **200 ms** for web-based prototypes.
> - **First Contentful Paint (FCP):** ≤ **1.8 s** (Google Lighthouse threshold).
> - **Time to Interactive (TTI):** ≤ **3.8 s** for acceptable interactivity.
> - **CPU Utilization:** should remain below **70%** under normal load.
> - **Crash-Free Sessions:** should be ≥ **99.9%** for production-grade prototypes.

### 1.4 Classification of Technical Performance Testing

| Category | Question Answered | Typical Tool |
|---|---|---|
| **Load Testing** | Does it work under expected user load? | Apache JMeter, Locust |
| **Stress Testing** | What is the breaking point? | Gatling, k6 |
| **Spike Testing** | How does it handle sudden traffic bursts? | BlazeMeter |
| **Endurance / Soak Testing** | Does it remain stable over time? | LoadRunner |
| **Scalability Testing** | Can it grow linearly with resources? | Locust + Cloud |
| **Capacity Testing** | What is the maximum user capacity? | JMeter |
| **Component Testing** | Are individual modules performant? | Chrome DevTools |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Seven-Stage Performance Testing Pipeline

Performance testing is **not a single event** but a structured engineering pipeline. Each stage produces a measurable artifact.

1. **Requirement Gathering & SLA Definition**
   - Identify non-functional requirements (NFRs) such as *"the prototype must respond within 200 ms for 95% of user actions"*.
   - Why? Because you cannot measure a property that has not been defined.

2. **Test Environment Setup**
   - Replicate production-like conditions: hardware, OS, network bandwidth, browser versions.
   - Why? Because testing on a developer's local laptop produces invalid results.

3. **Workload Modeling**
   - Define a realistic mix of user actions (e.g., 40% browsing, 30% searching, 20% form submission, 10% image upload).

4. **Test Script Authoring**
   - Scripts record user actions and replay them at scale using virtual users (VUsers).

5. **Test Execution (Baseline Run)**
   - Run the test once to establish a **baseline** — the reference numbers against which future iterations are compared.

6. **Performance Monitoring & Profiling**
   - Capture server logs, CPU/GPU utilization, memory, network I/O, and database query times.

7. **Analysis, Reporting & Iteration**
   - Compare results against SLAs. Identify bottlenecks. Optimize. Re-test.

### 2.2 Core Formulas (KTU Board-Exam Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **must-know** for KTU 2024 End Semester Examinations. The vertical bar symbol has been written as `\vert` to prevent markdown parsing errors.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Throughput** | $T = \dfrac{N}{t}$ | $N$ = total transactions, $t$ = duration | transactions/sec |
| 2 | **Average Response Time** | $RT_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} RT_i$ | $RT_i$ = response time of $i^{th}$ request | milliseconds (ms) |
| 3 | **Percentile Response Time** | $RT_{p} = \text{value at position } \dfrac{p \cdot N}{100}$ | $p$ = percentile (e.g., 95, 99) | ms |
| 4 | **Frame Rate (FPS)** | $FPS = \dfrac{1}{T_{frame}}$ | $T_{frame}$ = time to render one frame | frames/sec |
| 5 | **Frame Time Budget** | $T_{frame} = \dfrac{1000}{FPS}$ | Derives the allowed ms per frame | ms |
| 6 | **CPU Utilization** | $U_{CPU} = \dfrac{T_{busy}}{T_{total}} \times 100$ | $T_{busy}$ = time CPU is non-idle | percent (%) |
| 7 | **Little's Law** | $L = \lambda \times W$ | $L$ = avg users in system, $\lambda$ = arrival rate, $W$ = avg time in system | dimensionless / per-sec / sec |
| 8 | **Availability** | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | $MTBF$ = Mean Time Between Failures, $MTTR$ = Mean Time To Repair | ratio (0–1) |
| 9 | **Network Latency (RTT)** | $RTT = 2 \times T_{prop} + \dfrac{L}{B}$ | $T_{prop}$ = propagation time, $L$ = packet size, $B$ = bandwidth | seconds |
| 10 | **Amdahl's Law (Speedup)** | $S = \dfrac{1}{(1-p) + \dfrac{p}{n}}$ | $p$ = parallelizable fraction, $n$ = processors | dimensionless |
| 11 | **Error Rate** | $E = \dfrac{N_{err}}{N_{total}} \times 100$ | $N_{err}$ = failed transactions, $N_{total}$ = total | percent (%) |
| 12 | **Packets Per Second (PPS)** | $PPS = \dfrac{B}{S_{packet}}$ | $B$ = bandwidth (bps), $S_{packet}$ = packet size (bits) | packets/sec |

### 2.3 Why These Formulas Matter in Engineering Practice

- **Little's Law ($L = \lambda W$)** is foundational in queueing theory and is used to size servers in cloud infrastructure. KTU often tests this in numerical questions.
- **Amdahl's Law** quantifies the *theoretical maximum speedup* from parallelization. It is heavily used in CPU/GPU performance engineering for interactive prototypes.
- **Availability formula** is critical for safety-critical prototypes (medical, automotive) where uptime is non-negotiable.
- **Frame Time Budget** directly translates user-experience thresholds into engineering budgets. For 60 FPS, the GPU has only **16.67 ms** per frame.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **Smartphone App Prototypes:** Testers use Little's Law to size backend servers for a target user base.
> - **VR/AR Interactive Systems:** Frame Time Budget calculations determine whether a prototype avoids motion sickness (must sustain ≥ **90 FPS**).
> - **IoT Dashboard Prototypes:** RTT calculations help decide between edge vs. cloud processing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Derivation 1 — Frame Time Budget from FPS

**Problem:** A graphical interactive prototype targets 60 FPS. Calculate the maximum time allowed to render one frame.

**Step 1 — Start with the fundamental definition of frame rate.**

$$FPS = \frac{\text{Number of frames rendered}}{\text{Time elapsed in seconds}}$$

**Step 2 — For a single frame, set Number of frames = 1.**

$$FPS = \frac{1}{T_{frame}}$$

**Step 3 — Invert both sides to solve for frame time.**

$$T_{frame} = \frac{1}{FPS}$$

**Step 4 — Convert from seconds to milliseconds (multiply numerator and denominator by 1000).**

$$T_{frame} = \frac{1000}{FPS} \text{ ms}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute FPS = 60.**

$$T_{frame} = \frac{1000}{60}$$

**Step 6 — Perform the division.**

$$T_{frame} = 16.67 \text{ ms}$$

**Result:** The GPU/CPU pipeline has exactly **16.67 ms** to render, layout, and paint every single frame. Any frame exceeding this budget drops the FPS below 60, causing perceptible lag.

---

### 3.2 Worked Derivation 2 — Applying Little's Law to Prototype Sizing

**Problem:** A kiosk prototype is expected to serve an average of 50 users per minute, and each user spends an average of 3 minutes interacting with the system. How many users, on average, are inside the system at any moment?

**Step 1 — Identify the variables.**

$$\lambda = 50 \text{ users/min} \quad ; \quad W = 3 \text{ min} \quad ; \quad L = ?$$

**Step 2 — Apply Little's Law directly.**

$$L = \lambda \times W$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the values.**

$$L = 50 \times 3$$

**Step 4 — Compute.**

$$L = 150 \text{ users}$$

**Result:** On average, **150 users** are simultaneously inside the system. The prototype's server infrastructure must be sized to handle at least 150 concurrent sessions.

---

### 3.3 Worked Derivation 3 — System Availability Calculation

**Problem:** A prototype has a Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) of 800 hours and a Mean Time To Repair (MTTR) of 2 hours. Compute the system availability.

**Step 1 — Write the availability formula.**

$$A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the given values.**

$$A = \frac{800}{800 + 2}$$

**Step 3 — Add the denominator.**

$$A = \frac{800}{802}$$

**Step 4 — Perform the division.**

$$A \approx 0.9975$$

**Step 5 — Convert to percentage.**

$$A \approx 99.75\%$$

**Result:** The system is available **99.75%** of the time — corresponding to roughly **21.9 hours of downtime per year**. If the SLA demands **99.99%** ("four nines"), the prototype fails the requirement.

---

### 3.4 Worked Derivation 4 — Amdahl's Law for Parallelizable Rendering

**Problem:** In an interactive rendering engine, 80% of the rendering pipeline is parallelizable across the GPU cores. If the GPU has 16 cores, what is the maximum theoretical speedup?

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters.**

$$p = 0.80 \quad ; \quad n = 16 \quad ; \quad S = ?$$

**Step 2 — Apply Amdahl's Law.**

$$S = \frac{1}{(1-p) + \dfrac{p}{n}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute.**

$$S = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.80) + \dfrac{0.80}{16}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the denominator terms.**

$$(1 - 0.80) = 0.20 \quad ; \quad \frac{0.80}{16} = 0.05$$

**Step 5 — Add the denominator terms.**

$$S = \frac{1}{0.20 + 0.05} = \frac{1}{0.25}$$

**Step 6 — Final result.**

$$S = 4.0$$

**Result:** The maximum theoretical speedup is **4×**. The remaining 20% (unparallelizable) becomes the bottleneck — no matter how many cores are added, speedup is bounded by $\dfrac{1}{1-p}$.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Performance Test

| Scenario | Best Performance Test Type | Primary Metric | Tool Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 500 users expected to use a web prototype simultaneously | **Load Test** | Response time, throughput | Apache JMeter |
| Server crash suspected at 2000 concurrent users | **Stress Test** | Breaking point, error rate | Gatling |
| Black Friday-like traffic burst | **Spike Test** | Recovery time, error rate | BlazeMeter |
| Long-running stability of a mobile app | **Endurance Test** | Memory leaks, FPS drop | Xcode Instruments |
| Adding more servers should double capacity | **Scalability Test** | Throughput vs. resources | Locust on AWS |
| Single page load time of a prototype | **Component Test** | FCP, TTI, TTFB | Google Lighthouse |
| WebSocket chat prototype over 48 hours | **Soak Test** | Connection drops, memory | k6 Cloud |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Performance Testing Workflow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[StepA: Define NFRs and SLAs] --> B[StepB: Configure Test Environment]
    B --> C[StepC: Model User Workload]
    C --> D[StepD: Author Test Scripts]
    D --> E[StepE: Execute Baseline Test]
    E --> F[StepF: Monitor System Metrics]
    F --> G{StepG: Meets SLA Targets?}
    G -- Yes --> H[StepH: Sign Off and Document]
    G -- No --> I[StepI: Identify Bottleneck]
    I --> J[StepJ: Optimize Code or Infra]
    J --> D
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The loop between *StepD → StepE → StepF → StepG → StepI → StepJ → StepD* represents the **iterative optimization cycle** — performance testing is rarely a one-shot activity.

### 4.2 Functional Architecture of a Performance Testing Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CLIENT_SIDE
        U1[UserA: Simulated User 1]
        U2[UserB: Simulated User 2]
        U3[UserC: Simulated User 3]
    end
    subgraph LOAD_GENERATOR
        LG1[LoadEngine: Apache JMeter]
        COORD[Coord: Virtual User Coordinator]
    end
    subgraph SYSTEM_UNDER_TEST
        APP1[AppServer: Application Server]
        DB1[DataLayer: Database]
    end
    subgraph MONITORING_STACK
        M1[MonA: CPU and Memory Probe]
        M2[MonB: Network Probe]
        M3[MonC: Log Aggregator]
    end
    subgraph REPORTING_LAYER
        R1[ReportA: Dashboard]
        R2[ReportB: SLA Comparator]
    end
    U1 --> COORD
    U2 --> COORD
    U3 --> COORD
    COORD --> LG1
    LG1 --> APP1
    APP1 --> DB1
    APP1 --> M1
    APP1 --> M2
    DB1 --> M3
    M1 --> R1
    M2 --> R1
    M3 --> R1
    R1 --> R2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Functional Mapping Explained:**
> - **CLIENT_SIDE** represents virtual users (VUsers) emulating real-world interaction.
> - **LOAD_GENERATOR** scripts the actions and scales them (e.g., 1 → 1000 users).
> - **SYSTEM_UNDER_TEST** is the prototype being evaluated.
> - **MONITORING_STACK** is the "sensor layer" that captures quantitative metrics.
> - **REPORTING_LAYER** translates raw metrics into SLA compliance verdicts.

### 4.3 Bottleneck Identification Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[PerfIssue: High Response Time Detected]
    Q1 --> C1{Check1: Is CPU at 100 percent?}
    C1 -- Yes --> C2[ActionA: Optimize Algorithm]
    C1 -- No --> C2b[ActionB: Check Memory]
    C2b --> C3[ActionC: Memory Leak Suspected]
    C3 --> D1[ActionD: Profile Heap Dump]
    C1 -- No --> N1[ActionE: Check Network Latency]
    N1 --> N2[ActionF: Check Bandwidth]
    N2 --> P1[ActionG: Check Database Queries]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Define Technical Performance Testing. List any four key metrics used to evaluate an interactive prototype.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Technical Performance Testing is a non-functional testing methodology that quantitatively evaluates a prototype's responsiveness, throughput, scalability, and resource utilization under simulated workload conditions to verify compliance with predefined Service Level Agreements (SLAs).

**Four Key Metrics (2 Marks — ½ Mark Each):**

1. **Response Time** — the total time elapsed between user action and system feedback.
2. **Throughput** — the number of transactions the prototype completes per unit time.
3. **Frame Rate (FPS)** — frames rendered per second, critical for visual smoothness.
4. **CPU/Memory Utilization** — the percentage of system resources consumed during operation.

*(Acceptable alternatives: Latency, TTFB, FCP, Error Rate, MTBF, Availability.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Differentiate between Load Testing and Stress Testing in the context of prototype evaluation.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Load Testing | Stress Testing |
|---|---|---|
| **Objective** | Verify performance under *expected* normal load (1 Mark) | Verify behavior under *extreme* overload beyond capacity (1 Mark) |
| **Load Level** | Up to the SLA-defined peak (e.g., 500 users) | Beyond peak — until the system breaks |
| **Goal** | Confirm SLAs are met | Find the breaking point and recovery behavior |
| **Use Case** | Pre-launch capacity validation | Disaster-recovery planning |

**Conclusion (1 Mark):** Load Testing validates the *design* of the prototype, while Stress Testing validates its *resilience*.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — Performance Metrics & SLA Analysis

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the following performance metrics with one-line definitions and standard threshold values: **Response Time, Throughput, FPS, CPU Utilization, and Availability.** *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A prototype is expected to handle 200 concurrent users. Each user issues, on average, 4 requests per minute, and the average response time is 150 ms. Compute: (i) the arrival rate $\lambda$, (ii) the average number of users in the system $L$, and (iii) comment on whether the system meets the SLA of $\leq$ 200 ms response time. *(7 Marks)*

---

**Solution to (a):**

| # | Metric | Definition | Standard Threshold |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Response Time** | Time elapsed between user action and system response (1 M) | $\leq$ **100 ms** perceived instant, $\leq$ **1000 ms** acceptable (1 M) |
| 2 | **Throughput** | Number of successful transactions per unit time (1 M) | Application-specific; e.g., web servers aim $\geq$ **1000 req/s** (1 M) |
| 3 | **FPS** | Frames rendered per second (1 M) | $\geq$ **60 FPS** smooth, $\geq$ **90 FPS** VR-grade (½ M) |
| 4 | **CPU Utilization** | Percentage of CPU cycles used (½ M) | $\leq$ **70%** under normal load (½ M) |
| 5 | **Availability** | Uptime proportion over a period (½ M) | $\geq$ **99.9%** standard, $\geq$ **99.99%** mission-critical (½ M) |

**Awarded Marks: 7/7** *(Distribute 1 mark per row + 0.5 for value + 0.5 for threshold as shown.)*

---

**Solution to (b):**

**Given:**
- $N_{users}$ = 200 concurrent users
- Per-user request rate = 4 requests/minute
- Average Response Time = 150 ms

**Step 1 — Convert the per-user rate to a system-wide arrival rate (1 M).**

$$\lambda = 200 \text{ users} \times 4 \text{ req/min/user} = 800 \text{ req/min}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to per-second units (1 M).**

$$\lambda = \frac{800}{60} = 13.33 \text{ req/s}$$

**Step 3 — Convert the average response time into seconds (1 M).**

$$W = 150 \text{ ms} = 0.150 \text{ s}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Little's Law to compute L (2 Marks).**

$$L = \lambda \times W = 13.33 \times 0.150 = 2.0 \text{ requests}$$

**Step 5 — SLA Comparison (1 Mark).**

The measured average response time is **150 ms**, which is **less than** the SLA threshold of **200 ms**. Therefore, the prototype **MEETS the SLA**.

**Final Answer:** $\lambda = 13.33$ req/s, $L = 2.0$ concurrent in-flight requests, **SLA is satisfied**.

**Awarded Marks: 7/7**

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **phases of the Performance Testing Life Cycle (PTLC)**. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Using **Amdahl's Law**, compute the maximum speedup of an interactive prototype whose **85%** of the rendering code is parallelizable, executed on a system with **32 GPU cores**. If 95% of the code is parallelizable on the same hardware, what is the new speedup? Comment on the significance of the result. *(7 Marks)*

---

**Solution to (a) — PTLC Phases (7 Marks):**

The Performance Testing Life Cycle consists of **six phases** (diagram + explanation):

1. **Requirement Analysis (1 M):** Identify NFRs, define SLAs, identify critical user journeys.
2. **Test Planning (1 M):** Choose tools, define workload models, allocate test data.
3. **Test Environment Setup (1 M):** Provision hardware, network, and software identical to production.
4. **Test Design & Scripting (1 M):** Author scripts to simulate user workflows.
5. **Test Execution & Monitoring (1 M):** Run the tests, capture metrics in real-time.
6. **Analysis & Reporting (1 M):** Compare against SLAs, identify bottlenecks, document findings.

**Diagram (1 M):** Any structured block diagram with six sequential phases is acceptable.

---

**Solution to (b) — Amdahl's Law Numerical (7 Marks):**

**Case 1 — p = 0.85, n = 32 (3 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Apply Amdahl's Law.**

$$S_1 = \frac{1}{(1-p) + \dfrac{p}{n}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$S_1 = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.85) + \dfrac{0.85}{32}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute denominator.**

$$S_1 = \frac{1}{0.15 + 0.02656} = \frac{1}{0.17656}$$

**Step 4 — Result.**

$$S_1 \approx 5.66$$

---

**Case 2 — p = 0.95, n = 32 (3 Marks)**

**Step 5 — Substitute the new value.**

$$S_2 = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.95) + \dfrac{0.95}{32}}$$

**Step 6 — Compute denominator.**

$$S_2 = \frac{1}{0.05 + 0.02969} = \frac{1}{0.07969}$$

**Step 7 — Result.**

$$S_2 \approx 12.55$$

---

**Significance (1 Mark):** Increasing the parallelizable fraction from **85% to 95%** (only a 10% code refactor) more than **doubles** the speedup (from 5.66× to 12.55×). This proves that **optimizing the serial portion** of a prototype yields far greater returns than merely adding more cores.

**Awarded Marks: 7/7**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch:** Converting minutes to seconds (or vice versa) is where most students lose **1–2 marks**. Always state the unit conversion explicitly.
> 2. **Little's Law Abuse:** Students often write $L = \lambda W$ but plug in mismatched units. Ensure $\lambda$ and $W$ use the *same* time base (e.g., both per-second and seconds, or both per-minute and minutes).
> 3. **Amdahl's Law Neglect:** Forgetting to subtract $p$ from 1 to get the serial fraction is a common error. The denominator is $(1-p) + (p/n)$, **not** $p + ((1-p)/n)$.
> 4. **Missing SLA Comparison:** In numerical problems, computing the answer is only half the job. Always add a concluding statement such as *"the SLA is met / not met"* to earn the final mark.
> 5. **Threshold Value Omission:** For 3-mark definitional questions, KTU examiners award half-marks specifically for the *standard threshold values*. Memorize them.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **⚡ Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read this 5 minutes before the exam):**
> - **Definition (must-memorize):** Performance testing is *quantitative evaluation* of non-functional requirements.
> - **The 7 Tests:** Load, Stress, Spike, Endurance/Soak, Scalability, Capacity, Component.
> - **Core Formulas:** Throughput = N/t, FPS = 1/$T_{frame}$, Little's Law $L = \lambda W$, Availability, Amdahl's Law.
> - **Magic Numbers to Memorize:** **100 ms** (instant), **60 FPS / 16.67 ms** (smooth frame), **200 ms TTFB** (web fast), **70% CPU** ceiling, **99.9%** availability.
> - **Tools:** JMeter (load), Gatling (stress), Lighthouse (web), Locust (scalable), k6 (modern).
> - **Workflow Order:** Requirements → Environment → Workload → Scripts → Execute → Monitor → Analyze → Iterate.
> - **Most-tested Topic:** Little's Law and Amdahl's Law numericals (almost guaranteed in every KTU cycle).
> - **Board keyword triggers:** "**non-functional**, **quantify**, **SLA**, **baseline**, **bottleneck**, **throughput**, **latency**."
> - **Examiner loves:** Numerical problems framed as real-world scenarios (kiosks, mobile apps, VR).
> - **Common mistake to avoid:** Confusing **latency** (one-way delay) with **response time** (round-trip + processing) and **throughput** (volume, not speed).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Safety Standards Testing
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT748 - PROTOTYPING INTERACTIVE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Safety Standards Testing

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Safety Standards Testing** in the context of prototyping interactive systems refers to the systematic, structured, and documented process of evaluating a physical or digital prototype against a defined set of regulatory, technical, and ergonomic safety benchmarks established by national and international standardization bodies. These benchmarks are codified in standards such as **IEC 62368-1** (Audio/Video, Information and Communication Technology Equipment), **ISO 14971** (Risk Management for Medical Devices), **IEC 60950-1**, and **ISO 9241** (Ergonomics of Human-System Interaction).

For KTU 2024 Scheme PECNT748, Safety Standards Testing is the engineering discipline that ensures interactive prototypes — ranging from IoT wearables to embedded HMIs (Human-Machine Interfaces) — do not pose unacceptable risks of **electrical shock, mechanical injury, fire, thermal burns, radiation exposure, ergonomic strain, or data privacy breach** to end users during normal use, foreseeable misuse, or single-fault conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Safety Standards Testing is *not* the same as Quality Assurance (QA) testing. QA verifies *whether the product works as intended*; Safety Testing verifies *whether the product can hurt the user or the environment under any operating condition*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Aircraft Pre-Flight Checklist"

Imagine an aircraft engineer designing a new commercial jet. The prototype is brilliant — it flies, it is fuel-efficient, and the cabin is luxurious. But before passengers board, the aircraft must pass **hundreds of safety tests**: structural fatigue tests, fire resistance of seat materials, lightning strike simulation, bird-strike testing, and emergency oxygen deployment checks. These tests are codified in standards (e.g., **FAA Part 25**, **EASA CS-25**).

**Safety Standards Testing for interactive prototypes works identically.** Before a smartwatch, a medical infusion pump, or an automotive infotainment system reaches a real user, the prototype must be subjected to a battery of standardized tests that simulate:

- What happens if a child chews on the charging cable?
- What happens if the device is dropped from 1.5 meters onto concrete?
- What happens if a software bug causes the touchscreen to lock up while the user is driving?
- What happens if the battery overheats during fast-charging?

The **standards** are the rulebooks; the **tests** are the experiments; the **certificates** (CE, UL, FCC, BIS) are the boarding passes.

## 1.3 The "Three Pillars" of Interactive System Safety

| Pillar | Core Question | Governing Standards |
|---|---|---|
| **Electrical Safety** | "Can the user be shocked or can the device catch fire?" | IEC 62368-1, IEC 60950-1, IEC 60335-1 |
| **Mechanical & Thermal Safety** | "Can the device physically harm the user (cuts, burns, pinch points)?" | IEC 62133, ISO 13850, UL 94 |
| **Ergonomic & Behavioral Safety** | "Can the user make a mistake that leads to harm?" | ISO 9241, ISO 14971, IEC 60601-1-6 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Hazard vs. Risk:** A **Hazard** is a potential source of harm (e.g., a lithium cell). **Risk** is the probability that the hazard causes harm multiplied by the severity of that harm ($R = P \times S$). Safety testing quantifies and reduces $R$ to an *acceptable* level (called ALARP — As Low As Reasonably Practicable).

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics form the *vocabulary* of safety testing and are referenced universally:

- **Mains Voltage Limits:** **$V_{mains} = 230\text{ V} \pm 10\%$** (Europe/India), **$120\text{ V} \pm 10\%$** (North America)
- **Class II Insulation Test Voltage:** **$V_{test} = 1500\text{ V AC}$** (rms, 1 minute)
- **Standard Drop Test Height:** **$h = 1.5\text{ m}$** onto concrete (consumer electronics)
- **Ingress Protection (IP) Rating Scale:** **IP00 to IP69K** (defined in **IEC 60529**)
- **Operating Temperature Range (Consumer):** **$0^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $+35^{\circ}\text{C}$**
- **Acceptable Touch Current:** **$I_{touch} \le 0.25\text{ mA AC}$** (Class II, normal condition)
- **Sound Pressure Limit (Personal Audio):** **$L_{p} \le 100\text{ dB SPL}$** (EN 50332)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Safety Testing Lifecycle (Aligned with Prototyping Stages)

Safety testing is **not a final-stage gate**; it is integrated throughout the prototyping lifecycle. The KTU 2024 scheme explicitly emphasizes **iterative safety evaluation** at each prototype generation.

### 2.1.1 Stage 1 — Concept Stage Safety Analysis
Before any hardware is built, the design team performs a **Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PHA)**. The output is a list of *foreseeable misuses* and *inherent hazards*.

### 2.1.2 Stage 2 — Prototype Stage 1 (Form & Fit)
- **Dimensional compliance:** Does the prototype fit within the human anthropometric envelope defined by **ISO 7250**?
- **Material safety:** Are all skin-contact materials compliant with **REACH** (EU) and **RoHS 3** (Directive 2015/863)?
- **Sharp edges / corners:** Radius of curvature $r \ge 0.5\text{ mm}$ (per IEC 62368-1 clause 6.3).

### 2.1.3 Stage 2 — Prototype Stage 2 (Functional Prototype)
- **Dielectric strength test** (hipot test) on all accessible insulation.
- **Earth continuity test** for Class I devices: resistance $R_{earth} \le 0.1\ \Omega$.
- **Leakage current test** under normal and single-fault conditions.
- **Mechanical strength tests:** drop, impact, and push-pull (forces of **$30\text{ N}$**, **$250\text{ N}$**).

### 2.1.4 Stage 2 — Prototype Stage 3 (Pre-Production / Compliance)
- Full **Type Testing** at an accredited lab (NABL, A2LA, or equivalent).
- **EMC pre-compliance testing** in an anechoic chamber.
- **Usability summative test** (formative testing was done in earlier stages).
- Submission to **Notified Body** for CE marking or to **UL** for North American certification.

## 2.2 The IEC 62368-1 Hazard-Based Safety Engineering (HBSE) Model

The modern paradigm (replacing the older IEC 60950 and IEC 60065) is **Hazard-Based Safety Engineering**. Instead of prescriptive rules, the standard identifies **energy sources** and classifies them:

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Energy Sources (per IEC 62368-1 Clause 5):**
> 1. **Electrical Energy Source (ES):** Class 1, 2, or 3 — classified by voltage and current.
> 2. **Thermal Energy Source (TS):** Class 1, 2, or 3 — classified by accessible temperature vs. burn thresholds.
> 3. **Mechanical Energy Source (MS):** Class 1, 2, or 3 — classified by kinetic energy, sharp edges, moving parts.
> 4. (Plus) **Radiation, Chemical, Biological** energy sources.

For each identified energy source, the engineer must:
1. Classify its **severity** (Class 1 = no injury, Class 3 = severe injury/death).
2. Identify **safeguards** (basic, supplementary, or reinforced).
3. Verify the safeguard's effectiveness by a **standardized test**.

## 2.3 Risk Assessment — The ISO 14971 Framework

The KTU 2024 syllabus treats ISO 14971 as the *gold standard* for risk management of interactive prototypes, especially those with medical, automotive, or child-safety implications.

### 2.3.1 The Risk Equation

The core equation is:

$$R = P \times S$$

Where:
- $R$ = **Risk** (dimensionless score, often 1–25 or 1–125)
- $P$ = **Probability of occurrence** (1 = rare, 5 = frequent)
- $S$ = **Severity of harm** (1 = negligible, 5 = catastrophic)

The **Risk Acceptability Matrix** is a $5 \times 5$ grid. Typically, $R \le 4$ is *acceptable*, $R = 5\text{ to }12$ is *ALARP* (reduce if practicable), and $R \ge 15$ is *unacceptable* (mandatory redesign).

### 2.3.2 Risk Control Hierarchy (in Order of Effectiveness)

1. **Inherent Safe Design** — eliminate the hazard entirely (e.g., use lower voltage, rounded corners).
2. **Protective Measures** — guards, barriers, insulation.
3. **Information for Safety** — warnings, labels, user manuals (least effective — *never* the only safeguard).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** When asked "what is the *best* risk control?", the answer is **always** *Inherent Safe Design*. Warnings and labels are the *last* resort and are heavily penalized in board marking schemes.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Standard Value | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Risk Score | $R = P \times S$ | Dimensionless (1–25) | ISO 14971 risk matrix |
| 2 | Class I Earth Resistance | $R_{earth} \le 0.1$ | $\Omega$ | IEC 62368-1, Class I equipment |
| 3 | Hipot Test Voltage (Class II) | $V_{test} = 1500$ | V AC rms | Dielectric strength, 1 min |
| 4 | Acceptable Touch Current | $I_{touch} \le 0.25$ | mA AC | Single-fault, Class II |
| 5 | Standard Drop Height | $h = 1.5$ | m | Consumer electronics drop test |
| 6 | Finger Probe Force | $F = 30$ | N | Accessibility of hazardous parts |
| 7 | Steady Force (Push) | $F_{push} = 250$ | N | Mechanical strength on enclosure |
| 8 | Burn Threshold (Skin, 1s contact) | $T_{burn} = 44$ | $^\circ$C | IEC 62368-1 thermal classification |
| 9 | Maximum Battery Cell Temp (Discharge) | $T_{cell} \le 60$ | $^\circ$C | IEC 62133 Li-ion safety |
| 10 | SAR Limit (Head, FCC) | $SAR \le 1.6$ | W/kg | IEEE 1528, mobile phones |
| 11 | IP Rating Definition | $IP_{XY} = (\text{Solids } X) \times (\text{Water } Y)$ | — | IEC 60529 |
| 12 | Mains Nominal (India) | $V = 230 \pm 10\%$ | V AC rms | IS 302, IEC 60038 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Convention:** All boundary conditions are written with the $\le$ or $\ge$ operator, *not* strict equality, because safety standards are **inequalities** — they define the *worst acceptable* limit, not a target value.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Safety Standards Testing is the **legal gateway** to global markets:

- **European Union:** CE Marking (under the **Low Voltage Directive 2014/35/EU**, **EMC Directive 2014/30/EU**, and **RED 2014/53/EU**).
- **USA:** UL Listing (voluntary but *de facto* mandatory for retailers) and FCC certification (legally required for RF devices).
- **India:** **BIS (Bureau of Indian Standards)** ISI Mark under **Compulsory Registration Scheme (CRS)**.
- **Automotive:** **ISO 26262** (Functional Safety) for road vehicles — directly applicable to in-vehicle interactive prototypes.
- **Medical:** **IEC 60601-1** for medical electrical equipment and **MDR EU 2017/745**.

For a KTU 2024 PECNT748 student, the practical utility is in **designing prototypes that can pass these tests on the first attempt**, avoiding the costly **redesign loop** that consumes 30–40% of project budgets in industry.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Classifying an Energy Source per IEC 62368-1

**Problem:** A prototype interactive system has a **5 V DC** USB-C charging port. The port is user-accessible. Classify the energy source and determine the required safeguard.

**Step 1 — Identify the Energy Type:**
The port delivers electrical energy → it is an **Electrical Energy Source (ES)**.

**Step 2 — Measure/Determine the Limits:**
The accessible voltage under normal conditions is $V = 5\text{ V DC}$, and the maximum current is $I = 3\text{ A}$.

**Step 3 — Apply the Class 1 / 2 / 3 Thresholds (per IEC 62368-1 Table 5):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ES Class 1 (No injury):} \quad & V \le 30\text{ V rms or } 60\text{ V DC, AND} \quad I \le 0.5\text{ mA rms} \\
\text{ES Class 2 (Painful, not injurious):} \quad & 30\text{ V rms} < V \le 120\text{ V rms \\
\text{ES Class 3 (Injurious):} \quad & V > 120\text{ V rms OR } I > 100\text{ mA rms with } V > 30\text{ V rms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply to Our Values:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= 5\text{ V DC} \le 60\text{ V DC} \quad \text{(passes voltage criterion)} \\
I &= 3\text{ A} > 0.5\text{ mA} \quad \text{(fails current criterion — but only relevant if } V > 30\text{ V)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Final Classification:**
Since the voltage is below the **60 V DC** Class 1 limit, the source is classified as **ES Class 1** regardless of current availability. The accessible energy is not hazardous. Therefore, **no safeguard is required** for the user-accessible port, *but* the internal wiring that connects to mains (e.g., the AC-DC converter) **must** be treated as ES Class 2 or 3 and properly insulated.

**Conclusion:** The USB-C port is **ES Class 1**; the internal mains-connected circuitry is **ES Class 2 or 3**, requiring reinforced insulation.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Quantitative Risk Score for a Prototype Hazard

**Problem:** A prototype wearable fitness tracker has a lithium-polymer (LiPo) battery. Identify a hazard, assess its risk, and recommend a control.

**Step 1 — Identify the Hazard:**
The LiPo cell can undergo **thermal runaway** if overcharged, reaching internal temperatures of $T_{internal} > 150^\circ\text{C}$ and venting flammable electrolyte.

**Step 2 — Identify the Foreseeable Misuse:**
A user plugs in a non-compliant third-party charger delivering $V_{charge} = 6\text{ V}$ instead of the specified $5\text{ V}$ (over-voltage of 20%).

**Step 3 — Estimate Probability ($P$):**
Based on field data from similar devices, over-voltage events occur in approximately 1 in 10,000 charge cycles.
$$
P = 2 \quad (\text{on a 1–5 scale, where 1=remote, 5=frequent})
$$

**Step 4 — Estimate Severity ($S$):**
Thermal runaway can cause fire and 2nd-degree burns (skin contact with electrolyte at $T = 80^\circ\text{C}$ exceeds the **$T_{burn} = 44^\circ\text{C}$** threshold).
$$
S = 4 \quad (\text{on a 1–5 scale, where 1=negligible, 5=catastrophic})
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Risk Score:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= P \times S \\
R &= 2 \times 4 \\
R &= 8
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Interpret the Risk:**
With $R = 8$, the risk is in the **ALARP region** (typically $5 \le R \le 12$). It is *tolerable* only if further risk reduction is *impracticable*. Since practicable controls exist, **a risk control is mandatory**.

**Step 7 — Apply the Risk Control Hierarchy:**

| Priority | Control Option | Effectiveness |
|---|---|---|
| 1st | **Inherent Safe Design:** Add a redundant **battery management IC (BQ29700)** that cuts off charging at $V_{cell} = 4.25\text{ V}$. | **Highest** — eliminates the hazard pathway. |
| 2nd | **Protective Measure:** Add a **PTC resettable fuse** (e.g., $I_{hold} = 1.5\text{ A}$) in series with the cell. | Medium — limits current during fault. |
| 3rd | **Information for Safety:** Label the device *"Use only supplied charger"*. | **Lowest** — assumes the user reads and obeys. |

**Step 8 — Re-evaluate Risk After Control:**
After adding the battery management IC, the probability of over-voltage reaching the cell becomes:
$$
P_{new} = 1 \quad (\text{remote — the IC prevents the fault from propagating})
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{new} &= P_{new} \times S \\
R_{new} &= 1 \times 4 \\
R_{new} &= 4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 9 — Conclusion:**
With $R_{new} = 4 \le 4$, the risk is now **Acceptable** per the ISO 14971 matrix. The design is compliant and may proceed to the next prototype iteration.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Test Selection by Prototype Stage

| Test Type | Concept Stage | Stage 1 Prototype (Form) | Stage 2 Prototype (Functional) | Pre-Production (Type Test) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PHA)** | ✔ Required | — | — | — |
| **DFMEA / PFMEA** | ✔ Recommended | ✔ Required | — | — |
| **Dielectric / Hipot Test** | — | — | ✔ Required | ✔ Required |
| **Earth Continuity ($R \le 0.1\ \Omega$)** | — | — | ✔ Required | ✔ Required |
| **Drop Test ($h = 1.5\text{ m}$)** | — | — | ✔ Recommended | ✔ Required |
| **Ingress Protection (IP Rating)** | — | — | ✔ Recommended | ✔ Required |
| **EMC Radiated Emissions** | — | — | ✔ Pre-compliance | ✔ Required |
| **Thermal Mapping (IR Camera)** | — | — | ✔ Required | ✔ Required |
| **Formative Usability (ISO 9241-11)** | — | ✔ Required | ✔ Required | — |
| **Summative Usability (Validation)** | — | — | — | ✔ Required |
| **Cybersecurity Penetration Test** | — | — | ✔ Recommended | ✔ Required |
| **RoHS / REACH Material Test** | — | ✔ Recommended | ✔ Required | ✔ Required |
| **Battery Abuse (IEC 62133)** | — | — | ✔ Required | ✔ Required |
| **SAR / RF Exposure (IEEE 1528)** | — | — | — | ✔ Required (if wireless) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analytical Principle — The "V-Model" of Safety Testing:** The above matrix follows the **V-Model** of systems engineering. Each design phase (left side) generates a corresponding test phase (right side). Skipping a test on the left requires a *more expensive* test on the right. KTU valuation gives full credit only when students show this **traceability**.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — The Safety Testing Lifecycle (V-Model)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Needs and Use Scenarios] --> B[Concept Definition]
    B --> C[Preliminary Hazard Analysis PHA]
    C --> D[Risk Assessment ISO 14971]
    D --> E[Design with Inherent Safety]
    E --> F[Prototype 1 Form and Fit]
    F --> G[Prototype 2 Functional]
    G --> H[Prototype 3 Pre Production]
    H --> I[Type Test at Accredited Lab]
    I --> J[Certification CE UL BIS]
    J --> K[Market Release]

    C -.PHA Verification.-> I
    D -.Risk Re Evaluation.-> H
    E -.Design Verification Tests.-> G
    F -.Form Factor Validation.-> F
    G -.Functional Safety Tests.-> G
    H -.EMC and Pre Compliance.-> H
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — IEC 62368-1 Energy Source Classification Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Energy Source] --> B{Type of Energy}
    B -->|Electrical| C[ES Class]
    B -->|Thermal| D[TS Class]
    B -->|Mechanical| E[MS Class]
    B -->|Radiation| F[RS Class]
    B -->|Chemical| G[CS Class]

    C --> H{Voltage and Current}
    H -->|V le 30 Vrms or 60 VDC and I le 0.5 mA| I[ES1 No Safeguard]
    H -->|30 to 120 Vrms or 60 to 140 VDC| J[ES2 Basic Safeguard]
    H -->|V gt 120 Vrms or 140 VDC| K[ES3 Reinforced Safeguard]

    D --> L{Surface Temperature}
    L -->|T le 44 C| M[TS1 No Safeguard]
    L -->|44 C lt T le 70 C| N[TS2 Basic Safeguard]
    L -->|T gt 70 C| O[TS3 Reinforced Safeguard]

    E --> P{Kinetic Energy or Sharp Edge}
    P -->|r ge 0.5 mm and low energy| Q[MS1 No Safeguard]
    P -->|moderate energy| R[MS2 Basic Safeguard]
    P -->|high energy or sharp edge| S[MS3 Reinforced Safeguard]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Risk Assessment Decision Tree (ISO 14971)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Hazard] --> B[Estimate Probability P 1 to 5]
    B --> C[Estimate Severity S 1 to 5]
    C --> D[Calculate Risk R equals P times S]
    D --> E{R Score}

    E -->|R le 4| F[Acceptable Proceed]
    E -->|R 5 to 12| G[ALARP Reduce if Practicable]
    E -->|R ge 15| H[Unacceptable Mandatory Redesign]

    G --> I{Control Hierarchy}
    I --> J[1 Inherent Safe Design Preferred]
    J --> K[2 Protective Measure]
    K --> L[3 Information for Safety Last Resort]

    L --> M[Re calculate R new]
    M --> N{R new Acceptable}
    N -->|Yes| F
    N -->|No| H
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Functional Architecture of a Safety Test Station

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Prototype Unit Under Test UUT] --> B[Power Quality Analyzer]
    A --> C[Hipot Dielectric Tester]
    A --> D[Leakage Current Meter]
    A --> E[Thermal Imaging Camera]
    A --> F[EMC Spectrum Analyzer]
    A --> G[Mechanical Force Gauge]

    B --> H[Data Acquisition DAQ Module]
    C --> H
    D --> H
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H

    H --> I[Test Executive Software]
    I --> J[Pass Fail Decision]
    J --> K[Compliance Report PDF]
    J --> L[Flag Non Compliance to Design Team]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:**
> - In **Diagram 1**, dotted lines represent *verification* — confirming that a test at a later stage actually validates an earlier design decision. Solid lines represent *progressive development*.
> - In **Diagram 2**, note the **threshold boundaries** at **$V = 30/120\text{ V rms}$** and **$T = 44/70^\circ\text{C}$** — these are the canonical IEC 62368-1 limits.
> - In **Diagram 4**, the **Test Executive Software** implements the logic of Diagram 3 — a real-world automated safety test rig executes the ISO 14971 decision tree programmatically for every measurement.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]
**Define the term "Hazard" as used in ISO 14971 risk management. How does it differ from "Risk"?**

**Model Answer:**
A **Hazard** is a potential source of harm to a person, property, or the environment. In the context of interactive prototypes, hazards include electrical shock, sharp edges, high temperatures, toxic chemicals, and ionizing radiation.

**Risk**, on the other hand, is a quantitative measure of the probability that the hazard will cause harm and the severity of that harm. It is computed as $R = P \times S$, where $P$ is the probability of occurrence and $S$ is the severity of harm.

In short: a **hazard is a latent condition**; **risk is the activated consequence** of that condition under exposure. A 230 V AC terminal is a hazard whether or not anyone touches it; the *risk* is the probability and severity of injury if a user contacts it.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Many students write "hazard is a dangerous thing" without distinguishing it from risk. This is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction in KTU valuation. Always define *both* terms and give the equation $R = P \times S$.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]
**What is the difference between Class I and Class II equipment in IEC 62368-1? Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Class I Equipment:** Has a *protective earth* connection. The chassis is bonded to ground via the mains earth pin. If the basic insulation fails, fault current flows to earth and trips the circuit breaker. Examples: refrigerators, washing machines, desktop computers with metal chassis.

- **Class II Equipment:** Has *double or reinforced insulation* and **no protective earth**. Safety is provided by insulation alone, not by earthing. Examples: mobile phone chargers (the part with the wall plug), plastic-cased power tools, laptop power supplies.

The class determines the test sequence: Class I requires an **earth continuity test** ($R_{earth} \le 0.1\ \Omega$) and a **hipot test**; Class II requires a **higher hipot voltage** ($V_{test} = 1500\text{ V AC rms}$) and a **leakage current test**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Do not confuse Class II with "double-insulated wires inside a Class I product." Class II is a *whole-equipment* classification, not a wire property.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

### [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Question A (14 Marks):**
**(a)** Explain the ISO 14971 risk management process for an interactive prototype. Describe each step with reference to the Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PHA) and the risk acceptability matrix. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A prototype smart wearable has a 3.7 V Li-Po battery accessible to the user. The enclosure is made of ABS plastic. List **all** the safety tests you would perform before declaring the prototype safe for user trials, and justify each test with reference to the relevant standard. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution:

#### Part (a) — ISO 14971 Risk Management Process (7 Marks)

The ISO 14971 process consists of **seven sequential steps**, applied iteratively throughout the prototype lifecycle:

1. **Risk Management Process Definition:** Establish the organization's risk acceptability policy, including the $5 \times 5$ matrix thresholds and the ALARP principle. Document the scope (e.g., "for the smart wearable fitness tracker, from unboxing to disposal").

2. **Intended Use and Foreseeable Misuse Identification:** Define who will use the device (e.g., adults aged 18–65), where (indoor/outdoor), and how. Identify *foreseeable misuses*: wearing while swimming, dropping from a treadmill, exposure to sweat and skin oils, charging with non-OEM cables.

3. **Hazard Identification (PHA):** Use techniques such as **brainstorming**, **checklists**, and **historical incident databases** to list all hazards. For our prototype: electrical (battery short), thermal (LiPo thermal runaway), mechanical (choking on small parts), chemical (skin irritation from adhesives), radiation (Bluetooth EMF exposure).

4. **Risk Estimation:** For each hazard, estimate $P$ (1–5) and $S$ (1–5) and compute $R = P \times S$. Document the rationale.

5. **Risk Evaluation:** Plot each $R$ in the acceptability matrix. **$R \le 4$** = Acceptable; **$5 \le R \le 12$** = ALARP (reduce if practicable); **$R \ge 15$** = Unacceptable (mandatory redesign).

6. **Risk Control:** Apply the hierarchy — (1) Inherent safe design, (2) Protective measures, (3) Information for safety. Verify each control's effectiveness.

7. **Residual Risk Evaluation and Risk Management Report:** Compute $R_{new}$ after controls. If still in ALARP, perform a *cost-benefit* analysis. Document everything in a **Risk Management Report** and **Risk Management File** as required by ISO 14971 Clause 3.

#### Part (b) — Safety Tests for Smart Wearable Prototype (7 Marks)

| # | Test | Justification & Standard |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Dielectric Strength (Hipot) Test** | Apply 500 V AC between the battery terminals and the user-accessible surface for 1 minute. No breakdown allowed. (Reference: IEC 62368-1 Clause 5.4) |
| 2 | **Drop Test** | Drop the assembled prototype 6 times onto concrete from $h = 1.5\text{ m}$ (1 face, 4 edges, 1 corner). Verify no exposure of live parts and no crack exposing the cell. (Reference: IEC 62368-1 Annex T) |
| 3 | **Thermal Abuse Test** | Subject the Li-Po cell to $T = 70 \pm 2^\circ\text{C}$ for 4 hours. No fire, no explosion, no leakage. (Reference: IEC 62133 Clause 7.3.4) |
| 4 | **Short-Circuit Test** | Externally short the cell terminals through a $R \le 5\text{ m}\Omega$ resistor. Monitor temperature; no fire/explosion. (Reference: IEC 62133 Clause 7.3.2) |
| 5 | **Ingress Protection (IP Rating) Test** | Test to IP rating claimed (e.g., IP67 for sweat/water). Use water immersion and dust chamber. (Reference: IEC 60529) |
| 6 | **Biocompatibility Test** | Extract materials from skin-contact surfaces and test for cytotoxicity, sensitization, and irritation per ISO 10993-5. |
| 7 | **EMC Pre-Compliance** | Radiated emissions scan in a GTEM cell from 30 MHz to 6 GHz. Verify within CISPR 32 Class B limits. (Reference: EN 55032) |
| 8 | **SAR Evaluation** | If the device transmits RF (Bluetooth), evaluate Specific Absorption Rate per IEEE 1528 / EN 62209-1. Limit: $SAR \le 1.6\text{ W/kg}$ averaged over 1 g of tissue (FCC) or $2.0\text{ W/kg}$ over 10 g (ICNIRP). |
| 9 | **Sharp Edge / Point Test** | Apply a **$30\text{ N}$** force with a finger probe; verify no sharp edges become accessible that could cause a cut. (Reference: IEC 62368-1 Clause 6.3) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning (Question A):**
> - **Part (a):** Students often list the 7 steps but **fail to explicitly state the risk acceptability thresholds** ($R \le 4$, $5\text{–}12$, $\ge 15$). This is a mandatory 1-mark point in KTU marking schemes.
> - **Part (b):** A common mistake is to list *only electrical tests* and ignore **biocompatibility, SAR, and EMC**. The KTU 2024 PECNT748 syllabus explicitly lists these. Lose **2 marks** if any are missing.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2

### [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**Question B (14 Marks):**
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a typical Safety Test Station used during prototype compliance testing. List **five** measurements it performs. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **Hazard-Based Safety Engineering (HBSE)** as defined in IEC 62368-1. Compare the HBSE approach with the older prescriptive approach (IEC 60950-1) and give **two** advantages of HBSE for prototyping interactive systems. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution:

#### Part (a) — Safety Test Station Architecture (7 Marks)

A Safety Test Station is an integrated measurement system designed to perform automated compliance tests on prototypes.

**Block Diagram (textual representation for answer sheet):**

$$
\text{Prototype UUT} \rightarrow \begin{cases} \text{Power Quality Analyzer} \\ \text{Hipot Dielectric Tester} \\ \text{Leakage Current Meter} \\ \text{Thermal Imaging Camera} \\ \text{EMC Spectrum Analyzer} \\ \text{Mechanical Force Gauge} \end{cases} \rightarrow \text{Data Acquisition Module} \rightarrow \text{Test Executive Software} \rightarrow \text{Compliance Report}
$$

**Five Measurements Performed:**

1. **Hipot / Dielectric Strength:** Apply high voltage (typically $1500\text{ V AC}$ for Class II) between primary and accessible secondary circuits for **60 seconds**. Measure leakage current. Pass criterion: $I_{leakage} \le 5\text{ mA}$ with no breakdown.

2. **Earth Continuity:** Pass a **$25\text{ A}$ AC** current through the earth path of a Class I device. Measure resistance. Pass criterion: $R_{earth} \le 0.1\ \Omega$.

3. **Leakage Current (Touch Current):** Measure current through a standardized measuring circuit (the *Annex D network*, simulating human body impedance $Z_b = 1500\ \Omega \parallel 0.22\ \mu\text{F}$) under normal and single-fault conditions. Pass criterion: $I_{touch} \le 0.25\text{ mA AC}$ (Class II, normal).

4. **Thermal Mapping:** Use an IR camera to capture the steady-state surface temperature map of the prototype at full load. Compare with IEC 62368-1 Table 38 limits for TS classification.

5. **EMC Radiated Emissions:** Place the prototype in a GTEM cell or semi-anechoic chamber, sweep a spectrum analyzer from **30 MHz to 6 GHz**, and compare with **CISPR 32 Class B** quasi-peak and average limits.

#### Part (b) — Hazard-Based Safety Engineering (HBSE) (7 Marks)

**Definition:**
**Hazard-Based Safety Engineering (HBSE)** is the safety-engineering philosophy codified in **IEC 62368-1** (published 2014, fully replacing IEC 60950-1 and IEC 60065 since 2020). Instead of dictating *prescriptive rules* for every product type, HBSE identifies the *energy sources* in the product, classifies their severity, and mandates that the designer install *safeguards* appropriate to that classification.

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Prescriptive (IEC 60950-1) | HBSE (IEC 62368-1) |
|---|---|---|
| **Approach** | Rule-based: "X mm of creepage, Y mm of clearance" | Hazard-based: identify energy source, classify, then apply safeguard |
| **Innovation** | Restricted; any new topology required a new rule | Encouraged; designer can choose *any* safeguard that meets the safety objective |
| **Coverage** | A/V and ICT equipment only | All electronic equipment, including those with novel form factors |
| **Updating** | Slow; requires amendment cycle | Faster; principles-based, adapts to new technology |
| **Documentation** | Prescriptive checklist | Safety rationale document and energy source analysis |

**Two Advantages of HBSE for Prototyping Interactive Systems:**

1. **Design Flexibility:** For a novel prototype (e.g., a brain-computer interface headset or a flexible-display wearable), there are no IEC 60950-1 rules to follow. HBSE allows the designer to demonstrate safety by *engineering analysis* and *objective evidence*, enabling faster innovation.

2. **Future-Proofing:** As interactive systems evolve (e.g., neural implants, AR glasses), new energy sources appear (e.g., optical radiation, ultrasound). The HBSE framework naturally accommodates these because it is *hazard-based*, not *technology-based*. A new hazard is simply classified and a new safeguard designed.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning (Question B):**
> - **Part (a):** Students often forget to include the **Annex D measuring network** for leakage current, which is a hallmark of a well-prepared answer. Lose **1 mark** if omitted.
> - **Part (b):** Many students confuse "prescriptive" with "good" and "HBSE" with "loose." In fact, HBSE is *more rigorous* because it demands a *safety rationale* document for every design decision. Make this nuance explicit to score full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **Hazard ≠ Risk.** Hazard is the *source*; Risk is the *consequence* ($R = P \times S$).
- ✅ **The Three Energy Sources** (per IEC 62368-1): Electrical (ES), Thermal (TS), Mechanical (MS) — plus Radiation, Chemical, Biological.
- ✅ **Class I vs. Class II:** Earthed vs. Double-Insulated. Test voltages: **$1500\text{ V AC}$** for Class II hipot.
- ✅ **Touch Current Limit:** $I_{touch} \le 0.25\text{ mA AC}$ (Class II, normal condition, measured through the Annex D network).
- ✅ **Drop Test:** $h = 1.5\text{ m}$, concrete, 6 orientations (1 face + 4 edges + 1 corner).
- ✅ **Burn Threshold:** $T_{skin} = 44^\circ\text{C}$ for 1-second contact; $70^\circ\text{C}$ for 8-hour contact.
- ✅ **Risk Matrix:** $R \le 4$ = Acceptable; $5\text{–}12$ = ALARP; $\ge 15$ = Unacceptable.
- ✅ **Risk Control Hierarchy:** Inherent Safe Design (best) → Protective Measures → Information for Safety (worst).
- ✅ **Earth Continuity Limit:** $R_{earth} \le 0.1\ \Omega$ at $25\text{ A}$.
- ✅ **Key Standards to Memorize:** IEC 62368-1, IEC 62133, ISO 14971, IEC 60529 (IP), IEC 60601-1 (medical), ISO 26262 (automotive), IEEE 1528 (SAR).
- ✅ **Certifications:** CE (EU), UL/FCC (USA), BIS/ISI (India), KC (Korea), CCC (China).
- ✅ **V-Model Principle:** Each design phase generates a corresponding test phase. Skipping left-side analysis = expensive right-side test.
- ✅ **EMC Limits:** CISPR 32 Class B for residential equipment; radiated emissions 30 MHz – 6 GHz.
- ✅ **Biocompatibility:** Skin-contact materials need ISO 10993 testing (cytotoxicity, sensitization, irritation).
- ✅ **The $30\text{ N}$ Finger Probe Force** and the **$250\text{ N}$ Steady Force Test** are the canonical mechanical strength thresholds.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
