- **Use the DCI and the Pugh Matrix together**: WTS ranks, DCI validates, Pugh cross-checks.
- **Diagrams are rewarded**: A funnel diagram (Section 4.1) or a decision-matrix block diagram (Section 4.2) easily earns 1–2 bonus marks.
- **Accessibility, Privacy, and Trust** are *high-weight* criteria in any KTU HCI / interactive-system design question — never assign them a weight below 0.20 (20%).
- **Tie-breaking order** (memorize for the exam): (1) DCI margin, (2) Weighted Pugh delta, (3) Stakeholder veto, (4) Cost feasibility.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Prototyping Strategies - Horizontal Prototypes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT748 - PROTOTYPING INTERACTIVE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Prototyping Strategies - Horizontal Prototypes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PECNT748 - Prototyping Interactive Systems**, a **Horizontal Prototype** is defined as a surface-level, breadth-first prototype that simulates a *wide* range of user interface components, screens, and interactions across the *entire* functional scope of the proposed interactive system, while implementing *none* of those functions to full depth. Every screen, menu, and control is "skinned" so the user can navigate the system end-to-end, but the underlying computational, business-logic, or data-processing layers remain stubbed, mocked, or non-functional.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim-style):**
> *A horizontal prototype implements many features of a system to a shallow depth, typically limited to the presentation layer, to demonstrate the overall scope, navigation, and user experience without committing to full implementation of any single feature.*

The defining metric of a horizontal prototype is its **Breadth-to-Depth Ratio (BDR)**:

$$
\text{BDR} = \frac{\text{Number of Features Implemented (Breadth)}}{\text{Level of Functional Depth per Feature (Depth)}}
$$

For a pure horizontal prototype, $\text{BDR} \gg 1$ (ideally approaching infinity in the limit, since depth $\to 0$ while breadth covers the full feature set).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are an architect presenting the design of a 100-floor skyscraper to a client.

- A **Horizontal Prototype** is like building a full-scale, walkable **ground floor** that shows the *entire* lobby, every type of office layout, all restrooms, the cafeteria, the gym, and the helipad — but each room is just a **hollow shell with painted walls and fake furniture**. The client can walk the *whole building footprint* on one level, but cannot actually work, cook, or exercise in any room.
- A **Vertical Prototype** (the natural counterpart) would instead build *one single elevator shaft* going all 100 floors deep, with only the lift working, so the client experiences the building's *true vertical scale* but sees nothing of the side rooms.

The horizontal prototype is a **breadth-only** mock-up; the vertical prototype is a **depth-only** proof-of-concept.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition Summary:**
> A horizontal prototype answers the question: *"Does the system LOOK and FEEL right across every part of the application?"*
> It does NOT answer: *"Does the system WORK correctly in any one specific part?"*

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Characteristics

The following parameters are standard KTU-evaluable descriptors of a horizontal prototype:

| Parameter | Typical Value / Range | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Breadth coverage** | **80–100%** of total features | percent of feature set |
| **Depth per feature** | **10–20%** of full implementation | percent of functional logic |
| **Implementation layer** | **Presentation / UI layer only** | layer name |
| **Data layer** | **Stubbed / mocked** | state |
| **Navigation** | **Fully clickable** | boolean (TRUE) |
| **Time-to-build** | **Short (days to 1–2 weeks)** | duration |
| **Cost-of-change** | **Very low** | relative cost |
| **User feedback type** | **Scope, layout, IA, aesthetics** | category |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked to identify a horizontal prototype in a viva or theory question, always emphasize three keywords: **breadth**, **shallow depth**, and **UI layer only**. Examiners look for these exact phrases for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Decomposition — How a Horizontal Prototype Actually Works

A horizontal prototype is constructed by following a structured, layered approach. The KTU board expects you to be able to describe each step.

1. **Requirement Enumeration (Breadth Mapping):** List *every* feature, screen, dialog, and workflow identified in the SRS (Software Requirement Specification) document. The goal here is *coverage*, not depth.
2. **Screen Skeleton Creation (Wireframing):** For each feature, generate a static or semi-interactive **wireframe** that represents the visual layout — buttons, text fields, lists, menus, status bars.
3. **Inter-Screen Linking (Navigation Mocking):** Wire up hyperlinks, click events, and routing so the user can traverse the entire information architecture. This is the *only* part that needs real interactive behavior.
4. **Stub Data Injection:** Replace the real database / API calls with **dummy data** (Lorem Ipsum text, placeholder images, hard-coded arrays). The data must *look* real but contain *no* business meaning.
5. **Style & Branding Application:** Apply the visual design language — colors, typography, iconography, spacing — so the prototype looks like the final product on the surface.
6. **Feedback Collection:** The prototype is handed to end-users, stakeholders, and the design team. The focus is on **scope verification**, **navigation efficiency**, **information architecture**, and **visual consistency**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this works (The "Why"):** Users and stakeholders often cannot visualize a system from a textual SRS. A horizontal prototype gives them a *tangible, navigable* artifact that makes scope disagreements concrete and resolvable *before* costly backend development begins.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The table below contains every quantitative formula and boundary condition a KTU student is expected to know for problems on horizontal prototypes.

| Concept | Formula / Condition | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Breadth-to-Depth Ratio (BDR)** | $\text{BDR} = \frac{F_{\text{impl}}}{D_{\text{per feature}}}$ | $\text{BDR} > 5 \Rightarrow$ horizontal |
| **Feature Coverage (FC)** | $\text{FC} = \frac{F_{\text{impl}}}{F_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$ | Should approach **100%** |
| **Implementation Depth (ID)** | $\text{ID} = \frac{L_{\text{real}}}{L_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$ | Should be **$\leq 20\%$** for horizontal |
| **Horizontal Threshold** | $\text{ID} < 30\%$ AND $\text{FC} > 70\%$ | Boundary condition |
| **Cost Ratio** | $C_{\text{horiz}} \approx 0.15 \cdot C_{\text{full}}$ | Roughly **15%** of full-build cost |
| **Time Ratio** | $T_{\text{horiz}} \approx 0.20 \cdot T_{\text{full}}$ | Roughly **20%** of full-build time |
| **Vertical Counterpart** | $\text{ID} > 70\%$ AND $\text{FC} < 30\%$ | Depth-first prototype |
| **Effort Distribution** | $E_{\text{UI}} \approx 90\%$, $E_{\text{logic}} \approx 10\%$ | Most effort in presentation layer |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Rendering Safeguard:** Notice that all vertical bars (absolute value, set notation, conditional separators) inside table cells are written using `\vert` or `\mid` — for example, $E_{\text{logic}} \approx 10\%$ is rendered without any `|` character. This prevents the table syntax from breaking.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Horizontal prototypes are indispensable in several real-world HCI and software engineering scenarios:

- **Stakeholder Demos:** Pre-investment pitch decks where investors must *see* the entire product vision.
- **Usability Heuristic Evaluation:** The Nielsen 10 heuristics can be evaluated across a wide surface area in a single sitting.
- **Information Architecture (IA) Validation:** Card-sorting, tree-testing, and navigation-flow validation.
- **Design System Verification:** Confirming that the visual style works across diverse screen types (forms, lists, dashboards, modals).
- **Contract / Scope Negotiation:** Locking down what is in-scope vs. out-of-scope before sprint planning.
- **Accessibility Audits (broad):** Running automated WCAG scanners across all screens at once.

> [!TIP]
> **Cross-Disciplinary Link:** This concept directly connects to *Throwaway Prototyping* (Module 2 of KTU PECNT748) and the *Spiral Model* of software development, where horizontal prototypes are used in early spiral cycles to reduce risk.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Classifying a Given Prototype as Horizontal

The KTU 2024 scheme often presents a scenario and asks you to *decide* whether the described prototype is horizontal, vertical, or hybrid. The decision must be defensible mathematically. Below is the exhaustive derivation of the classification rule.

**Given:**
- Let $F_{\text{total}}$ be the total number of features in the SRS.
- Let $F_{\text{impl}}$ be the number of features actually implemented in the prototype.
- Let $L_{\text{total}}$ be the total number of code/logic lines required to fully implement one feature.
- Let $L_{\text{real}}$ be the number of code/logic lines actually written for that feature in the prototype.

**Step 1:** Define **Feature Coverage (FC)**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FC} &= \frac{F_{\text{impl}}}{F_{\text{total}}} \times 100\% \\[6pt]
\text{FC} &\in [0\%, 100\%]
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* This expresses what percentage of the system's *breadth* is represented in the prototype.

**Step 2:** Define **Implementation Depth (ID)** for an average feature.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ID} &= \frac{L_{\text{real}}}{L_{\text{total}}} \times 100\% \\[6pt]
\text{ID} &\in [0\%, 100\%]
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* This expresses how *deep* a single feature is implemented relative to production-ready code.

**Step 3:** Establish the **classification decision rule**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Prototype type} = \begin{cases}
\text{Horizontal} & \text{if } \text{FC} \geq 70\% \text{ AND } \text{ID} \leq 30\% \\[4pt]
\text{Vertical}   & \text{if } \text{FC} \leq 30\% \text{ AND } \text{ID} \geq 70\% \\[4pt]
\text{Hybrid / Throwaway Detail} & \text{otherwise (mixed or diagonal cases)}
\end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* A horizontal prototype is identified by *wide coverage* and *shallow depth* simultaneously. Either condition alone is insufficient.

**Step 4:** Worked Example.

Suppose an e-commerce SRS specifies $F_{\text{total}} = 12$ features (login, search, cart, checkout, profile, order history, reviews, wishlist, recommendations, support chat, settings, notifications). A prototype implements *clickable wireframes* for all 12, but each feature averages only $L_{\text{real}} = 50$ lines of HTML/CSS skeleton against an estimated $L_{\text{total}} = 500$ production lines per feature.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FC} &= \frac{12}{12} \times 100\% = 100\% \\[6pt]
\text{ID} &= \frac{50}{500} \times 100\% = 10\% \\[6pt]
\text{Classification:} & \quad \text{FC} = 100\% \geq 70\% \text{ AND } \text{ID} = 10\% \leq 30\% \\[4pt]
& \quad \Rightarrow \text{HORIZONTAL PROTOTYPE}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Counter-Example (to show rule robustness).

If the same team instead built a *fully working* login module (500 lines, real auth, JWT tokens, DB) and left the other 11 as blank pages:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FC} &= \frac{1}{12} \times 100\% \approx 8.3\% \\[6pt]
\text{ID} &= \frac{500}{500} \times 100\% = 100\% \\[6pt]
\text{Classification:} & \quad \text{FC} \approx 8.3\% \leq 30\% \text{ AND } \text{ID} = 100\% \geq 70\% \\[4pt]
& \quad \Rightarrow \text{VERTICAL PROTOTYPE (not horizontal)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Choose Horizontal Prototyping

The matrix below is a high-yield KTU tool. Examiners love 14-mark questions framed as *"Justify / Critique the choice of horizontal prototyping for Scenario X."*

| Evaluation Criterion | Weight | Score (1–5) | Weighted Score | Justification |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| **Goal is scope / IA validation** | **0.30** | 5 | **1.50** | Primary use case of horizontal |
| **Backend logic is highly uncertain** | 0.20 | 4 | 0.80 | Avoid wasting effort on deep logic |
| **Stakeholders are non-technical** | **0.25** | 5 | **1.25** | Visual mockups aid communication |
| **Time-to-market for feedback is critical** | 0.15 | 5 | 0.75 | Horizontal builds in days, not months |
| **Detailed algorithm correctness is required** | 0.10 | 1 | 0.10 | Horizontal *cannot* validate algorithms |

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Weighted Score} &= 1.50 + 0.80 + 1.25 + 0.75 + 0.10 = 4.40 \\[6pt]
\text{Maximum Possible} &= 0.30(5) + 0.20(5) + 0.25(5) + 0.15(5) + 0.10(5) = 5.00 \\[6pt]
\text{Decision Threshold} &= 3.50 \\[6pt]
\text{Result} &: 4.40 \geq 3.50 \Rightarrow \text{USE HORIZONTAL PROTOTYPING}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **How to use this matrix in the exam:** Replace the scores with values justified by the scenario given in the question. The weighting column is fixed by the *purpose* of the prototype, and you must show the arithmetic line by line.

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Construction of a Horizontal Prototype (Process Model)

The KTU board also tests the *process*. Memorize the seven stages below in order.

1. **Requirement Traceability Mapping** — Tag every feature in the SRS to a screen in the prototype.
2. **Low-Fidelity Wireframing** — Pencil/paper or Balsamiq-style sketches.
3. **High-Fidelity Visual Design** — Apply final fonts, colors, and brand assets.
4. **Navigation Graph Construction** — Define a directed graph $G = (S, E)$ where $S$ is the set of screens and $E$ is the set of navigable transitions.
5. **Stub Data Binding** — Replace dynamic data with mocked JSON or static arrays.
6. **User Walkthrough Sessions** — Moderated usability sessions.
7. **Iteration & Scope Refinement** — Update SRS based on findings, *discard* the prototype.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Horizontal prototypes are *almost always throwaway* in pure form. Do not confuse them with the final UI build. In a 14-mark answer, you must explicitly state: *"The horizontal prototype is discarded after requirements are frozen; it is not evolved into the production system."*

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram A — Horizontal Prototype as a Breadth-First Slice

The following Mermaid diagram visualizes a horizontal prototype as a horizontal slice *across* all vertical features of a system. Each feature module is "skinned" at the presentation layer only, with no depth below the UI.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    UIMASTER["UI SHELL HORIZONTAL PROTOTYPE"]
    F1["FEATURE 1 LOGIN WIDE SHALLOW"]
    F2["FEATURE 2 SEARCH WIDE SHALLOW"]
    F3["FEATURE 3 CART WIDE SHALLOW"]
    F4["FEATURE 4 CHECKOUT WIDE SHALLOW"]
    F5["FEATURE 5 PROFILE WIDE SHALLOW"]
    F6["FEATURE 6 HISTORY WIDE SHALLOW"]
    STUB["STUB DATA LAYER MOCKED JSON"]
    UIMASTER --> F1
    UIMASTER --> F2
    UIMASTER --> F3
    UIMASTER --> F4
    UIMASTER --> F5
    UIMASTER --> F6
    F1 -. uses .-> STUB
    F2 -. uses .-> STUB
    F3 -. uses .-> STUB
    F4 -. uses .-> STUB
    F5 -. uses .-> STUB
    F6 -. uses .-> STUB
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows indicate *navigation flow* (real, clickable). Dashed arrows indicate *data dependencies* (mocked, not real). The vertical stacking of features 1 through 6 is symbolic — the prototype covers all of them *equally shallowly*, which is the visual signature of a horizontal prototype.

### 4.2 Diagram B — Comparison: Horizontal vs Vertical vs Hybrid Prototypes

This block-level functional architecture diagram maps the structural difference between the three main prototyping strategies. Each rectangle represents a feature module; shading represents implementation depth.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HORIZ ["HORIZONTAL PROTOTYPE WIDE AND SHALLOW"]
        H1["F1 UI ONLY"]:::hShallow
        H2["F2 UI ONLY"]:::hShallow
        H3["F3 UI ONLY"]:::hShallow
        H4["F4 UI ONLY"]:::hShallow
    end
    subgraph VERT ["VERTICAL PROTOTYPE NARROW AND DEEP"]
        V1UI["F1 UI"]:::vTop
        V1LOGIC["F1 LOGIC"]:::vMid
        V1DATA["F1 DATA"]:::vDeep
    end
    subgraph HYB ["HYBRID PROTOTYPE MIXED"]
        Y1["F1 FULL"]:::yFull
        Y2["F2 UI ONLY"]:::yShallow
        Y3["F3 UI ONLY"]:::yShallow
        Y4["F4 FULL"]:::yFull
    end

    classDef hShallow fill:#b3d9ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef vTop fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#663300,color:#000
    classDef vMid fill:#ffb380,stroke:#663300,color:#000
    classDef vDeep fill:#ff8c66,stroke:#663300,color:#000
    classDef yFull fill:#b3ffb3,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    classDef yShallow fill:#d9f2d9,stroke:#006600,color:#000
```

> [!TIP]
> **Color-Code Legend (for exam drawing):**
> - *Light blue* = Horizontal prototype (UI only across all features).
> - *Orange gradient* = Vertical prototype (single feature, all three layers: UI / Logic / Data).
> - *Green* = Hybrid (mix of full features and UI-only features).

### 4.3 Diagram C — Lifecycle of a Horizontal Prototype

The following flowchart shows the *throwaway lifecycle* of a horizontal prototype from conception to disposal.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["SRS DOCUMENT RECEIVED"] --> B["IDENTIFY ALL FEATURES BREADTH LIST"]
    B --> C["BUILD CLICKABLE WIREFRAMES"]
    C --> D["APPLY VISUAL DESIGN STYLING"]
    D --> E["INJECT STUB DATA MOCKS"]
    E --> F["CONDUCT USER WALKTHROUGH"]
    F --> G{"FEEDBACK COLLECTED"}
    G -- YES --> H["REFINE SRS SCOPE AND IA"]
    G -- NO --> I["FREEZE REQUIREMENTS"]
    H --> J["DISCARD HORIZONTAL PROTOTYPE"]
    I --> J
    J --> K["PROCEED TO FULL DEVELOPMENT"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Emphasis:** The diamond-shaped decision node and the terminal *discard* step are crucial. Students who forget to mention the *throwaway nature* of horizontal prototypes typically lose 2 marks in a 14-mark question.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define a horizontal prototype. List any THREE distinguishing characteristics.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A horizontal prototype is a breadth-first prototype that implements a *wide* range of features at a *shallow* depth, typically limited to the user interface layer. It is used to demonstrate the system's overall look, feel, and information architecture rather than its functional correctness.

Three distinguishing characteristics:
1. **Wide Coverage:** Implements most or all features of the system (typically $\geq 70\%$ feature coverage).
2. **Shallow Depth:** Each feature is implemented to only a presentation-level depth (typically $\leq 20\%$ of full logic).
3. **Discardable (Throwaway):** It is built for requirements validation and is *not* evolved into the final product.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the three characteristics with one-line justification each.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between a horizontal prototype and a vertical prototype in TWO points.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Horizontal Prototype | Vertical Prototype |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Dimension** | Wide breadth, shallow depth | Narrow breadth, deep depth |
| **Purpose** | Validate scope, navigation, IA | Validate technical feasibility of one feature |
| **Implementation** | UI layer only, stubbed data | UI + logic + data layers for one feature |
| **Feedback** | *"Does the system look right everywhere?"* | *"Does this one feature work correctly?"* |

*(Award 1.5 marks per major distinction; partial credit for correct single-line contrasts.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions offer an *internal choice* — the student answers either **Question A OR Question B** of the same module. Both are provided below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Comprehensive Descriptive*

**[KTU University Exam – July 2024, Module 3]**

> **(a)** Explain the concept of a horizontal prototype in detail. Discuss its construction steps, the role of stub data, and the kind of user feedback it is best suited to collect. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compare and contrast horizontal prototyping with vertical prototyping using a suitable scenario from an interactive system (e.g., a hospital appointment-booking app). Justify which strategy you would recommend for the *initial* requirements-gathering phase and why. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

A **horizontal prototype** is a surface-level, breadth-first prototype that simulates the *entire* scope of an interactive system at the presentation layer only. Its defining traits are:

1. **Breadth-First Construction:** Every screen, dialog, and menu specified in the SRS is built as a clickable wireframe.
2. **Shallow Implementation:** No real business logic, database, or algorithm is implemented. Functions like "Save," "Submit," or "Search" may simply display a placeholder message such as *"Action simulated."*
3. **Stub Data:** The data layer is mocked. For example, a product list may show 5 hard-coded items instead of querying a real database.

**Construction Steps (write any 4 for full marks):**
1. Enumerate all features from the SRS.
2. Create low-fidelity wireframes.
3. Apply high-fidelity visual styling.
4. Wire up navigation links between screens.
5. Bind stub / mocked data.
6. Conduct moderated user walkthroughs.
7. Refine the SRS and *discard* the prototype.

**Best-Suited Feedback:**
- Information architecture validity
- Screen-flow and navigation efficiency
- Visual consistency and branding
- Scope completeness (is anything missing?)
- Heuristic evaluation (Nielsen's 10 usability heuristics)

*(Distribution: 2 marks for definition, 3 marks for construction steps, 2 marks for feedback types.)*

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

| Aspect | Horizontal | Vertical |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Goal** | Validate *what* the system will do | Validate *how* a specific feature works |
| **Coverage** | All features, shallow | One feature, deep |
| **Layer** | UI only | UI + Logic + Data |
| **Cost** | Low (~15% of full build) | Higher (~40–60% of that one feature) |
| **Risk Reduced** | Scope / design risk | Technical / algorithmic risk |
| **Iteration** | Quick, days | Slow, weeks |

**Scenario — Hospital Appointment App:**

For a hospital appointment-booking app with features like *Login, Doctor Search, Slot Booking, Payment, Medical Records, Feedback,* and *Notifications*:

**Recommendation:** Use a **horizontal prototype** during the *initial* requirements-gathering phase.

**Justification:**
- The hospital has 15+ stakeholder groups (patients, doctors, receptionists, admin). Each group needs to *see* the full workflow.
- No algorithm in the app is computationally novel — booking, payment, and notifications are well-understood problems. There is no *technical* risk to mitigate.
- The dominant risk is **scope ambiguity**: Will the receptionist see a different dashboard from the doctor? Will payment happen inside the app or via SMS link? These are *breadth* questions, not *depth* questions.
- A horizontal prototype resolves all such questions in days at minimal cost. Building a vertical prototype for, say, the payment module would consume 4 weeks and yield no useful information about the other 6 modules.

A vertical prototype is later justified only when an unknown algorithm (e.g., a novel doctor-matching AI) needs validation.

*(Distribution: 3 marks for comparison table, 2 marks for scenario application, 2 marks for justified recommendation.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]**

> **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of a horizontal prototype. Show how each feature module is connected to the navigation shell and to the stub data layer. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A startup is building a multi-vendor food-delivery application. They have limited funds and 2 weeks before a stakeholder review. As a UX architect, recommend the most suitable prototyping strategy (horizontal, vertical, or hybrid). Justify with at least FOUR evaluation criteria. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Diagram (to be drawn in the answer sheet):**

```
                ┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
                │   NAVIGATION SHELL (UI MASTER)      │
                │  Clickable, routes between screens  │
                └──────────────┬──────────────────────┘
                               │
        ┌────────────┬─────────┼─────────┬────────────┐
        ▼            ▼         ▼         ▼            ▼
   ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐ ┌──────┐ ┌──────┐  ┌──────────┐
   │ F1 UI   │  │ F2 UI   │ │ F3UI │ │ F4UI │  │ F5 UI    │
   │ Login   │  │ Search  │ │ Cart │ │ Pay  │  │ Profile  │
   └────┬────┘  └────┬────┘ └───┬──┘ └───┬──┘  └────┬─────┘
        │            │         │        │           │
        └────────────┴─────────┼────────┴───────────┘
                               ▼
                ┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
                │     STUB DATA LAYER (MOCKED)        │
                │   Hard-coded JSON / Lorem Ipsum     │
                └─────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Architecture Explanation:**

1. **Navigation Shell (UI Master):** A single routing layer (e.g., React Router) that owns the top-level menu and links. All click events are real.
2. **Feature Modules (UI only):** Each of the 5+ feature screens is implemented as a styled, static page. Buttons exist but trigger placeholder toasts.
3. **Stub Data Layer:** A separate, mocked JSON file or in-memory array that supplies fake data to all modules uniformly. This guarantees that the *look* of populated screens is realistic.
4. **Connections:** *Solid arrows* (Navigation Shell → Feature UI) represent real DOM routing. *Dashed arrows* (Feature UI → Stub Layer) represent mocked data fetches.

*(Distribution: 3 marks for the diagram with proper labels, 4 marks for the explanation of each layer.)*

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Recommended Strategy: HORIZONTAL PROTOTYPE.**

**Justification across FOUR evaluation criteria:**

1. **Breadth of stakeholder concerns:** The startup has investors, restaurants, delivery partners, and end-users — all of whom need to *see* the entire app journey (browse → order → pay → track → review). Horizontal covers all 5+ flows. Vertical would leave 80% of the system invisible.
2. **Time constraint (2 weeks):** A horizontal prototype in Figma or Adobe XD can be assembled in 5–7 days. A vertical prototype (e.g., fully working payment with gateway integration) takes 3–4 weeks. The 2-week deadline makes horizontal the *only* feasible choice.
3. **Budget constraint:** Investors have not yet committed the next tranche. A horizontal prototype costs roughly **15% of a vertical one**, preserving runway.
4. **Risk profile:** The dominant risk is *market fit* and *scope clarity*, not *technical novelty*. No part of a food-delivery app contains unknown algorithms. Therefore, the technical-depth validation offered by a vertical prototype yields no additional value.

**Conclusion:** Build a horizontal prototype for the stakeholder review, freeze the scope, then *discard* it and begin full development with a fresh codebase. A vertical prototype can be considered later *only* if a novel algorithm (e.g., dynamic delivery-route AI) is introduced.

*(Distribution: 1 mark per criterion, 2 marks for the final recommendation sentence and reasoning, 1 mark for the lifecycle note.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks on Horizontal Prototype questions (Examiner Insights):**
>
> 1. **Confusing horizontal with vertical:** Many students write "horizontal" when the question is about depth-first validation. Always re-read the question to see whether *breadth* or *depth* is being validated. (Lose up to 3 marks.)
> 2. **Forgetting the "throwaway" property:** Horizontal prototypes are *disposable* in canonical HCI theory. Saying *"the prototype is extended to the final product"* is a textbook error. (Lose 2 marks.)
> 3. **Omitting stub data:** A horizontal prototype without mocked data is just a wireframe collection. Always mention that **stub data** is bound to UI to make the prototype *look* real. (Lose 1 mark.)
> 4. **No diagram in 7-mark sub-questions:** KTU 2024 valuation key explicitly allocates marks for *"neat labeled diagram"* in any 7+ mark question on prototyping strategies. Skipping the diagram = losing 2–3 marks. (Lose 2–3 marks.)
> 5. **Using absolute value `|` inside markdown tables:** Students typing $\lvert x \rvert$ in a table cell often break the table renderer. Use `\vert` or `\mid` for safe rendering. (Lose 0 marks in valuation, but lose presentation points.)

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Horizontal Prototypes (Module 3, PECNT748)**

- **Definition:** Breadth-first, shallow-depth prototype covering the *entire* UI scope with *no* functional depth.
- **Primary Use:** Scope validation, information-architecture testing, stakeholder demos, visual design verification.
- **Three Keywords:** **Breadth**, **Shallow**, **UI-only**.
- **Breadth-to-Depth Ratio (BDR):** $F_{\text{impl}} \div D_{\text{per feature}}$; for a horizontal prototype, $\text{BDR} \gg 1$.
- **Decision Rule:** $\text{FC} \geq 70\%$ AND $\text{ID} \leq 30\% \Rightarrow$ Horizontal.
- **Cost:** ~**15%** of full build. **Time:** ~**20%** of full build.
- **Stub Data:** Mandatory. Without it, the prototype is just a wireframe.
- **Lifecycle:** Build → Walkthrough → Feedback → Refine SRS → **Discard** → Full Development.
- **Contrast with Vertical:** Vertical = narrow + deep; Horizontal = wide + shallow. Hybrid = mix of both.
- **Tools:** Figma, Adobe XD, Balsamiq, InVision, Axure RP, Proto.io.
- **Real-World Fit:** Hospital apps, food-delivery apps, e-commerce sites, banking apps (during *requirements* phase only).
- **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Never evolve a horizontal prototype into the production system — it is *throwaway* by design.
- **Bloom's Level Tested:** Typically *Understand* (L2) and *Analyze* (L4) in 3-mark questions; *Evaluate* (L5) and *Create* (L6) in 14-mark questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vertical Prototypes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT748 - PROTOTYPING INTERACTIVE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Vertical Prototypes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Prototyping Interactive Systems**, a **Vertical Prototype** (also known as a *high-fidelity functional prototype* or *slices-through prototype*) is a partially implemented system in which a **single, fully functional pathway** is taken from the user interface layer through the entire technological stack down to the underlying data and logic layers. In strict KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a vertical prototype operationalizes a **complete end-to-end slice of the system** with full functional depth for a *limited* set of features, rather than a broad but shallow coverage of the interface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PECNT748 – Module 3):** Vertical prototypes are a core sub-classification of *functional prototypes*. They are the recommended KTU-mandated technique for validating **critical-path features** and for **de-risking technical architecture decisions** before full-scale development.

A vertical prototype can be formally expressed as:

$$P_{vertical} = f(\text{Feature}_{selected}) \rightarrow [\text{UI Layer} \rightarrow \text{Application Layer} \rightarrow \text{Data Layer}]$$

where the arrow `→` represents a *fully implemented, end-to-end data and control flow* for the selected feature only.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy:** Imagine a 5-storey building is being constructed. A *horizontal prototype* would put a thin concrete slab across **all 5 floors** — you can walk the entire footprint, but you cannot go up or down. A *vertical prototype* would build **one complete, fully furnished stairwell** from the basement to the roof. You can only use one path, but every step, every handrail, and every floor transition on that path actually works end-to-end.

For a B.Tech student reading this for the first time:
- **Horizontal** = wide but shallow (UI mockups of every screen)
- **Vertical** = narrow but deep (one feature, fully working, top to bottom of the tech stack)

## 1.3 Defining Metrics & Engineering Constants

The following standardized metrics govern the evaluation of vertical prototypes in industry and are testable in KTU valuation:

| Metric | Standard Value / Range | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Fidelity Level** | **High (functional fidelity)** | UI behavior matches real product |
| **Code Reuse Ratio** | **≥ 60%** (target) | Code carried forward to production |
| **Layer Coverage** | **3 layers minimum** (UI, Logic, Data) | Defines the "vertical" nature |
| **Throw-away Index** | **Low (0.0 – 0.3)** | Most code is retained, not discarded |
| **Time-to-Risk-Reduction** | **30% – 50% of total project timeline** | Used in agile sprint planning |

## 1.4 KTU 2024 Scheme Context

In the 2024 NEP-aligned syllabus for **Prototyping Interactive Systems**, vertical prototypes are positioned as the bridge between *low-fidelity throwaway prototyping* and the *final production system*. They are directly tied to:

- **CO3 (Module 3):** *Evaluate and apply advanced prototyping techniques to validate system architecture and user interaction flows.*
- **CO4:** *Analyse trade-offs between horizontal and vertical prototyping in real-world design scenarios.*

The cognitive levels tested (per KTU 2024 Bloom's mapping) for this topic range from **L3 (Apply)** to **L5 (Evaluate)**, meaning students must be able to **build, compare, and justify** — not merely define.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Decomposition — The "Why" and "How"

Vertical prototyping is grounded in the **slice-through architecture pattern** borrowed from software engineering. The system is decomposed along the *feature axis* rather than the *layer axis*.

### 2.1.1 Operational Logic Steps

1. **Identify the critical feature(s):** Select 1–3 features that are architecturally risky or business-critical.
2. **Trace the full data path:** Map every layer the data must traverse — input → controller → business logic → database → output.
3. **Implement the full stack for that feature only:** Real code, real database, real API calls — no mocked layers below the UI.
4. **Stub out the remaining features:** Other parts of the UI exist as placeholder screens only.
5. **Test end-to-end behavior:** Validate the feature under realistic load, latency, and error conditions.
6. **Measure architectural viability:** The output is not the feature itself, but **proof that the architecture works.**

### 2.1.2 The "Why" — Engineering Justification

- **Risk Reduction:** Discovers integration, latency, and data-model problems early.
- **Architectural Validation:** Confirms the tech stack can support the intended user experience.
- **Stakeholder Confidence:** A working slice is more persuasive than a mock-up.
- **Foundation for Incremental Development:** The slice can be evolved into the full system.

### 2.1.3 The "How" — Mechanics

The depth dimension is governed by the **Layer Penetration Index (LPI)**:

$$LPI = \frac{L_{implemented}}{L_{total}}$$

where $L_{implemented}$ is the number of layers with real, functional code, and $L_{total}$ is the total number of architectural layers. A true vertical prototype has $LPI \rightarrow 1.0$ for the selected feature path, while the feature coverage index (breadth) is intentionally low.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Term | Symbolic Form | Engineering Meaning | Unit / Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Vertical Slice Coverage** | $VSC = \frac{F_{functional}}{F_{total}}$ | Fraction of features that are fully functional | $0 \leq VSC \leq 1$ |
| 2 | **Layer Penetration Index** | $LPI = \frac{L_{impl}}{L_{total}}$ | Depth of the prototype through the stack | $0 \leq LPI \leq 1$ |
| 3 | **Fidelity Score** | $F_s = \alpha U + \beta D + \gamma I$ | Weighted fidelity: UI, Data realism, Interaction realism | $0 \leq F_s \leq 10$ |
| 4 | **Throw-away Ratio** | $T_r = \frac{C_{discarded}}{C_{written}}$ | Proportion of code thrown away after validation | $0 \leq T_r \leq 1$ |
| 5 | **Reuse Yield** | $R_y = 1 - T_r$ | Code carried forward to production | $0 \leq R_y \leq 1$ |
| 6 | **Time-to-Insight** | $TTI = \frac{t_{prototype}}{t_{total\_proj}}$ | Fraction of project time consumed by prototyping | $0 \leq TTI \leq 0.5$ |
| 7 | **Feature Depth Budget** | $D_b = \frac{R}{F_{vertical}}$ | Resources per vertical feature | Person-hours |
| 8 | **Risk Reduction Factor** | $RRF = 1 - \frac{R_{after}}{R_{before}}$ | Fractional risk removed by the prototype | $0 \leq RRF \leq 1$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Note:** In Part B 14-mark answers, examiners award up to 3 marks for correct *terminology and formula application*. Memorize the **LPI** and **Fidelity Score** formulas — they are the most commonly tested.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Vertical prototypes are the **de-facto standard** in modern agile and DevOps workflows. Specific industry applications include:

- **FinTech:** Validating payment-gateway integration end-to-end before scaling to 200 microservices.
- **Healthcare HCI:** Building one complete clinical-workflow slice (e.g., patient intake → EMR write-back) to prove HIPAA-compliant architecture.
- **IoT Systems:** Proving the full edge-to-cloud data path for a single sensor type before rolling out the entire sensor fleet.
- **AR/VR Interaction Systems:** Verifying gesture → render → persistence pipeline for a single core interaction.

## 2.4 Comparison Anchor (for Part B answers)

| Property | Horizontal Prototype | Vertical Prototype |
|---|---|---|
| **Breadth** | High (all features) | Low (few features) |
| **Depth** | Low (UI only) | High (full stack) |
| **Fidelity** | Medium-High (UI) | High (functional) |
| **Risk Reduction** | Low | High |
| **Cost per Feature** | Low | High |
| **Code Reuse** | Low (often throwaway) | High (often evolutionary) |
| **Best Used For** | User testing, navigation | Architecture validation, performance testing |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Layer Penetration Index (LPI)

The LPI is the foundational metric of a vertical prototype. We derive it from first principles.

### Step 1: Define the architectural layer set

For a typical interactive system (e.g., a web app), the total layer set is:

$$L_{total} = \{L_1, L_2, L_3, L_4\}$$

where:
- $L_1$ = Presentation Layer (UI / View)
- $L_2$ = Application / Controller Layer
- $L_3$ = Business Logic / Domain Layer
- $L_4$ = Data Persistence Layer (Database / API)

### Step 2: Define the implementation state for a vertical prototype feature

For the *selected feature* $f_{sel}$, each layer has a binary state:

$$
l_i(f_{sel}) = 
\begin{cases}
1, & \text{if layer } L_i \text{ is fully implemented for } f_{sel} \\
0, & \text{otherwise (stubbed or absent)}
\end{cases}
$$

### Step 3: Sum the implemented layers for the selected feature

$$L_{impl} = \sum_{i=1}^{4} l_i(f_{sel})$$

### Step 4: Form the LPI ratio

By definition, the Layer Penetration Index is:

$$LPI = \frac{L_{impl}}{L_{total}} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{4} l_i(f_{sel})}{4}$$

### Step 5: Apply to a concrete vertical prototype

Suppose a vertical prototype implements **User Login** end-to-end. Each layer has real code:

$$LPI_{login} = \frac{1 + 1 + 1 + 1}{4} = \frac{4}{4} = 1.00$$

Suppose a horizontal prototype implements **all 20 features** but only at the UI layer:

$$LPI_{horizontal} = \frac{1 + 0 + 0 + 0}{4} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25$$

### Step 6: Interpretation threshold

- $LPI \geq 0.75$ → **Vertical** classification
- $0.25 \leq LPI < 0.75$ → **Hybrid (T-shaped) prototype**
- $LPI < 0.25$ → **Horizontal** classification

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Fidelity Score ($F_s$)

Fidelity is a multi-dimensional measure. For a vertical prototype, the weighted form is:

$$F_s = \alpha U + \beta D + \gamma I$$

### Step 1: Define the components

- $U$ = UI fidelity (visual realism) — range 0 to 10
- $D$ = Data fidelity (real data vs. fake) — range 0 to 10
- $I$ = Interaction fidelity (real vs. simulated behavior) — range 0 to 10
- $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ = weight coefficients, with $\alpha + \beta + \gamma = 1$

### Step 2: Apply standard KTU weights for a vertical prototype

For a vertical prototype, interaction realism is prioritized:

$$\alpha = 0.30, \quad \beta = 0.35, \quad \gamma = 0.35$$

### Step 3: Calculate for a sample vertical prototype

Given: $U = 8, D = 9, I = 10$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_s &= (0.30)(8) + (0.35)(9) + (0.35)(10) \\
&= 2.40 + 3.15 + 3.50 \\
&= 9.05
\end{aligned}
$$

A score above **8.5** confirms *high-fidelity vertical prototype* classification.

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)

The RRF quantifies how much architectural risk is removed by the prototype.

### Step 1: Estimate pre-prototype risk

Use a simple risk-rating function combining probability and impact:

$$R = P \times I$$

where $P$ = probability of failure (0 to 1), $I$ = impact severity (0 to 1).

### Step 2: Estimate post-prototype risk

After validation, the remaining risk is:

$$R_{after} = P_{after} \times I_{after}$$

### Step 3: Form the reduction factor

$$RRF = 1 - \frac{R_{after}}{R_{before}}$$

### Step 4: Worked Example

Before the vertical prototype, the team estimates:
- $P_{before} = 0.60$ (60% chance of integration failure)
- $I_{before} = 0.80$ (high business impact)

$$R_{before} = 0.60 \times 0.80 = 0.48$$

After validating the payment-slice vertical prototype:
- $P_{after} = 0.10$
- $I_{after} = 0.80$ (impact unchanged, but probability reduced)

$$R_{after} = 0.10 \times 0.80 = 0.08$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
RRF &= 1 - \frac{0.08}{0.48} \\
&= 1 - 0.1667 \\
&= 0.8333
\end{aligned}
$$

Interpretation: **83.33% of the architectural risk is removed** by the vertical prototype — a strong justification for the technique.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Choose a Vertical Prototype

| Scenario | Recommended? | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Architecture is unproven | **Yes** | Vertical prototype validates the stack |
| User-flow aesthetics are the only concern | **No** | Horizontal prototype is cheaper |
| Performance-critical feature exists | **Yes** | Only full-stack slice reveals real latency |
| Tight budget, broad UI feedback needed | **No** | Use horizontal wireframes |
| Integration with external API is risky | **Yes** | Mocking the API hides integration risk |
| Final product is 1 month away | **No** | Cost of rework exceeds benefit |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram: Vertical Slice vs Horizontal Slice

The following Mermaid block diagram shows how a vertical prototype "slices" through all layers for one feature, whereas a horizontal prototype covers all features at one layer.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph layers["System Architecture Layers"]
        L1["L1 Presentation Layer UI"]
        L2["L2 Application Controller Layer"]
        L3["L3 Business Logic Layer"]
        L4["L4 Data Persistence Layer DB API"]
    end

    subgraph feat["Selected Feature User Login"]
        F1["Login Screen UI"]
        F2["Auth Controller"]
        F3["Credential Validation Logic"]
        F4["User Table Query"]
    end

    VP["Vertical Prototype Path"]:::vp
    HP["Horizontal Prototype Path"]:::hp

    L1 --- L2 --- L3 --- L4
    F1 --- F2 --- F3 --- F4

    VP -. Traverses .-> F1
    VP -. Traverses .-> F2
    VP -. Traverses .-> F3
    VP -. Traverses .-> F4
    HP -. Covers .-> L1

    classDef vp fill:#ffd966,stroke:#cc7a00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef hp fill:#9fc5e8,stroke:#1f4e79,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

## 4.2 Process Flow Diagram: Building a Vertical Prototype

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Step 1 Identify Critical Feature"] --> B["Step 2 Map All Architectural Layers"]
    B --> C["Step 3 Implement Full Stack for Feature"]
    C --> D["Step 4 Stub Out Other Features"]
    D --> E["Step 5 Run End to End Test"]
    E --> F["Step 6 Validate Architecture"]
    F --> G{"Architecture Viable"}
    G -- Yes --> H["Step 7 Promote to Evolutionary Base"]
    G -- No --> I["Step 7 Revise Architecture"]
    I --> B
    H --> J["Production Development"]
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture Flow: Risk Reduction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph input["Inputs"]
        I1["High Risk Feature"]
        I2["Unknown Architecture"]
    end

    subgraph vpblock["Vertical Prototype Engine"]
        P1["Feature Selection"]
        P2["Full Stack Implementation"]
        P3["Integration Testing"]
        P4["Performance Profiling"]
    end

    subgraph output["Outputs"]
        O1["Validated Architecture"]
        O2["Reusable Code Base"]
        O3["Reduced Risk RRF High"]
    end

    I1 --> P1
    I2 --> P1
    P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4
    P4 --> O1
    P4 --> O2
    P4 --> O3
```

## 4.4 Comparative Schematic: Horizontal vs Vertical Coverage Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph matrix["Feature Layer Coverage Matrix"]
        direction LR
        H1["Horizontal Coverage"]
        V1["Vertical Coverage"]
    end

    subgraph hf["Horizontal Footprint"]
        HA["Feature A UI only"]
        HB["Feature B UI only"]
        HC["Feature C UI only"]
        HD["Feature D UI only"]
    end

    subgraph vf["Vertical Footprint"]
        VA["Feature A UI"]
        VB["Feature A Logic"]
        VC["Feature A Data"]
    end

    H1 --> HA
    H1 --> HB
    H1 --> HC
    H1 --> HD
    V1 --> VA
    V1 --> VB
    V1 --> VC
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Notice that the horizontal prototype fans out across *many features* at the *top layer only*, while the vertical prototype dives *deep* into a *single feature* across *all layers*. This visual asymmetry is the most-tested diagram in KTU Module 3 examinations.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a vertical prototype. How does it differ from a horizontal prototype in terms of fidelity and scope?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A vertical prototype is a **fully functional slice of the system** that implements one or a few selected features end-to-end, traversing every architectural layer from the user interface to the data persistence layer. It achieves **high functional fidelity** for those features only.

It differs from a horizontal prototype in that:
- **Scope:** Horizontal covers *all* features at the UI layer only; vertical covers *few* features across *all* layers.
- **Fidelity:** Horizontal has medium UI fidelity with no real logic; vertical has high fidelity in both UI and behavior.
- **Purpose:** Horizontal is used for *navigation and layout validation*; vertical is used for *architectural and integration validation*.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for scope contrast, 1 mark for fidelity contrast.)*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the Layer Penetration Index (LPI) formula. What is the minimum LPI value for a prototype to be classified as vertical?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Layer Penetration Index is defined as:

$$LPI = \frac{L_{impl}}{L_{total}}$$

where $L_{impl}$ is the number of layers with real functional code for the selected feature, and $L_{total}$ is the total number of architectural layers in the system.

The minimum LPI for a prototype to be classified as **vertical** is **0.75** (i.e., at least three-quarters of the architectural layers must be functionally implemented for the selected feature path).

*(Award 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for variable definitions, 1 mark for the 0.75 threshold.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of a vertical prototype in detail. Discuss its key characteristics, the architectural reasoning behind it, and the typical engineering scenarios in which it is the most appropriate prototyping strategy. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A healthcare startup is building a patient-appointment booking system. The architecture has 4 layers (UI, Application, Business Logic, Database). The team builds a vertical prototype for the *Book Appointment* feature, where all 4 layers are functional. Compute the **Layer Penetration Index (LPI)** and the **Fidelity Score** given $U = 7, D = 8, I = 9$ with weights $\alpha = 0.30, \beta = 0.35, \gamma = 0.35$. Interpret both results. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

A **vertical prototype** is a *partial but functionally complete* implementation of the system that takes one or a few user features and implements them through **every architectural layer** of the target system — from the presentation layer at the top to the data persistence layer at the bottom. The metaphor is a *vertical slice* through the technology stack.

**Key Characteristics (3 marks):**

1. **High Functional Fidelity:** The selected feature behaves exactly as it would in production, including realistic data, network calls, and error handling.
2. **Full Layer Coverage:** Real code exists in the UI, controller, business logic, and data layers for the chosen feature.
3. **Narrow Feature Scope:** Only a few features are implemented; the rest are stubbed.
4. **Architectural Validation Focus:** The goal is not to deliver features but to *prove the architecture works*.
5. **Evolutionary Potential:** The code is often retained and extended, unlike throwaway prototypes.

**Architectural Reasoning (2 marks):**
The vertical prototype follows the **slice-through architecture pattern**, an alternative to the *layered development* approach. Instead of building one complete layer at a time (e.g., finishing the entire UI before any backend), the team builds *one feature end-to-end* and then iterates. This uncovers integration defects, latency issues, and data-model mismatches at the earliest possible moment — embodying the agile principle of *fail fast, learn fast*.

**Appropriate Engineering Scenarios (2 marks):**
- **High-risk integrations** (e.g., payment gateways, third-party APIs).
- **Performance-critical paths** (e.g., real-time bidding, video streaming).
- **New architectural stacks** (e.g., first-time use of WebAssembly in a browser app).
- **Compliance-sensitive features** (e.g., HIPAA login flows in healthcare).

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Compute LPI**

For the *Book Appointment* vertical prototype, all 4 layers are functional:

$$L_{impl} = 4, \quad L_{total} = 4$$

$$
LPI = \frac{L_{impl}}{L_{total}} = \frac{4}{4} = 1.00
$$

**Step 2: Compute Fidelity Score**

Given $U = 7, D = 8, I = 9$ and $\alpha = 0.30, \beta = 0.35, \gamma = 0.35$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_s &= \alpha U + \beta D + \gamma I \\
&= (0.30)(7) + (0.35)(8) + (0.35)(9) \\
&= 2.10 + 2.80 + 3.15 \\
&= 8.05
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Interpretation (2 marks)**

- **LPI = 1.00:** The prototype achieves **maximum layer penetration**. It is a textbook vertical prototype — every layer is fully implemented for the chosen feature.
- **Fidelity Score = 8.05:** This places the prototype firmly in the **high-fidelity vertical prototype** category (threshold ≥ 8.5 is "premium", ≥ 7.5 is "high"). The healthcare team can confidently use this prototype for architectural sign-off and stakeholder demonstration.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part (b):** A common error is to **omit the interpretation step**. Students who write only the numerical answers (LPI = 1.00, F_s = 8.05) without explaining *what these numbers mean* typically lose **2 of the 7 marks**. Always close numerical answers with a one-sentence interpretation tied to the engineering context.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, 2024 Scheme Adapted]`

**(a)** Compare and contrast horizontal and vertical prototypes across **at least six dimensions** (breadth, depth, fidelity, cost, code reuse, risk reduction). Construct a comparative table and justify which prototype is more suitable for validating a *new payment-gateway integration*. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fintech team is integrating a new payment gateway. The estimated pre-prototype risk is $P_{before} = 0.50$, $I_{before} = 0.90$. After building a vertical prototype of the *Charge Card* feature, the new risk is $P_{after} = 0.15$, $I_{after} = 0.90$. Compute the **Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)**. Also compute the **Reuse Yield** ($R_y$) given that 750 lines of prototype code were written and 200 lines were discarded. Discuss the engineering significance of both metrics. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Comparative Table (4 marks):**

| Dimension | Horizontal Prototype | Vertical Prototype |
|---|---|---|
| **Breadth** | All features covered | Few features covered |
| **Depth** | UI layer only | Full stack (UI → Data) |
| **Fidelity** | Medium (visual) | High (functional) |
| **Cost per Feature** | Low | High |
| **Code Reuse** | Low (mostly throwaway) | High (often evolutionary) |
| **Risk Reduction** | Low (no integration tested) | High (integration validated) |
| **Time to Build** | Short | Long |
| **Stakeholder Confidence** | Medium | High |

**Justification for Payment Gateway (3 marks):**
A payment-gateway integration is **architecturally risky** because it involves external API contracts, security (PCI-DSS), and failure-mode handling (timeouts, retries, idempotency). A horizontal prototype — being UI-only — would expose **none** of these risks. Only a vertical prototype that *actually calls the gateway, handles the network response, and persists the transaction* can validate the integration. Therefore, the **vertical prototype is decisively more suitable** for this scenario.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Compute RRF**

$$
R_{before} = P_{before} \times I_{before} = 0.50 \times 0.90 = 0.45
$$

$$
R_{after} = P_{after} \times I_{after} = 0.15 \times 0.90 = 0.135
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
RRF &= 1 - \frac{R_{after}}{R_{before}} \\
&= 1 - \frac{0.135}{0.45} \\
&= 1 - 0.30 \\
&= 0.70
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Reuse Yield**

Given $C_{written} = 750$ and $C_{discarded} = 200$:

$$
T_r = \frac{C_{discarded}}{C_{written}} = \frac{200}{750} = 0.2667
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_y &= 1 - T_r \\
&= 1 - 0.2667 \\
&= 0.7333
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Engineering Significance (2 marks)**

- **RRF = 0.70 (70%):** The vertical prototype removed **70% of the integration risk** before full-scale development. This is a strong justification for the prototyping investment and aligns with the agile principle of *front-loading risk reduction*.
- **R_y = 0.7333 (73.33%):** Approximately **73% of the prototype code is reusable** in production. This high reuse yield confirms that the vertical prototype is functioning as an *evolutionary prototype*, not a throwaway — saving the team substantial development time.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part (b):** Students frequently **swap the formula direction for RRF**, writing $RRF = R_{after} / R_{before}$ instead of $1 - R_{after}/R_{before}$. This yields 0.30 instead of 0.70 and the interpretation becomes "70% risk remains" — an *engineering inversion error* that costs **full marks for that sub-question**. Always state the formula *before* substituting values.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Vertical Prototypes (Module 3, PECNT748)**

- **Definition:** A vertical prototype is a *complete end-to-end functional slice* of the system covering all architectural layers for a *limited* set of features.
- **Mnemonic "V-FLIES":** **V**ertical = **F**ull **L**ayer **I**mplementation for a f**E**w feature**S**.
- **Layer Penetration Index (LPI):** $LPI = L_{impl} / L_{total}$. Threshold for "vertical" = **≥ 0.75**.
- **Fidelity Score:** $F_s = \alpha U + \beta D + \gamma I$. Standard KTU weights: $\alpha = 0.30, \beta = 0.35, \gamma = 0.35$.
- **Risk Reduction Factor (RRF):** $RRF = 1 - R_{after}/R_{before}$. Higher is better; > 0.60 is a strong result.
- **Reuse Yield:** $R_y = 1 - T_r$. Vertical prototypes typically score **> 0.70**.
- **Primary Use Case:** **Architectural validation** and **high-risk integration de-risking**.
- **Contrast with Horizontal:** Wide & shallow vs. narrow & deep.
- **Evolutionary Potential:** Vertical prototypes are *retained* and extended, not thrown away.
- **Best for:** Payment systems, healthcare HCI flows, IoT pipelines, AR/VR gesture loops.
- **Avoid for:** Late-stage projects, low-risk UI changes, broad user-navigation testing.
- **KTU Board Hot-Spots:** (1) LPI threshold value, (2) RRF formula direction, (3) Comparative table of horizontal vs vertical, (4) Real-world scenario justification.
- **Common Valuation Errors to Avoid:**
  1. Confusing *throwaway* with *evolutionary* prototypes.
  2. Inverting the RRF formula.
  3. Omitting interpretation after numerical answers.
  4. Using horizontal prototype terminology when describing a vertical slice.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Task Oriented Prototypes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT748 - PROTOTYPING INTERACTIVE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Task Oriented Prototypes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> A **Task-Oriented Prototype** is an interactive prototype constructed specifically to validate, demonstrate, or evaluate the performance of a *specific user task* or set of tasks within an interactive system. Unlike general-purpose or feature-driven prototypes, the fidelity, scope, and interaction design are deliberately tuned around one or more well-defined tasks drawn from user research, personas, or scenario analyses.

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

A *task* in HCI (Human-Computer Interaction) is a logically grouped sequence of user actions required to achieve a concrete goal. A **Task-Oriented Prototype (TOP)** is therefore a functional (or semi-functional) artifact — paper, low-fidelity, high-fidelity, or digital — that simulates *only* the workflow, decision branches, and data states necessary to perform that task from start to finish.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight:**
> Task-Oriented Prototypes fall under the category of **horizontal vs. vertical prototypes** as a *vertical* prototype, because they implement complete task depth in selected areas, rather than breadth across the entire system.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **choreographer staging a musical**. Instead of rehearsing the whole 3-hour show every day, you isolate the *opening duet* or the *finale number* and rehearse only those until they are flawless. A **Task-Oriented Prototype** does exactly this for an interactive system: it isolates one critical user task — say, "Book a flight" or "Reset forgotten password" — and builds *only* the screens, transitions, and states required to complete that one task end-to-end.

> [!TIP]
> **Think of it this way:** A traditional prototype is like a *map of a country*. A Task-Oriented Prototype is like a *detailed street-level map of one neighborhood* — fewer streets, but every alley, dead-end, and turn is precisely rendered.

### 1.3 Why Task-Oriented Prototypes Matter in Interactive Systems

In real-world product design (apps, kiosks, medical devices, ATMs), certain tasks are **mission-critical** or **conversion-critical**. KTU emphasizes that:

- **Empirical Validation** – Designers can measure *task completion time*, *error rate*, and *learnability* (usability metrics) without building the entire system.
- **Risk Reduction** – The most uncertain or high-impact user journeys are tested early.
- **Communication** – Stakeholders can see *behavior* of the system, not just screens.
- **Iteration Speed** – Smaller scope means faster changes.

### 1.4 Core Components of a Task-Oriented Prototype

A Task-Oriented Prototype typically includes the following structural elements:

1. **Task Statement** – A one-line description of the goal (e.g., "User adds an item to cart and checks out").
2. **Pre-conditions** – What must be true before the task starts (logged in, account exists, etc.).
3. **Step Sequence** – Ordered user actions and system responses.
4. **Decision Branches** – What happens if the user makes an error or chooses alternatives.
5. **Post-conditions / Success State** – The end goal achieved by the task.
6. **Evaluation Hooks** – Data captured for usability testing (time-on-step, click count, etc.).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Standard Metrics (must memorize):**
> - **Task Completion Rate (TCR)** — measured in **%**.
> - **Time on Task (ToT)** — measured in **seconds**.
> - **Error Rate (ER)** — measured in **errors per task**.
> - **Subjective Satisfaction (SUS Score)** — standard System Usability Scale, range **0 to 100**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The "Why" Behind Task-Oriented Prototypes

The theoretical underpinning of task-oriented prototyping comes from **Norman’s Action Cycle** and **GOMS (Goals, Operators, Methods, Selection Rules)** models in HCI:

- **Goals** are decomposed into **Tasks**.
- **Tasks** are decomposed into **Actions** and **Operators**.
- A prototype that exercises a complete task allows the designer to validate the *method selection* and *execution layer* of user interaction.

When you build a **Task-Oriented Prototype**, you are essentially materializing the **M (Methods)** layer of GOMS into a runnable artifact.

### 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Construction

The construction of a Task-Oriented Prototype follows a logical, six-step pipeline:

- **Step 1 — Task Selection**
  - *Why:* You cannot prototype everything; prioritization is mandatory.
  - *How:* Choose tasks with the highest *business impact × user frequency × risk* score. This is often done via a **Prioritization Matrix**.

- **Step 2 — Task Analysis**
  - *Why:* To know exactly what must be simulated.
  - *How:* Use techniques such as **Hierarchical Task Analysis (HTA)**, **Use Case mapping**, or **User Story mapping** to decompose the task into sub-tasks.

- **Step 3 — Fidelity Selection**
  - *Why:* Fidelity must match the question being asked. A paper prototype can answer "is the workflow understandable?" but cannot answer "does it load in under 2 seconds?"
  - *How:* Apply the **Fidelity-Question Matrix** — low fidelity for conceptual questions, high fidelity for performance and polish questions.

- **Step 4 — Prototype Construction**
  - *Why:* To create the testable artifact.
  - *How:* Build only the screens, transitions, and data states needed for the chosen task. Avoid scope creep into unrelated features.

- **Step 5 — User Test Execution**
  - *Why:* Empirical validation is the whole point.
  - *How:* Recruit representative users, give them the task, observe, and record metrics.

- **Step 6 — Iteration Based on Findings**
  - *Why:* The prototype is a learning tool, not a deliverable.
  - *How:* Convert observed pain points into design changes, then re-prototype.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Insight (KTU Expectation):**
> Examiners often test whether students know *why* a particular step exists, not just the step name. Always explain the *why* and *how* in two-mark parts of any 14-mark question.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Metric | Formula | Units | What it Measures |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Task Completion Rate (TCR)** | $\text{TCR} = \frac{N_{\text{completed}}}{N_{\text{attempted}}} \times 100$ | **%** | Proportion of users who finish the task |
| **Average Time on Task (ToT)** | $\overline{\text{ToT}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} t_i$ | **seconds** | Mean task duration |
| **Error Rate (ER)** | $\text{ER} = \frac{E_{\text{total}}}{N_{\text{users}}}$ | **errors/user** | Mistakes per user attempt |
| **Efficiency (E)** | $E = \frac{1}{\overline{\text{ToT}} \cdot \text{ER}}$ | **1/(s·errors)** | Combined speed/accuracy indicator |
| **Learnability (L)** | $L = \frac{T_1 - T_N}{T_1} \times 100$ | **%** | Improvement from 1st to Nth attempt |
| **SUS Score** | $\text{SUS} = 2.5 \times \sum_{k=1}^{10} (U_k - 1)$ | **0 – 100** | Subjective usability rating |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LaTeX Note for Tables:** All vertical bars in formulas above use `\vert` semantically via plain parentheses or explicit `·` (middle dot) to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

### 2.4 Task-Oriented vs. Other Prototype Types — Comparative Analysis

| Dimension | Task-Oriented Prototype | Feature-Oriented Prototype | Horizontal Prototype |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Deep in 1–N tasks | Deep in 1 feature | Broad, shallow |
| **Goal** | Validate task completion | Validate feature behavior | Show overall feel |
| **Best For** | Usability testing | API/functionality testing | Stakeholder demos |
| **Risk Profile** | Low (focused) | Medium | High (lots of design) |
| **KTU 2024 Emphasis** | **High** | Medium | Medium |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical Devices** – Validating that a surgeon can complete "Set defibrillator to 200 J" in < 5 s under stress.
- **Avionics Cockpits** – FAA mandates task-oriented simulation before certification.
- **Automotive HMI** – "Set navigation destination while driving" tested for driver distraction.
- **ATMs & Banking** – "Withdraw cash using cardless mode" tested for first-time users.
- **Kerala KTU Capstone Projects** – Most UI/UX final-year projects use TOP for evaluation chapters.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Task-Oriented Prototype Effectiveness Score (TOPES)

A KTU-style analytical problem often asks you to *derive* or *apply* a composite effectiveness metric for a task prototype. We will derive **TOPES** (Task-Oriented Prototype Effectiveness Score) from first principles.

**Given:** $N$ users attempt a task, with completion success $C_i \in \{0, 1\}$, time on task $t_i$ in seconds, and errors $e_i$.

**Step 1 — Define the binary success indicator.**

$$
C_i = \begin{cases} 1, & \text{if user } i \text{ completes the task} \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

*Logic:* This converts a subjective "did the user succeed?" observation into a numeric value usable in formulas.

**Step 2 — Compute the Task Completion Rate (TCR).**

$$
\text{TCR} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} C_i \times 100
$$

*Logic:* Sum all successes, divide by total users, scale to percentage.

**Step 3 — Compute the Mean Time on Task.**

$$
\overline{t} = \frac{1}{N_C} \sum_{i=1}^{N} C_i \cdot t_i
$$

*Logic:* Only successful completions count toward time efficiency — failed attempts pollute the average.

**Step 4 — Compute the normalized time score.**

$$
T_{\text{norm}} = \frac{t_{\text{target}}}{\overline{t}} \times 100
$$

*Logic:* If users complete in less than the target time, score > 100 (over-efficient). If they take longer, score < 100.

**Step 5 — Compute the Error Score.**

$$
E_{\text{score}} = 100 - 10 \cdot \overline{e}
$$

*Logic:* Penalize 10 points per average error, floor at 0.

**Step 6 — Combine into TOPES.**

$$
\text{TOPES} = w_1 \cdot \text{TCR} + w_2 \cdot T_{\text{norm}} + w_3 \cdot E_{\text{score}}
$$

Subject to the constraint:

$$
w_1 + w_2 + w_3 = 1
$$

*Logic:* Weights $w_1, w_2, w_3$ are chosen by the team; typical values are $w_1 = 0.5$, $w_2 = 0.3$, $w_3 = 0.2$.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem (KTU-style):** 10 users test a task-oriented prototype. Data given:

| User $i$ | Success $C_i$ | Time $t_i$ (s) | Errors $e_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | 45 | 0 |
| 2 | 1 | 60 | 1 |
| 3 | 0 | 90 | 3 |
| 4 | 1 | 50 | 0 |
| 5 | 1 | 55 | 1 |
| 6 | 1 | 48 | 0 |
| 7 | 0 | 120 | 4 |
| 8 | 1 | 52 | 0 |
| 9 | 1 | 49 | 1 |
| 10 | 1 | 51 | 0 |

**Target time:** $t_{\text{target}} = 55$ s

**Step 1 — Compute TCR.**

$$
\text{TCR} = \frac{8}{10} \times 100 = 80\%
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\overline{t}$ over successful users only.**

$$
\overline{t} = \frac{45 + 60 + 50 + 55 + 48 + 52 + 49 + 51}{8} = \frac{410}{8} = 51.25 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $T_{\text{norm}}$.**

$$
T_{\text{norm}} = \frac{55}{51.25} \times 100 \approx 107.32
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\overline{e}$ over successful users.**

$$
\overline{e} = \frac{0 + 1 + 0 + 1 + 0 + 0 + 1 + 0}{8} = \frac{3}{8} = 0.375
$$

**Step 5 — Compute $E_{\text{score}}$.**

$$
E_{\text{score}} = 100 - 10 \times 0.375 = 96.25
$$

**Step 6 — Apply weights** $w_1 = 0.5, w_2 = 0.3, w_3 = 0.2$.

$$
\text{TOPES} = (0.5 \times 80) + (0.3 \times 107.32) + (0.2 \times 96.25)
$$

$$
\text{TOPES} = 40.0 + 32.196 + 19.25 = 91.446
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation Cheat Sheet:**
> - $\text{TOPES} \geq 90$ → Production-ready prototype.
> - $75 \leq \text{TOPES} < 90$ → Minor revisions required.
> - $\text{TOPES} < 75$ → Major redesign required.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flow for Choosing Task-Oriented Prototype

| Condition | Recommended Approach | KTU Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Unknown user behavior | Low-fidelity paper TOP | Fastest to iterate |
| High-stakes domain (medical/aviation) | High-fidelity digital TOP | Realistic measurement |
| Comparing 2 design alternatives | A/B TOP with same task | Isolate design variable |
| Stakeholder buy-in needed | Wizard-of-Oz TOP | Demonstrates future vision |
| Performance-critical task | Functional high-fi TOP | Measures latency & error |
| Educational/teaching context | Paper TOP + think-aloud | Reveals mental model |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Task-Oriented Prototype Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[User Research Phase] --> A1[Identify Candidate Tasks]
    A1 --> A2{Prioritization Matrix}
    A2 -->|High Priority| A3[Selected Task Set]
    A2 -->|Low Priority| A4[Task Backlog]

    A3 --> B1[Task Analysis HTA or GOMS]
    B1 --> B2[Define Pre and Post Conditions]
    B2 --> B3[Map Decision Branches]
    B3 --> B4[Build Step Sequence]

    B4 --> C1{Fidelity Decision}
    C1 -->|Low Fi| C2[Paper or Wireframe TOP]
    C1 -->|Mid Fi| C3[Interactive Mockup TOP]
    C1 -->|High Fi| C4[Functional Coded TOP]

    C2 --> D1[User Testing Session]
    C3 --> D1
    C4 --> D1

    D1 --> D2[Capture Metrics TCR, ToT, ER, SUS]
    D2 --> D3[Calculate TOPES Score]
    D3 --> D4{TOPES Threshold Check}
    D4 -->|Above Threshold| E1[Promote to Design Spec]
    D4 -->|Below Threshold| E2[Iterate and Rebuild]
    E2 --> B1
    E1 --> F1[Production Hand Off]
```

### 4.2 Task Decomposition Schematic (HTA-Style)

```mermaid
graph TD
    root0[0. Reset User Password]
    root0 --> n1[1. Navigate to Login Page]
    root0 --> n2[2. Click Forgot Password]
    root0 --> n3[3. Enter Registered Email]
    root0 --> n4[4. Verify OTP]
    root0 --> n5[5. Set New Password]
    root0 --> n6[6. Confirm and Login]

    n1 --> n1a[1.1 Launch App or Browser]
    n1 --> n1b[1.2 Click Sign In Tab]
    n3 --> n3a[3.1 Validate Email Format]
    n3 --> n3b[3.2 Send OTP Request]
    n4 --> n4a[4.1 Enter 6 Digit Code]
    n4 --> n4b[4.2 Handle Expired OTP]
    n5 --> n5a[5.1 Enforce Strength Rules]
    n5 --> n5b[5.2 Confirm Match]
```

### 4.3 Task-Oriented Prototype Fidelity Selection Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1[Question Type?] --> Q1a[Conceptual?]
    Q1 --> Q1b[Behavioral?]
    Q1 --> Q1c[Performance?]

    Q1a --> R1[Use Low Fi Paper]
    Q1b --> R2[Use Mid Fi Interactive]
    Q1c --> R3[Use High Fi Functional]

    R1 --> O1[Cheap and Fast]
    R2 --> O2[Balanced Insight]
    R3 --> O3[Realistic Data]
```

### 4.4 Component Map of a Task-Oriented Prototype (Functional Blocks)

```mermaid
graph TB
    UI[User Interface Layer]
    LOGIC[Task Logic Engine]
    STATE[State Manager]
    DATA[Mock Data Store]
    METRIC[Metrics Recorder]
    FEEDBACK[Feedback Renderer]

    UI --> LOGIC
    LOGIC --> STATE
    STATE --> DATA
    LOGIC --> METRIC
    METRIC --> FEEDBACK
    STATE --> UI
    DATA --> LOGIC
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note for KTU Students:**
> When asked to "draw a diagram of task-oriented prototyping" in exams, the simplest acceptable figure is **Block 4.4** — it shows all architectural pieces (UI, Logic, State, Data, Metrics, Feedback) clearly, and the examiner can reward marks for correct labeling and arrows.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define a Task-Oriented Prototype. Mention any two situations where it is most useful.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Task-Oriented Prototype (TOP)** is a focused interactive prototype built to evaluate one or a small set of specific user tasks end-to-end, rather than the entire system. It simulates the *actions, decisions, and system responses* required to complete a defined goal.

**Two situations where TOP is most useful:**

1. **Usability Testing of Critical Workflows** — When a single task has high business or safety impact (e.g., ATM cash withdrawal, defibrillator operation), a TOP isolates that flow for empirical validation.

2. **Early-Stage Design Validation** — When the team is uncertain about how users will perform a workflow, a low-fidelity TOP can be built in hours to test assumptions before committing to full development.

*(Examiner's Note: Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark per valid situation. Maximum 3 marks.)*

---

#### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**List and briefly explain any three usability metrics used to evaluate a Task-Oriented Prototype.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Task Completion Rate (TCR)** — The percentage of users who successfully complete the task. Measured in **%**. Formula: $\text{TCR} = \frac{N_{\text{completed}}}{N_{\text{attempted}}} \times 100$.

2. **Time on Task (ToT)** — The average time taken by users to complete the task. Measured in **seconds**. Lower values indicate higher efficiency.

3. **Error Rate (ER)** — The average number of mistakes made per user during the task. Measured in **errors/user**. Lower is better.

4. *(Optional bonus)* — **SUS Score** (System Usability Scale), range **0 to 100**, captures subjective satisfaction.

*(Award 1 mark per metric + explanation.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - Writing only the *definition* and skipping *example/diagram* → lose 2–3 marks.
> - Confusing **Task-Oriented** with **Feature-Oriented** prototypes → lose conceptual marks.
> - Not mentioning **fidelity choice** when discussing implementation → lose 2 marks.
> - Forgetting the **evaluation phase** (TCR, ToT, ER) → lose up to 3 marks.
> - Skipping the *iteration loop* in answers → examiner interprets as incomplete methodology.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`
**(a)** Explain the methodology of constructing a Task-Oriented Prototype in detail. Include the role of task analysis. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe any four usability metrics used to evaluate a Task-Oriented Prototype, with formulas and units. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Methodology of Constructing a Task-Oriented Prototype (7 marks):**

The construction follows a six-stage pipeline:

1. **Task Identification (1 mark)** — Using personas, scenarios, and user research, list candidate tasks. Apply a *Prioritization Matrix* (impact × frequency × risk) to select the most critical task(s).

2. **Task Analysis (1.5 marks)** — Use **Hierarchical Task Analysis (HTA)** or **GOMS** modeling to decompose the chosen task into sub-tasks, actions, and decision points. This produces a clear blueprint of what the prototype must simulate.

3. **Fidelity Selection (1 mark)** — Decide whether the prototype will be low-fi (paper/wireframe), mid-fi (interactive mockup using Figma/Adobe XD), or high-fi (functional code). The decision is governed by the *question being answered* — conceptual, behavioral, or performance.

4. **Prototype Construction (1.5 marks)** — Build only the screens, transitions, and state changes needed for the chosen task. The key principle is *vertical depth in a narrow scope* — implement the task fully, but do not include unrelated features.

5. **User Testing (1 mark)** — Recruit representative users, brief them with the task statement, observe their actions, and capture metrics. Use techniques such as *think-aloud protocol* to surface mental-model issues.

6. **Iteration (1 mark)** — Analyze captured data, identify pain points, modify the prototype, and re-test. The prototype is a *learning artifact*, not a final deliverable.

> **Role of Task Analysis:** It ensures the prototype is built around *real user behavior* rather than *designer assumptions*. HTA provides a structured hierarchy of operations; GOMS provides goal-method decomposition. Together they guarantee that no critical step is omitted from the simulated workflow.

---

**(b) Four Usability Metrics with Formulas (7 marks):**

1. **Task Completion Rate (TCR)** — (1.5 marks)

$$
\text{TCR} = \frac{N_{\text{completed}}}{N_{\text{attempted}}} \times 100 \;\; (\%)
$$

2. **Average Time on Task (ToT)** — (1.5 marks)

$$
\overline{\text{ToT}} = \frac{1}{N_C} \sum_{i=1}^{N_C} t_i \;\; (\text{seconds})
$$

3. **Error Rate (ER)** — (1.5 marks)

$$
\text{ER} = \frac{E_{\text{total}}}{N_{\text{users}}} \;\; (\text{errors/user})
$$

4. **System Usability Scale (SUS)** — (2.5 marks)

$$
\text{SUS} = 2.5 \times \sum_{k=1}^{10} (U_k - 1) \;\; (0 - 100)
$$

where $U_k \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ is the user's response to each of the 10 SUS questionnaire items (odd items: $U_k - 1$; even items: $5 - U_k$).

*Examiners expect students to mention that SUS is the most widely used standardized questionnaire for subjective usability assessment.*

---
