#### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`
**(a)** Compare and contrast Task-Oriented Prototypes with Feature-Oriented Prototypes. Highlight situations where each is preferred. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a Task-Oriented Prototype plan for a "Book a Blood Test" feature in a hospital mobile app. Specify task statement, pre-conditions, key steps, and the metrics you would record. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Task-Oriented vs. Feature-Oriented Prototypes (7 marks):**

| Dimension | Task-Oriented Prototype | Feature-Oriented Prototype |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Complete depth of one user task | Complete depth of one feature module |
| **Primary Goal** | Validate task completion & usability | Validate feature behavior & data flow |
| **Driven by** | User goals and scenarios | Functional specification |
| **Typical Use** | Usability testing | Technical validation, API testing |
| **Fidelity** | Often mid-to-high | Often high (functional code) |
| **Metrics** | TCR, ToT, ER, SUS | Response time, API correctness, throughput |
| **Failure Focus** | Did the user understand the workflow? | Does the feature return correct data? |

**Situations where Task-Oriented is preferred (2 marks):**
- Validating first-time user onboarding.
- Safety-critical workflows (medical, aviation).
- A/B testing alternative workflows.

**Situations where Feature-Oriented is preferred (2 marks):**
- Backend feature integration testing.
- API contract validation.
- Performance benchmarking of a single function.

---

**(b) Design Plan for "Book a Blood Test" TOP (7 marks):**

1. **Task Statement (1 mark):**
   *"As a patient, I want to book a blood test appointment at a nearby lab and receive confirmation within 60 seconds."*

2. **Pre-conditions (1 mark):**
   - User is logged into the hospital app.
   - User profile contains valid name, age, and contact number.
   - Internet connection is available.

3. **Key Steps (3 marks):**
   1. Tap "Book Test" from home screen.
   2. Select "Blood Test" from test catalog.
   3. Choose preferred lab (map view or list).
   4. Select date and time slot.
   5. Confirm patient details.
   6. Apply coupon code (optional).
   7. Make payment via saved card.
   8. Receive confirmation with appointment ID.

4. **Metrics to Record (2 marks):**
   - **Task Completion Rate (TCR)** — % of patients who successfully book.
   - **Time on Task (ToT)** — Should be < 60 s for "happy path".
   - **Error Rate (ER)** — Especially at payment and slot selection steps.
   - **SUS Score** — Subjective satisfaction post-task.
   - **Drop-off Point** — Which step did abandoning users stop at?

> **Note:** A real TOP for this scenario would build *only* these 8 screens and their transitions, ignoring unrelated features like "View Medical History" or "Chat with Doctor."

---

### 5.1 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist (read this 5 minutes before the exam):**

- **Definition:** A Task-Oriented Prototype focuses on *one or a few user tasks* end-to-end.
- **Category:** It is a *vertical prototype* (depth over breadth).
- **Construction Pipeline:** Identify → Analyze → Choose Fidelity → Build → Test → Iterate.
- **Task Analysis Tools:** HTA (Hierarchical Task Analysis), GOMS, Use Cases, User Stories.
- **Fidelity Rule of Thumb:**
  - Conceptual question → Low-fi paper.
  - Behavioral question → Mid-fi interactive.
  - Performance question → High-fi functional.
- **Key Metrics & Formulas:**
  - $\text{TCR} = \frac{N_c}{N} \times 100$ (%)
  - $\overline{\text{ToT}} = \frac{1}{N_c}\sum t_i$ (seconds)
  - $\text{ER} = \frac{E}{N}$ (errors/user)
  - $\text{SUS} = 2.5 \sum (U_k - 1)$ (0–100)
  - $\text{TOPES} = w_1 \cdot \text{TCR} + w_2 \cdot T_{\text{norm}} + w_3 \cdot E_{\text{score}}$
- **Do Not Confuse:** Task-Oriented ≠ Feature-Oriented ≠ Horizontal Prototype.
- **Exam Trap:** Always include *fidelity choice* and *iteration loop* in long answers.
- **Real-World Examples:** ATM cash withdrawal, medical device setup, KTU capstone project evaluation chapters, e-commerce checkout flows.
- **One-liner to remember:** *"A Task-Oriented Prototype is a microscope, not a telescope — it zooms in on one task, not the whole system."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Scenario Based Prototypes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT748 - PROTOTYPING INTERACTIVE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Scenario Based Prototypes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the KTU 2024 scheme framework for **Prototyping Interactive Systems**, a **Scenario-Based Prototype** is defined as a *narrative-driven, contextual prototype* that simulates user interaction with a future system by embedding the system within a richly described, realistic usage situation. It is a hybrid prototyping technique that combines the **narrative power of a scenario** (a story describing how a user might accomplish a task with the system) with the **tangibility of a prototype** (a low-fidelity, high-fidelity, or working artifact).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Exam Ready):**
> *A Scenario-Based Prototype is a contextual prototype in which the system's functionality, behavior, and interface are evaluated not in isolation, but through a written, enacted, or simulated story of use. The scenario provides the "why," "who," "when," and "where" — while the prototype provides the "how."*

According to **Carroll (2000)** and **Rosson & Carroll (2002)**, scenarios are "concrete but flexible descriptions of system use that bridge the gap between abstract requirements and detailed design." When combined with a prototype, they form a powerful requirements-validation and design-communication artifact.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview: The "Movie Rehearsal" Analogy

Think of designing a smart home system. Before building the actual house, the architect doesn't just draw a floor plan — they stage a **day in the life of the family** living in it.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎬 The Movie Rehearsal Analogy:**
> Imagine a film director preparing a movie scene. The director writes a **scenario** (a script describing what each character does, says, and feels in a given situation) and then stages a **prototype** of the scene using stand-in actors and props. Before the actual filming, the director watches the staged scene to see if the story works, if the dialogue feels natural, and if the props are placed intuitively.
>
> In the same way, an interaction designer writes a **usage scenario** (the script) and then builds a **prototype** (the stand-in system) to "act out" the scenario. Stakeholders watch this "rehearsal" and provide feedback *before* the real system is built.

A scenario alone is just text. A prototype alone has no context. **Scenario + Prototype = Contextualized, evaluatable, communicable design insight.**

## 1.3 Three Pillars of a Scenario-Based Prototype

| Pillar | Description | Engineering Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| **The User (Actor)** | A specific persona with goals, skills, and limitations | A "test load" on a structure |
| **The Story (Scenario)** | A narrative of tasks, events, decisions, and outcomes | A "use-case walkthrough" |
| **The Artifact (Prototype)** | A tangible or simulated system that the user interacts with | A "scale model" of a building |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for Board Exams:** Scenario-based prototyping is one of the **contextual design** techniques popularized by Hugh Beyer and Karen Holtzblatt. It emphasizes that a system cannot be designed in a vacuum — it must be evaluated *in situ* (in its real context of use).

## 1.4 Where Does It Sit in the Prototyping Landscape?

Prototypes are typically classified along three axes:
1. **Fidelity** — Low (paper, sketches) vs. High (functional code).
2. **Horizontal vs. Vertical** — Horizontal covers the breadth of features shallowly; vertical goes deep into one feature.
3. **Exploratory vs. Evolutionary vs. Throwaway** — Used in early ideation, iterative refinement, or one-time validation.

Scenario-based prototypes can be **any fidelity**, **horizontal or vertical**, and serve all three lifecycles. What distinguishes them is the **narrative wrapper**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse "scenario" with "use case." A *use case* is a structured, formal UML artifact. A *scenario* is an **informal, story-based description** that includes emotions, context, environment, and subjective user experiences. Scenarios are more human; use cases are more machine-readable.

## 1.5 Why Use Scenario-Based Prototypes? (The Five C's)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Five C's of Scenario-Based Prototyping:**
> 1. **Communication** — Bridges designer-developer-stakeholder language gaps.
> 2. **Context** — Forces consideration of real-world environments.
> 3. **Critique** — Enables early usability evaluation without full implementation.
> 4. **Creativity** — Encourages "what-if" exploration of edge cases.
> 5. **Commitment** — Provides a shared, tangible vision of the future system.

---

**Section 1 Self-Check:** Before moving on, ensure you can answer:
- What is the difference between a scenario and a use case?
- Why is the prototype described as "stand-in" rather than "real"?
- Which three elements form a scenario-based prototype?

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Concept Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Anatomy of a Scenario

A scenario, in the sense of **Rosson & Carroll (2002)**, has a **canonical structure**. Understanding this structure is essential for KTU board exams.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield — The Six Components of a Scenario:**
> 1. **Setting** — The physical, social, and organizational environment.
> 2. **Actors** — The users, with their goals, backgrounds, and tools.
> 3. **Goals** — What the actors want to accomplish.
> 4. **Actions/Events** — The ordered sequence of activities.
> 5. **Evaluation** — How the actor (and designer) measures success.
> 6. **Resolutions / Problems** — What happens when things go wrong.

### 2.1.1 The Four Scenario Types (Critical for 14-Mark Questions)

Carroll's taxonomy is the **most-tested** classification in KTU/HCI exams:

| Scenario Type | What It Describes | KTU Exam Cue Words |
|---|---|---|
| **Problem Scenario** | A current, problematic situation the user faces *before* any new system exists. | "As is," current pain points, baseline. |
| **Activity Scenario** | What the user does step-by-step in the *current* system or environment. | "Today, when…," workflow analysis. |
| **Information Scenario** | How information flows between users, systems, and other actors. | "Data exchange," "when X triggers Y." |
| **Interaction Scenario** | A **new, idealized** future use of the proposed system — the designer’s vision. | "Will be able to," "the new system should…" |

> [!TIP]
> **Board-Exam Heuristic:** If a question says *"describe a future use of the system"* → it is an **Interaction Scenario**. If it says *"describe how the user currently works"* → it is an **Activity Scenario**. Memorize this mapping.

## 2.2 The Scenario-Based Prototyping Process (Carroll's Method)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Four-Phase KTU Methodology:**
> 1. **Phase 1 — Claim Analysis:** Extract and enumerate design claims (assertions about what the system *should* do).
> 2. **Phase 2 — Scenario Instantiation:** Convert each claim into one or more concrete scenarios.
> 3. **Phase 3 — Prototyping:** Build a low-to-high fidelity prototype that operationalizes the scenario.
> 4. **Phase 4 — Evaluation:** Walk stakeholders through the scenario using the prototype, capturing feedback, errors, and design opportunities.

### 2.2.1 Phase 1: Claim Analysis (Deep Dive)

A **claim** is a testable statement about the system's intended effect on the user. Examples:

- "Users will complete a checkout in under 60 seconds."
- "The system will reduce data-entry errors by 40%."
- "A novice user will be able to set up the device without a manual."

Claims can be **designer claims** (about the system), **user claims** (about what users will do), or **world claims** (about the environment).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Claims Matter:** Claims transform abstract requirements into *testable hypotheses*. A scenario-based prototype is essentially a *physical hypothesis test* — you build it to see if the claim holds.

### 2.2.2 Phase 2: Scenario Instantiation (Deep Dive)

For each claim, you write a **narrative** that:
- Names a specific user (or persona).
- Places them in a specific setting.
- Has them perform a specific task.
- Reaches a specific outcome.

**Example (KTU-style):**
> *"Priya, a 32-year-old doctor in a district hospital, arrives at 9 AM to a clinic with intermittent Wi-Fi. She needs to pull up a patient's prior ECG record, annotate it, and add a new prescription. She opens the medical app, taps 'Patient Lookup,' enters the ID, scans the QR on the patient's wristband, and is presented with the ECG visualization. She rotates the screen to see the rhythm strip better, marks an irregular beat, and adds a text annotation. The app syncs the data to the cloud when Wi-Fi returns. She finishes in 90 seconds."*

This scenario instantiates claims about: (a) offline capability, (b) QR code scanning, (c) touch-optimized ECG viewing, (d) annotation tools, (e) cloud sync delay tolerance.

### 2.2.3 Phase 3: Prototyping (Deep Dive)

The prototype can be:
- **Paper-based** (sketched screens the user taps on)
- **Wizard-of-Oz** (a human behind the curtain simulates the system)
- **Wireframe interactive** (clickable Figma/Balsamiq mockup)
- **Functional high-fidelity** (working software, perhaps for one vertical slice)

The **fidelity of the prototype should match the riskiest claim**. If the claim is about emotional response, a paper prototype may suffice. If the claim is about performance under load, a functional prototype is mandatory.

### 2.2.4 Phase 4: Evaluation (Deep Dive)

Evaluation can be:
- **Subjective:** user satisfaction surveys, think-aloud protocols, interviews.
- **Objective:** task completion time, error counts, task success rate.
- **Comparative:** A/B testing between two scenario-driven prototypes.

## 2.3 The Concept Cheat Sheet (KTU 14-Mark Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet — Scenario-Based Prototyping:**

| # | Concept | One-Line Definition | Key Author/Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Scenario** | An informal story of system use, including context and emotion. | Carroll (2000) |
| 2 | **Persona** | A fictional, archetypal user used as an actor in a scenario. | Cooper (1999) |
| 3 | **Claim** | A testable assertion about the system's effect on the user. | Carroll (2000) |
| 4 | **Problem Scenario** | A current, painful situation the user faces. | Rosson & Carroll |
| 5 | **Activity Scenario** | Step-by-step description of current work practice. | Rosson & Carroll |
| 6 | **Information Scenario** | Description of information flow among actors. | Rosson & Carroll |
| 7 | **Interaction Scenario** | Idealized future use of the proposed system. | Rosson & Carroll |
| 8 | **Wizard-of-Oz Prototyping** | A human simulates the system's intelligence behind the scenes. | Kelley (1984) |
| 9 | **Walkthrough Evaluation** | A stakeholder-driven narrative run-through of a scenario + prototype. | Nielsen (1993) |
| 10 | **Contextual Inquiry** | Field study that inspires scenarios and grounds prototypes in reality. | Beyer & Holtzblatt (1998) |
| 11 | **Throwaway vs. Evolutionary** | Throwaway = explored then discarded; Evolutionary = iteratively refined. | Budde et al. (1984) |
| 12 | **Horizontal vs. Vertical Prototype** | Horizontal = broad but shallow; Vertical = narrow but deep. | Yourdon (1986) |

## 2.4 When to Use Scenario-Based Prototyping (Engineering Heuristics)

> [!TIP]
> **Use Scenario-Based Prototypes When:**
> - The system is **novel** and stakeholders cannot imagine it from a specification.
> - The **context of use** is critical (mobile, in-motion, multi-user).
> - You need to **align cross-functional teams** (designers, developers, marketers, executives).
> - The product is **emotionally sensitive** (health, finance, social interaction).
> - You want to **identify edge cases** and unintended use patterns early.

> [!WARNING]
> **Avoid Scenario-Based Prototyping When:**
> - The system's behavior is **purely algorithmic** (e.g., a database engine) — formal specs work better.
> - The user population is **highly homogeneous** and the context is trivial.
> - You need **statistical evidence** at scale — use A/B testing on a functional build instead.

## 2.5 The Linkage: Contextual Design Methodology

Scenario-based prototyping is a cornerstone of **Beyer & Holtzblatt's Contextual Design (1998)**, which consists of six techniques:

1. Contextual Inquiry
2. Interpretation Sessions
3. Work Modeling
4. Consolidation
5. **Personas & Scenarios (← this is where scenario-based prototypes live)**
6. Iterative Design and Testing

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Contextual Design is a frequent 14-mark question. The most common phrasing is *"Explain how scenario-based prototyping fits into the Contextual Design process."* The answer: it is the **5th of 6 steps**, used to translate consolidated user data into testable, communicable design visions.

---

**Section 2 Self-Check:**
- Can you name all four scenario types and give one example of each?
- What is a "claim" in the Carroll sense?
- Why is the Wizard-of-Oz method particularly suited to scenario-based prototypes?

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Methodological Walkthrough & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This topic is from the HCI/Interaction Design discipline and is **non-quantitative**. Following the KTU adaptive-complexity rule, "derivations" are replaced by a rigorous, step-by-step **methodological walkthrough** with worked examples. All notations, hypotheses, and transitions are explicitly written out, mirroring the rigor of a mathematical derivation.

## 3.1 The Derivation of a Scenario-Based Prototype: A Canonical 7-Step Method

Let us treat the construction of a scenario-based prototype as a **derivable process**, where each step has inputs, transformations, and outputs, analogous to a mathematical function.

### **Step 1: Define the Design Problem (the "Premise")**

> *"What user, what context, what problem are we solving?"*

- **Input:** Business goals, user research, competitive analysis.
- **Transformation:** Decompose the problem into a *single-sentence problem statement*.
- **Output:** A clear, narrow design question.

**Worked Example:**
> *Problem Statement:* "Busy parents in urban Kerala want to find trustworthy, same-day domestic help without making 10 phone calls or risking a stranger's safety."

---

### **Step 2: Construct the Persona (the "Actor")**

A persona is a **fictional but data-grounded** archetype. Constructing one is a multi-step transformation:

**Step 2a:** Identify 3–5 demographic axes (age, occupation, tech literacy, location, family structure).
**Step 2b:** Assign a *specific* name, photo, and background story.
**Step 2c:** Identify **goals**, **frustrations**, and **behavioral patterns**.

**Worked Persona:**
> *Anjali, 36, working mother of two, lives in Thrissur, owns a OnePlus Nord, moderate tech literacy. Frustrations: unreliable maids, no way to verify background, language barrier with migrant workers. Goals: trustworthy, fast, transparent booking.*

---

### **Step 3: Extract the Claims (the "Hypotheses")**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Each claim must be **testable** — i.e., it can be *disproved* by observing a user with a prototype.

| Claim ID | Claim (Hypothesis) | Risk if False |
|---|---|---|
| C1 | "Anjali will book a helper within 3 minutes." | UX too complex. |
| C2 | "Anjali will trust a helper who has a photo + Aadhaar-verified badge." | Trust architecture is wrong. |
| C3 | "A 3-tap path from app open to booking confirmation is feasible." | Information architecture is wrong. |
| C4 | "Anjali will return within 30 days for a second booking." | Product-market fit is wrong. |

---

### **Step 4: Instantiate the Scenarios (the "Story")**

For each claim, write **at least one interaction scenario**. Use the canonical narrative form:

> *"On a [day], at [time], [persona] is at [setting] with [available tools]. She wants to [goal]. She [action 1]…[action n]. Then [outcome / new state]."*

**Worked Interaction Scenario (instantiating C1 + C3):**
> *"On a Wednesday at 8:42 AM, Anjali is rushing to get her kids ready for school and herself for work. She opens the 'Sahayi' app on her phone. Tap 1: she selects 'Need a helper today.' Tap 2: she picks the morning 9-to-12 slot. Tap 3: she confirms the booking. The app shows a verified helper named 'Lakshmi' with a 4.8-star rating and a green Aadhaar-verified badge. Anjali taps 'Confirm.' Total time: 1 minute 48 seconds."*

**Worked Problem Scenario (instantiating C2):**
> *"Last month, Anjali booked a helper through a local WhatsApp group. The helper arrived 90 minutes late, did not speak Malayalam, and Anjali could not verify her background. The children were left alone for 20 minutes. Anjali had to cancel her morning meeting."*

---

### **Step 5: Build the Prototype (the "Artifact")**

Match prototype fidelity to the **riskiest claim**.

For our example:
- C1 (speed) and C3 (3-tap) are **navigation and IA claims** → a **clickable wireframe** is sufficient.
- C2 (trust) is an **emotional claim** → use a **Wizard-of-Oz paper prototype** where the verification badge is shown to test trust perception.

**Prototype Specification Table:**

| Claim | Prototype Artifact | Fidelity | Method of Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| C1 | Figma clickable mockup (3 screens) | Medium | Task time measurement |
| C2 | Paper prototype with hand-drawn verification badge | Low | Trust survey, think-aloud |
| C3 | Figma with hot-spotted arrows | Medium | Path-length click count |
| C4 | Same as C1 + C2 (used over 1 week) | Combined | Re-visit frequency |

---

### **Step 6: Run the Walkthrough (the "Evaluation")**

**Procedure:**
1. Recruit 5–8 users matching the persona (or use *substitute users* if exact match is impossible).
2. Sit with each user. Give them the **scenario in writing**.
3. Hand them the **prototype**. Ask them to perform the actions.
4. **Think-aloud protocol:** users narrate their thoughts.
5. Record: completion rate, time, errors, and **critical incidents** (moments of confusion, surprise, or delight).

**Analytical Matrix — What to Measure:**

| Metric | Type | Tool |
|---|---|---|
| Task completion (yes/no) | Binary | Observation |
| Time-on-task | Quantitative | Stopwatch |
| Number of errors | Quantitative | Log |
| Subjective satisfaction (1–5) | Likert | Post-task questionnaire |
| Net Promoter Score (NPS) | Likert | Post-session survey |
| Critical incidents | Qualitative | Field notes / video |

---

### **Step 7: Synthesize, Iterate, and Document (the "Closure")**

Convert raw observations into **design claims revised** and a **scenario refinement table**:

| Claim | Initial Hypothesis | Result | Revised Claim |
|---|---|---|---|
| C1 | 3-minute booking | Avg 2:10 | Confirmed |
| C2 | Badge builds trust | 6/8 users said "yes, I'd book" | Confirmed |
| C3 | 3-tap path | Users did it in 3 taps | Confirmed |
| C4 | Return in 30 days | (Pending) | TBD |

> [!TIP]
> **Board-Exam Ladder:** When asked *"How do you know the scenario-based prototype worked?"* the answer is: **"We ran 5 users, measured 4 metrics, and 3 of 4 claims were confirmed. We then re-scenarioed the 4th claim with a revised prototype and re-tested."** This is the *iterative loop* that examiners love to see.

## 3.2 Worked-Out KTU-Style Numerical-Analogue Exercise

> Although this is a non-quantitative topic, the KTU 2024 scheme often asks for **comparison tables** and **decision matrices** in place of numerical answers. The following is a rigorous **comparative analysis** that mirrors a derivation.

**Problem:** *Compare Scenario-Based Prototyping with Three Other Prototyping Techniques across Five Criteria.*

**Criteria:** (1) Realism, (2) Cost, (3) Time, (4) Stakeholder Buy-In, (5) Error Discovery.

**Methodology:**
- For each technique, score 1–5 (5 = best).
- Sum and interpret.

| Technique | Realism | Cost (inverse) | Time (inverse) | Buy-In | Error Discovery | **Total** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Paper Sketches | 1 | 5 | 5 | 2 | 2 | **15** |
| Wireframes (Balsamiq) | 3 | 4 | 4 | 3 | 3 | **17** |
| High-Fi Functional | 5 | 1 | 1 | 5 | 5 | **17** |
| **Scenario-Based Prototype** | **4** | **3** | **3** | **5** | **5** | **20** |

**Interpretation (to be written in the exam):**
> *Scenario-based prototypes score highest overall because they combine high realism and excellent error discovery (via the narrative walkthrough) with moderate cost/time, while uniquely delivering the highest stakeholder buy-in (because non-technical stakeholders can follow a story).*

---

**Section 3 Self-Check:**
- Can you name all 7 steps of the canonical method?
- Why is the persona step placed *before* the claim step?
- What is the analytical purpose of "critical incidents"?

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Scenario-Based Prototyping Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Define Design Problem] --> B[Step 2: Construct Persona]
    B --> C[Step 3: Extract Design Claims]
    C --> D[Step 4: Instantiate Scenarios]
    D --> E[Step 5: Build Prototype]
    E --> F[Step 6: Run Walkthrough Evaluation]
    F --> G{All Claims Confirmed?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Step 7: Document and Hand Off]
    G -- No --> I[Revise Scenario and Prototype]
    I --> D
    H --> J[End of Process]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the diagram:** The diamond `G{All Claims Confirmed?}` is a *decision gate*. If the evaluation reveals unconfirmed claims, the loop back to Step 4 (redrawing the scenario) is mandatory. This is the **iterative core** of the methodology and is worth 2–3 marks in a 14-mark KTU answer.

## 4.2 The Four Scenario Types — Visual Classification

```mermaid
graph TB
    A[Scenarios in Carrolls Taxonomy] --> B[Problem Scenario]
    A --> C[Activity Scenario]
    A --> D[Information Scenario]
    A --> E[Interaction Scenario]
    
    B --> F[Current painful situation BEFORE any new system]
    C --> G[Step-by-step current work practice]
    D --> H[Information flow among users and systems]
    E --> I[Idealized FUTURE use of the proposed system]
```

## 4.3 The Anatomy of a Scenario (Decomposition)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Persona Actor] --> S[Setting Environment]
    S --> G[Goal and Motivation]
    G --> AC[Action Sequence]
    AC --> EV[Evaluation of Outcome]
    EV --> R[Resolution or Problem]
    R -. Feedback Loop .-> AC
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Functional Mapping:** The above diagram is a *functional architecture* of a single scenario. The dashed line from `R[Resolution or Problem]` back to `AC[Action Sequence]` indicates that in real use, the user may loop back to a previous action when a problem occurs — a critical observation for KTU exam answers.

## 4.4 The Relationship Matrix: Scenario, Persona, Claim, Prototype

```mermaid
graph TB
    CL[Design Claim Testable Hypothesis] --> SC[Scenario Narrative]
    PE[Persona Fictional User] --> SC
    SC --> PR[Prototype Stand-in System]
    PR --> EV[Walkthrough Evaluation]
    EV --> DC[Design Claim Revised or Confirmed]
    DC -. feeds back to .-> CL
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Interpretation for Board Exams:** This is the **closed feedback loop** that distinguishes scenario-based prototyping from simple prototyping. The *Claim → Scenario → Prototype → Evaluation → Claim* cycle is the heart of the method. Memorize this loop.

## 4.5 Contextual Design Pipeline (Where Scenario-Based Prototyping Sits)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CI[1 Contextual Inquiry] --> IS[2 Interpretation Sessions]
    IS --> WM[3 Work Modeling]
    WM --> CO[4 Consolidation]
    CO --> SP[5 Personas and Scenarios]
    SP --> ID[6 Iterative Design and Testing]
    ID -. repeats .-> CI
    SP -. is operationalized by .-> SB[Scenario-Based Prototyping]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Hot Tip:** Step 5 of Contextual Design is *exactly* where scenario-based prototyping lives. Drawing this pipeline and marking Step 5 earns easy 3 marks in a 14-mark question on Contextual Design.

---

**Section 4 Self-Check:**
- Can you draw the closed feedback loop of claim → scenario → prototype → evaluation?
- Which step in Contextual Design corresponds to scenario-based prototyping?

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1:** [KTU University Exam - May 2023]
> **Define the term "Scenario" as used in Interaction Design. Differentiate it from a "Use Case."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- A scenario is an **informal, narrative description of a user interacting with a system** in a specific context. It includes the setting, the user's goals, emotions, actions, and outcomes. (1.5 marks)
- A use case, in contrast, is a **formal, structured UML artifact** that specifies actor-system interactions as a set of pre-conditions, post-conditions, and alternative flows. (1 mark)
- Scenarios are **human-centered and exploratory**; use cases are **developer-centered and prescriptive**. (0.5 mark)

> **Question 2:** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]
> **List and briefly define the four types of scenarios in Carroll's taxonomy.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Problem Scenario** — Describes a current, painful situation the user faces *before* the new system exists. (0.75)
2. **Activity Scenario** — Describes the step-by-step current work practice, *as is*. (0.75)
3. **Information Scenario** — Describes how information flows among users, systems, and external actors. (0.75)
4. **Interaction Scenario** — Describes the **idealized future use** of the proposed system. (0.75)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module 3 Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 scheme mandates **internal choice** for Part B 14-mark questions. **Answer ANY ONE** of the following.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):**

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024 — Modified for Module 3]**
> *(a)* Explain the four-phase Carroll methodology of scenario-based prototyping in detail, with a suitable example. (7 marks)
>
> *(b)* Compare and contrast scenario-based prototyping with paper prototyping and high-fidelity functional prototyping. Justify which is most suitable for designing a mobile health-monitoring app for rural India. (7 marks)

**Model Answer (a) — 7 marks:**

The four phases, in order:

**Phase 1 — Claim Analysis (2 marks):**
- Define the term *claim* as a testable assertion about the system's effect on the user.
- Categorize claims into **designer claims, user claims, and world claims**.
- *Example:* For a rural health-monitoring app, a claim is: *"A 55-year-old farmer with no prior smartphone experience will be able to record his blood pressure using this app after a single 5-minute demonstration."*

**Phase 2 — Scenario Instantiation (2 marks):**
- Convert each claim into a narrative scenario with setting, actor, goal, action sequence, and outcome.
- *Example:* *"Raman, a 55-year-old paddy farmer in Palakkad, has just been given a smartphone by his daughter. She shows him the health app once. The next morning, he wants to record his BP. He taps the home screen, the app launches with a large green 'Measure BP' button. He taps it, holds the phone to his chest… [continues]. He successfully records the value in 2 minutes."*

**Phase 3 — Prototyping (1.5 marks):**
- Select a fidelity appropriate to the claim's risk. For a usability claim about a novice user, a **paper prototype with simulated phone screen** is best — it forces you to confront the layout decisions.
- Mention **Wizard-of-Oz** as a technique for the "measurement" step if the BP-measuring feature is too complex to build.

**Phase 4 — Evaluation (1.5 marks):**
- Use the **walkthrough** method: give the user the scenario, hand them the prototype, ask them to perform, observe, and interview.
- Capture: completion, time, errors, satisfaction, critical incidents.
- Confirm or refute each claim, then re-scenario the failed claims.

---

**Model Answer (b) — 7 marks:**

**Comparison Table (3.5 marks):**

| Criterion | Paper Prototype | Scenario-Based Prototype | High-Fidelity Functional |
|---|---|---|---|
| Realism | Low (sketches) | Medium (real flow, fake content) | High (real screens, real data) |
| Cost | Very low | Low to medium | High |
| Time | Hours | Days | Weeks–months |
| Stakeholder Buy-In | Low (looks unfinished) | **Very high** (story is engaging) | High (looks real) |
| Edge-Case Discovery | Medium | **Very high** (scenarios force it) | Medium |
| Novice-User Testing | Difficult (paper confusion) | **Easy** (narrative guides) | Easy |
| Iteration Speed | Very fast | Fast | Slow |

**Justification (3.5 marks):**
For a **rural health-monitoring app**, scenario-based prototyping is the most suitable because:
1. The **target users are non-technical** — a paper prototype would confuse them, and a high-fidelity build would waste effort. A scenario-based paper prototype is a happy medium: the narrative carries the user.
2. The **context is critical** (low literacy, intermittent connectivity, dusty environment, sunlight glare) — scenarios force the designer to think about these.
3. **Stakeholders (govt. health officials, NGOs, doctors)** can engage with a *story* far better than a wireframe or code.
4. The **edge cases** (low battery, no network, motor tremor in the user's hand, ASHA worker-assisted use) are best surfaced by *interaction scenarios*.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students forget to **categorize claims** into designer/user/world. This loses 1 mark easily.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Students give a *single example* for all four phases instead of a **continuing example** that threads through Claim → Scenario → Prototype → Evaluation. Examiners reward continuity.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** In the comparison question, students produce a *narrative* comparison instead of a *table*. A table is mandatory for 7-mark questions; it shows the examiner you can be systematic.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Students do not **justify** the choice — they say "scenario-based is best" without explaining *why it beats the other two* on at least 3 specific criteria.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Modified for Module 3]**
> *(a)* Describe the six canonical components of a scenario as per Rosson and Carroll. Construct a complete interaction scenario for an online grievance-redressal system of a municipal corporation. (7 marks)
>
> *(b)* What is a "design claim"? Explain with two examples how claims are operationalized through scenario-based prototyping to evaluate a smart-ATM interface. (7 marks)

**Model Answer (a) — 7 marks:**

**The Six Components (3 marks — list with one-line definitions):**
1. **Setting** — physical, social, organizational environment.
2. **Actors** — users with goals, skills, and tools.
3. **Goals** — what actors want to accomplish.
4. **Actions/Events** — ordered sequence of activities.
5. **Evaluation** — how success is measured.
6. **Resolution/Problem** — what happens when things go right/wrong.

**Constructed Interaction Scenario (4 marks):**

> *"On a Monday morning at 10:15 AM, Suresh, a 42-year-old shopkeeper in Kochi, opens the Kochi Municipal Corporation's 'Sahaya' grievance app on his Android phone. He wants to report a broken streetlight near his shop that has made the area unsafe at night. Tap 1: he selects 'Report a Problem.' Tap 2: he picks 'Streetlight' from a category list. Tap 3: he taps 'Use My Location' — the GPS auto-fills the ward and pincode. Tap 4: he takes a photo of the broken streetlight. Tap 5: he adds a 2-line description in Malayalam. Tap 6: he submits. The app shows a confirmation screen with a tracking ID 'KMC-2024-00483' and an estimated 7-day resolution time. Suresh bookmarks the ID. Three days later, the app pushes a notification: 'Your complaint KMC-2024-00483 is being inspected by Ward 12 Junior Engineer.' Seven days later: 'Resolved. Field engineer has replaced the bulb.' Suresh is satisfied and gives a 5-star rating."*

The scenario demonstrates all six components: **Setting** (Kochi street), **Actor** (Suresh), **Goal** (report streetlight), **Actions** (6-step flow), **Evaluation** (7-day SLA, rating), **Resolution** (inspection + fix).

---

**Model Answer (b) — 7 marks:**

**Definition of a Design Claim (2 marks):**
A design claim is a **testable assertion** about the intended effect of a system on a user, a task, or a context. It is the *hypothesis* of design — to be **confirmed or refuted** by user testing.

**Example 1 — Operationalized Claim (2.5 marks):**
- **Claim:** *"An elderly user will be able to withdraw cash from a smart ATM in under 90 seconds using only the regional language."*
- **Scenario Instantiation:** *"On a Saturday morning, 67-year-old Lakshmi from Kottayam, who has never used an ATM, approaches the smart ATM. She selects Malayalam. The screen shows a single large 'Withdraw' button. She taps it. She inserts her card. She enters the amount (₹2000) using large on-screen buttons. She collects the cash and card. Total time: 1 minute 22 seconds."*
- **Prototype:** A clickable Figma mockup of the ATM flow, tested with 5 elderly users.
- **Evaluation Metric:** Task time, error count, abandonment rate.
- **Result:** 4 of 5 users completed in under 90s. Claim confirmed.

**Example 2 — Operationalized Claim (2.5 marks):**
- **Claim:** *"The smart ATM will refuse to dispense cash to a person who is not looking at the camera, enhancing security."*
- **Scenario Instantiation:** *"A man wearing a cap and a mask approaches the ATM. The face-recognition module does not detect his eyes. The screen displays 'Please remove your cap and face the camera clearly.' The man complies. The system dispenses the cash."*
- **Prototype:** A **Wizard-of-Oz** setup where a hidden operator simulates the face-recognition response.
- **Evaluation Metric:** Whether the system communicates the problem clearly; whether the user complies within 15 seconds.
- **Result:** 3 of 5 users took off their cap after the prompt. 2 required a second prompt. Claim partially confirmed; scenario revised to include a louder audio prompt.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Question B):**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students give a **one-paragraph scenario** without explicitly labeling the **six components**. The examiner cannot award full marks for an unmarked answer. Always **bold** or **bullet** the components.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Students define "claim" but do not show the **operationalization loop** (Claim → Scenario → Prototype → Evaluation). Examiners look for this loop; without it, you lose 3 marks.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** For the smart-ATM question, students describe the prototype *but not the evaluation metrics*. Always close the loop: "How will you know the claim holds?"
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Students describe **only one example** when two are asked. Even if both examples are short, give two — examiners expect 2 × 2.5 = 5 marks worth of examples.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Scenario-Based Prototypes:**

- [ ] **Definition:** A scenario-based prototype is a **narrative-driven, contextual prototype** that embeds a system in a realistic usage story.
- [ ] **Three pillars:** Persona (Actor) + Scenario (Story) + Prototype (Artifact).
- [ ] **Carroll's four phases:** Claim Analysis → Scenario Instantiation → Prototyping → Evaluation.
- [ ] **Rosson & Carroll's six components of a scenario:** Setting, Actors, Goals, Actions, Evaluation, Resolutions.
- [ ] **Four scenario types:** Problem, Activity, Information, **Interaction** (most important for new designs).
- [ ] **Key difference from a use case:** Scenario = informal, narrative, emotional; Use case = formal, structured, UML.
- [ ] **Personas are fictional but data-grounded** (not real users).
- [ ] **Claims must be testable** — they can be refuted by observation.
- [ ] **Match prototype fidelity to claim risk:** emotional claims → low-fi paper; performance claims → high-fi code.
- [ ] **Wizard-of-Oz** is a scenario-friendly prototype technique for unbuilt AI/ML features.
- [ ] **Evaluation methods:** think-aloud, walkthrough, task-time, error count, satisfaction surveys.
- [ ] **Contextual Design (Beyer & Holtzblatt):** Step 5 is Personas & Scenarios → scenario-based prototyping lives here.
- [ ] **Iterative loop:** Claim → Scenario → Prototype → Evaluation → Revise Claim → re-test.
- [ ] **For 14-mark answers:** Always provide a **comparison table** + a **justified choice**.
- [ ] **For 3-mark answers:** Always define the term, differentiate from a near-synonym, and give a 1-line example.
- [ ] **Common traps to avoid:** Confusing scenario with use case; giving examples that do not thread through all 4 phases; omitting the iteration loop.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Board-Exam Mantra:**
> *When in doubt, structure your answer as:*
> *(1) Definition, (2) Components/Phases, (3) Example, (4) Comparison, (5) Justification.*
> *This five-part structure is the gold standard for 14-mark KTU ESE answers in HCI/Interaction Design modules.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Traditional Prototyping Methods and Tools - Paper Prototypes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT748 - PROTOTYPING INTERACTIVE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Traditional Prototyping Methods and Tools - Paper Prototypes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECNT748 - Prototyping Interactive Systems)**, **Paper Prototyping** is formally defined as a **low-fidelity, throwaway prototyping technique** in which the designer physically sketches, draws, or assembles representations of an interactive system's user interface using paper-based artifacts such as sheets, sticky notes, index cards, and markers. The technique is rooted in the early *Participatory Design* and *Scenario-Based Design* traditions of Human-Computer Interaction (HCI), and it is used to simulate the structural, navigational, and interactional behavior of a digital system before any code is written.

According to the canonical HCI literature (as reflected in KTU syllabus outcomes mapped to **CO4: Apply traditional prototyping techniques**), paper prototypes serve three engineering purposes:
1. **Communication artifact** between designers, developers, and stakeholders.
2. **Empirical instrument** for early-stage usability evaluation.
3. **Cognitive scaffold** that allows designers to externalize and iterate on ideas cheaply.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim from syllabus spirit):** "A paper prototype is any hand-drawn or paper-based representation of an interactive system that allows designers to simulate user interaction through role-playing, screen-switching, and verbal narration, without the use of functional software."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are an **architect designing a house**. Before pouring any concrete or laying bricks, you first sketch the floor plan on tracing paper using a pencil. The drawing is rough, full of crossed-out lines, and not at all "pretty" — but it lets you walk the client through the layout, change a wall's position in 10 seconds, and discuss flow and proportion cheaply.

**Paper prototyping in interactive systems is exactly this:** it is the *blueprint stage* of UI/UX design. Instead of dragging widgets in Figma or writing HTML, you literally draw rectangles for buttons, sticky notes for menus, and flip between paper "screens" with your hands while a teammate plays the role of the computer. The interaction is *simulated* through human role-play, not executed by software.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Anchor:** Think of paper prototyping as **"Theatre for Software Design"** — the designer is the *stage manager*, the paper sheets are the *sets*, and the user is the *audience* giving live feedback.

### 1.3 Core Characteristics (Bold-Standard KTU Metrics)

A paper prototype is characterized by five universally accepted HCI properties, which KTU examiners frequently test:

- **Fidelity Level: Low** (lowest on the fidelity spectrum alongside sketching)
- **Material Cost: Near-zero** (typically less than **₹100** per prototype session)
- **Iteration Time: 5 to 15 minutes per screen change**
- **Skill Barrier: Minimal** (no programming, no software)
- **Cognitive Focus: Conceptual (what & why)** rather than visual (how it looks)

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Heuristic:** If a question mentions "low-cost, fast, collaborative, early-stage" — the answer is almost always **paper prototyping**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — How Paper Prototyping Actually Works

The engineering logic of paper prototyping can be broken into **six structured steps**, each of which corresponds to a design decision the team must consciously make:

1. **Scope Definition** — The team defines the *boundary* of the system to be prototyped (e.g., only the checkout flow, not the entire e-commerce site).
2. **Screen Enumeration** — The designer lists every distinct visual state the user can encounter (home, search, results, detail, cart, payment, confirmation).
3. **Sketch Production** — Each state is rendered as a rough, hand-drawn screen on an index card or sheet. Layout boxes for headers, content, buttons, and navigation are drawn freehand.
4. **Interaction Mapping** — Adjacent states are linked via *narrative*: "When the user taps *Add to Cart*, the prototype switches from the detail card to the cart card." This mapping is often written on small sticky arrows between cards.
5. **Wizard-of-Oz Simulation** — A human "computer" (often the designer or a teammate) physically manipulates the paper: slides cards, flips sheets, peels off sticky notes, and narrates system responses aloud.
6. **User Walkthrough & Capture** — A real user performs realistic tasks (e.g., "Find a red shoes under ₹2000 and add it to cart") while observers log errors, hesitations, and verbal confusions.

### 2.2 The Fidelity Spectrum — Where Paper Sits

In the KTU 2024 syllabus, prototypes are classified along a **fidelity continuum** that examiners routinely test in Part A. The spectrum runs from **lowest** to **highest** fidelity:

- **Sketch** (pencil on napkin)
- **Paper Prototype** (structured card-based hand-drawn UI)
- **Wireframe** (digital grayscale layout, e.g., Balsamiq)
- **Clickable Mockup** (interactive but non-functional, e.g., InVision)
- **High-Fidelity Prototype** (polished, near-pixel-accurate, e.g., Figma)
- **Functional / Code Prototype** (working software, e.g., HTML/CSS/JS)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fidelity Rule of Thumb (KRH Heuristic):** As fidelity increases, cost increases exponentially, but stakeholder interpretation bias *also* increases. Paper prototypes are deliberately low-fidelity to **suppress visual bias** and force conversations about logic, flow, and content.

### 2.3 Categories of Paper Prototypes

The KTU syllabus specifically recognizes **three sub-categories** under the paper prototyping umbrella:

| Category | Description | Typical Use |
|----------|-------------|-------------|
| **Static Paper Prototype** | A single hand-drawn screen, no transitions simulated | Concept validation, layout discussion |
| **Interactive Paper Prototype** | Multiple cards with explicit transition rules | Full task-flow testing, navigation validation |
| **Role-Playing / Bodystorming Prototype** | Humans act as system components (e.g., one person = the database) | Workflow simulation, error-handling design |

### 2.4 Tools and Materials — The KTU Canonical Toolkit

| Tool / Material | Primary Function |
|-----------------|------------------|
| A4 / Letter paper | Base screen canvas |
| Index cards (3"x5", 5"x7") | Modular, swappable screens |
| Sticky notes (Post-its) | Dynamic elements (popups, menus, toasts) |
| Markers / pens | Drawing UI elements |
| Scissors | Cutting interface components |
| Glue stick | Layering UI overlays |
| Whiteboard / large paper | Wall-based layout planning |
| Camera (smartphone) | Archiving iterations |

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet (Key Metrics & Usability Formulas)

> [!NOTE]
> Although paper prototyping is qualitative, KTU examiners expect familiarity with the **usability metrics** that are *measured during* a paper prototype session. These formulas frequently appear in 14-mark derivations.

| Symbol | Metric Name | Formula | Unit / Range |
|--------|-------------|---------|--------------|
| $T_{s}$ | Task Success Rate | $T_{s} = \dfrac{N_{success}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage (0–100%) |
| $E$ | Error Count | $E = \sum_{i=1}^{N} e_{i}$ | Integer count |
| $t_{task}$ | Mean Task Time | $\bar{t} = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} t_{i}$ | Seconds or minutes |
| $S_{us}$ | System Usability Scale (SUS) | $S_{us} = \sum_{j=1}^{10} (a_{j} - b_{j}) \times 2.5$ | Score (0–100) |
| $R$ | Retention Ratio | $R = \dfrac{C_{retained}}{C_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage |
| $C_{proto}$ | Prototype Cost | $C_{proto} = C_{materials} + C_{labor} \times t_{build}$ | ₹ INR or USD |
| $I_{eff}$ | Iteration Efficiency | $I_{eff} = \dfrac{N_{insights}}{t_{session}}$ | Insights per hour |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Symbol Notice:** All tables above use `\vert`-style separators as standalone columns. Never write absolute values like $\vert x \vert$ inside a table cell — write them as $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\vert\mathit{x}\vert$ in inline math to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Paper prototyping is not a "toy technique" — it is heavily used in **industry-grade** design workflows:

- **Google, Microsoft, IDEO, and IBM** all run internal paper-prototype "design sprints" in the *Understand* and *Sketch* phases of the *Double Diamond* model.
- **Banking & ATM UI design** historically used paper prototypes before regulatory approval for code-based builds.
- **Medical device design (FDA-regulated)** mandates low-fidelity formative evaluations — paper prototypes are the standard first pass.
- **Accessibility research** uses paper prototypes to test cognitive load for users with motor or visual impairments *before* a single pixel is committed.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Process, Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Process Walkthrough — From Blank Sheet to Usability Insight

Because this is an HCI design topic rather than a pure mathematics topic, "derivation" here refers to the **logical, step-by-step transformation of design intent into a tested, validated prototype**. Each step is shown with explicit reasoning.

---

**STEP 1 — Requirement Capture from PRD**

The designer extracts a *user story* from the Product Requirements Document.

$$\text{User Story} = \{\text{As a } \langle role \rangle, \text{ I want } \langle action \rangle, \text{ so that } \langle value \rangle\}$$

Example:
$$\text{As a } \langle \text{online shopper} \rangle, \text{ I want } \langle \text{to filter shoes by size and color} \rangle, \text{ so that } \langle \text{I can quickly find what fits me} \rangle$$

This story defines the **task boundary** for the paper prototype.

---

**STEP 2 — Screen State Enumeration**

From the user story, the designer enumerates all visual states the system must exhibit:

$$S = \{s_{1}, s_{2}, s_{3}, \dots, s_{n}\}$$

For a simple filter-and-find flow:
$$S = \{\text{Home},\ \text{Search},\ \text{Filter Panel},\ \text{Results},\ \text{Product Detail},\ \text{Cart}\}$$

$$\text{Count: } \vert S \vert = 6 \text{ distinct screens}$$

---

**STEP 3 — Card Production (Per Screen)**

Each state $s_{i}$ becomes a physical index card. Standard elements include:

- Header bar (rectangle, top)
- Content area (large rectangle, center)
- Action buttons (rounded rectangles, bottom)
- Navigation breadcrumbs (small text, top-left)

$$\text{Card}_{i} = f(\text{state } s_{i}) \rightarrow \text{hand-drawn artifact}$$

---

**STEP 4 — Transition Matrix Construction**

The designer builds a *transition matrix* $T$ where each entry $T_{ij}$ describes what happens when a user interacts on screen $s_{i}$ to reach screen $s_{j}$:

$$T = \begin{bmatrix} T_{11} & T_{12} & T_{13} & \dots & T_{1n} \\ T_{21} & T_{22} & T_{23} & \dots & T_{2n} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ T_{n1} & T_{n2} & T_{n3} & \dots & T_{nn} \end{bmatrix}$$

Each cell is a *narrative transition*, e.g.:
$$T_{23} = \text{``User taps \textit{Filter} icon on Search} \rightarrow \text{Filter Panel slides in''}$$

---

**STEP 5 — Wizard-of-Oz Simulation Setup**

A human "computer" is assigned the role $H_{c}$ (human-computer). The roles are formally partitioned:

$$R_{total} = \{R_{designer}, R_{user}, R_{observer}, R_{computer}\}$$

$$\text{Where } R_{user} \cap R_{computer} = \emptyset \quad \text{(disjoint — user never plays the system)}$$

The "computer" executes the transition matrix manually:
$$\forall (i,j) \text{ where } T_{ij} \neq \emptyset, \quad H_{c} \text{ swaps Card}_{i} \rightarrow \text{Card}_{j}$$

---

**STEP 6 — Empirical Measurement During the Session**

For each task performed by the user, the observer logs:

$$\text{Log}_{k} = \{t_{start}, t_{end}, e_{count}, s_{initial}, s_{final}, \text{verbal notes}\}$$

Aggregated metrics are then computed:

$$\bar{t}_{task} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=1}^{N} (t_{end,k} - t_{start,k})$$

$$T_{s} = \frac{\sum_{k=1}^{N} \mathbb{1}[\text{success}_{k}]}{N} \times 100\%$$

$$E_{total} = \sum_{k=1}^{N} e_{count,k}$$

---

**STEP 7 — Insight Generation and Iteration**

The team converts raw logs into *design insights*:

$$I = \phi(\text{Log}_{1}, \text{Log}_{2}, \dots, \text{Log}_{N})$$

Where $\phi$ is a qualitative coding function that clusters errors, hesitations, and verbal confusions. Each insight triggers a card modification:

$$\text{Card}_{i}^{new} = \text{Card}_{i}^{old} + \Delta(\text{insight})$$

The cycle repeats until the iteration efficiency metric falls below a threshold (e.g., $I_{eff} < 1$ insight per hour), signaling *design saturation*.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — When to Choose Paper Prototyping

| Decision Variable | Choose Paper Prototype If... | Choose Digital Prototype If... |
|-------------------|------------------------------|--------------------------------|
| Budget | $\leq$ **₹500** | $\geq$ **₹5,000** |
| Time available | $\leq$ **1 day** | $\geq$ **1 week** |
| Fidelity need | Conceptual, low | Visual polish, high |
| Stakeholder count | $\geq$ **3 non-technical** | $\leq$ **2 technical** |
| Iteration count expected | $\geq$ **5** | $\leq$ **2** |
| Interaction complexity | Simple, linear flow | Rich, stateful, animated |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Heuristic:** If a question asks *"Justify the use of paper prototyping in early design stages"*, the answer must invoke at least three of: (1) cost, (2) speed, (3) bias suppression, (4) collaboration, (5) throwaway nature.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Paper Prototyping End-to-End Process Flow

The following Mermaid block renders the complete **paper prototyping lifecycle** as a block-level functional architecture flow. All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed to comply with Mermaid compilation safeguards.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Requirement Capture] --> A2[Screen State Enumeration]
    A2 --> A3[Card Production Per Screen]
    A3 --> A4[Transition Matrix Construction]
    A4 --> A5[Role Assignment]
    A5 --> A6[Wizard of Oz Simulation]
    A6 --> A7[Empirical Data Logging]
    A7 --> A8{Insight Generated?}
    A8 -- YES --> A9[Modify Card and Reiterate]
    A9 --> A6
    A8 -- NO --> A10[Design Saturation Achieved]
    A10 --> A11[Document Final Prototype]
    A11 --> A12[Archive and Discard]
```

### 4.2 Role-Partition Architecture for a Paper Prototype Session

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Designer] --> B2[Builds Cards]
    B2 --> B3[Transition Matrix]
    B3 --> B4[Session Setup]
    B4 --> B5[Human Computer Role]
    B4 --> B6[User Role]
    B4 --> B7[Observer Role]
    B5 --> B8[Live Simulation]
    B6 --> B8
    B8 --> B9[Empirical Logs]
    B7 --> B9
    B9 --> B10[Insight Coding]
    B10 --> B11[Iteration Trigger]
```

### 4.3 Fidelity Spectrum Positioning Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1[Sketch] --> C2[Paper Prototype]
    C2 --> C3[Wireframe]
    C3 --> C4[Clickable Mockup]
    C4 --> C5[High Fidelity Prototype]
    C5 --> C6[Functional Code Prototype]
    C1 -.Low Cost.-> C2
    C2 -.High Bias Suppression.-> C3
    C5 -.High Visual Polish.-> C6
```

### 4.4 Component Inventory — Paper Prototype Materials Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D1[Paper Prototyping Toolkit] --> D2[Static Materials]
    D1 --> D3[Dynamic Materials]
    D1 --> D4[Annotation Tools]
    D2 --> D2a[A4 Sheets]
    D2 --> D2b[Index Cards]
    D2 --> D2c[Printed Templates]
    D3 --> D3a[Sticky Notes]
    D3 --> D3b[Transparent Overlays]
    D3 --> D3c[Cut Out UI Components]
    D4 --> D4a[Markers]
    D4 --> D4b[Whiteboard]
    D4 --> D4c[Camera for Archival]
```

### 4.5 Decision Flow — When to Use Paper vs. Digital Prototyping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    E1[Design Problem Arrives] --> E2{Budget less than 500 rupees?}
    E2 -- YES --> E3{Time less than 1 day?}
    E2 -- NO --> E4[Choose Digital Prototype]
    E3 -- YES --> E5{Early stage concept validation?}
    E3 -- NO --> E4
    E5 -- YES --> E6[Use Paper Prototype]
    E5 -- NO --> E4
    E6 --> E7[Run Wizard of Oz Session]
    E7 --> E8[Iterate or Discard]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Fallback Note:** Per the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE protocol, all physical UI sketches (button shapes, screen layouts) have been abstracted into functional flow diagrams. A hand-drawn screen mockup cannot be faithfully rendered in Mermaid, so the architecture flows above convey the *interactive structure* that a paper prototype simulates.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Define paper prototyping. List any three of its distinguishing characteristics.**

**Model Answer:**

Paper prototyping is a low-fidelity, throwaway prototyping technique in which the user interface of an interactive system is represented using hand-drawn artifacts such as paper sheets, index cards, and sticky notes. The prototype is manipulated by a human "computer" who simulates system responses through role-play, narration, and physical card-switching.

Three distinguishing characteristics:
1. **Low fidelity** — deliberately rough, no visual polish.
2. **Low cost** — material cost is negligible (typically under ₹100).
3. **High modifiability** — cards can be redrawn, swapped, or discarded in minutes.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Differentiate between low-fidelity and high-fidelity prototypes. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Low-Fidelity Prototype | High-Fidelity Prototype |
|-----------|------------------------|--------------------------|
| Visual realism | Rough, hand-drawn | Pixel-accurate, polished |
| Cost | Negligible | High (tools + labor) |
| Time to build | Minutes to hours | Days to weeks |
| Skill required | Drawing only | Specialized software |
| Bias level | Suppresses visual bias | Invites visual critique |
| Example | Paper prototype, sketch | Figma mockup, HTML/CSS prototype |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **step-by-step process of conducting a usability test using paper prototypes**. Include the roles involved, the Wizard-of-Oz technique, and the metrics collected. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **advantages and limitations of paper prototyping** in interactive system design. When would you *not* recommend its use? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

The paper prototype usability test is executed through the following structured steps:

**Step 1 — Define the task scenario.** A realistic user goal is set, e.g., *"Find a black laptop bag under ₹2000 and add it to the cart."*

**Step 2 — Assign roles.** Four roles are partitioned:
- *Designer* — narrates context, answers questions
- *User* — performs the task on the paper prototype
- *Observer* — silently logs time, errors, and verbal cues
- *Human Computer* — physically swaps cards and simulates responses

**Step 3 — Set up the prototype.** Cards representing all screen states are laid out on a table. The transition matrix is documented on a separate sheet.

**Step 4 — Conduct the Wizard-of-Oz simulation.** The human computer executes transitions based on the user's actions. The user believes they are interacting with a real system; in reality, the "computer" is a person following the transition matrix.

**Step 5 — Capture empirical data.** For each task, the following are recorded:
- Start and end times → enables computation of $\bar{t}_{task}$
- Error count $e_{count}$ → enables computation of $E_{total}$
- Success / failure flag → enables computation of $T_{s}$
- Verbal think-aloud transcripts → qualitative insight source

**Step 6 — Aggregate and analyze.** The metrics are computed:

$$T_{s} = \frac{N_{success}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$$

$$\bar{t}_{task} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=1}^{N} t_{k}$$

**Step 7 — Iterate.** Insights are translated into card modifications, and the cycle repeats until design saturation is reached.

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Advantages:**
1. **Cost-effective** — materials cost is negligible.
2. **Speed** — a multi-screen flow can be sketched in 30–60 minutes.
3. **Bias suppression** — rough visuals prevent stakeholders from fixating on colors, fonts, or icons.
4. **Collaboration-friendly** — non-technical stakeholders can contribute by drawing on the same sheets.
5. **Throwaway nature** — designers are psychologically liberated to discard bad ideas.
6. **Early detection of flow errors** — navigation and logic flaws surface before any code is written.

**Limitations:**
1. **No realistic interaction** — gestures, animations, hovers, and transitions cannot be simulated.
2. **Static fidelity** — does not test visual design quality, typography, or color contrast.
3. **Slow to manipulate** — for very complex flows (50+ screens), paper becomes unwieldy.
4. **Not suitable for asynchronous remote testing** — requires physical co-presence.
5. **Risk of being dismissed** — stakeholders accustomed to polished mockups may underestimate its value.

**When *not* to use paper prototyping:**
- When testing animations, micro-interactions, or rich media.
- When conducting remote or unmoderated usability tests.
- When the project requires accessibility validation that depends on actual rendering (e.g., screen reader testing).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Supplementary 2023]`

**(a)** Describe the **tools and materials used in paper prototyping**. Categorize them by purpose. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **paper prototyping with digital prototyping tools** (Figma, Balsamiq, Axure). Under what design conditions is each preferred? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

The paper prototyping toolkit is conventionally grouped into three purpose-based categories:

**Category 1 — Base Materials (Static Canvases):**
- A4 / Letter paper sheets
- Index cards (3"×5" or 5"×7")
- Pre-printed UI templates (often called "stencil kits")
- Graph paper for layout precision

**Category 2 — Dynamic Materials (Movable Components):**
- Sticky notes (Post-its) — for popups, dropdowns, toasts
- Transparent acetate overlays — for hover states or focus modes
- Pre-cut UI element pieces (rectangles, circles, arrows)

**Category 3 — Annotation and Capture Tools:**
- Markers / pens (multiple colors for semantic coding: red = error, green = success, blue = navigation)
- Whiteboard or large butcher paper for wall-based flow mapping
- Smartphone / camera for iteration archival

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Marker Color Convention (Industry Standard):** Black for layout, red for errors or warnings, green for confirmations, blue for navigation. This semantic coding makes paper prototypes more legible in stakeholder reviews.

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

| Parameter | Paper Prototype | Digital Prototype (Figma, Balsamiq, Axure) |
|-----------|-----------------|---------------------------------------------|
| **Fidelity** | Low | Low to high (depends on tool) |
| **Cost** | Negligible | Subscription-based (₹0 to ₹15,000/year) |
| **Time to build** | Minutes | Hours to days |
| **Iteration speed** | Instant (redraw card) | Medium (undo, edit layers) |
| **Collaboration** | Physical co-location | Real-time remote |
| **Animation support** | None | Full (in Figma, Axure) |
| **Stakeholder perception** | Sometimes dismissed | Often over-interpreted |
| **Documentation** | Manual (photos) | Automatic (version history) |

**Preferred Conditions:**

- **Use Paper Prototyping** when: budget is tight, time is short, fidelity is intentionally low, and the goal is to validate *concepts and flows* before any visual commitment.

- **Use Digital Prototyping** when: visual polish is being validated, remote collaboration is required, animations and micro-interactions must be tested, or stakeholder sign-off demands a "presentation-ready" artifact.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Most Common Mark Loss:**
> 1. **Forgetting to name the human role explicitly** — always say *"a human plays the role of the computer"*. Saying only "we test the prototype" loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Conflating "low-fidelity" with "low quality"** — paper prototypes are deliberately rough; this is a *feature*, not a bug. Examiners deduct marks if you imply paper prototyping is "inferior."
> 3. **Skipping the Wizard-of-Oz explanation** — many students describe the cards but forget to explain *who manipulates them* and *how transitions are simulated*. This is a 3-mark deduction in 14-mark answers.
> 4. **Ignoring metrics** — usability answers without $T_{s}$, $\bar{t}_{task}$, or $E_{total}$ are considered incomplete. Always include at least two formulas in a 14-mark derivation.
> 5. **Omitting the throwaway vs. evolutionary distinction** — paper prototypes are *throwaway* (discarded after learning), unlike evolutionary prototypes that grow into the final product. This distinction is worth 1–2 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Read this 5 minutes before the exam):**

- **Paper prototyping = low-fidelity, throwaway, paper-based, hand-drawn UI representation** simulated by human role-play.
- **Primary purpose:** early-stage concept validation, flow testing, stakeholder communication.
- **Three sub-categories:** Static, Interactive, Role-Playing (Bodystorming).
- **Four canonical roles:** Designer, User, Observer, Human-Computer.
- **Wizard-of-Oz technique:** a human simulates the system's responses; the user is unaware.
- **Fidelity spectrum order (low → high):** Sketch → Paper → Wireframe → Clickable Mockup → High-Fi → Code Prototype.
- **Key advantages (must mention at least 3):** cost, speed, bias suppression, collaboration, throwaway nature.
- **Key limitations (must mention at least 2):** no animation, not for remote testing, scale issues for complex flows.
- **Mandatory formulas for 14-mark questions:**
  - $T_{s} = \dfrac{N_{success}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$
  - $\bar{t}_{task} = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{k=1}^{N} t_{k}$
  - $E_{total} = \sum_{k=1}^{N} e_{count,k}$
- **Throwaway vs. Evolutionary:** Paper prototypes are *throwaway* — they are discarded once learnings are extracted.
- **Color-coding convention:** Black = layout, Red = errors, Green = success, Blue = navigation.
- **Industry users:** Google, Microsoft, IDEO, IBM, FDA-regulated medical device design, banking/ATM design.
- **KTU 2024 CO mapping:** This topic directly satisfies **CO4** — *Apply traditional prototyping techniques* and partially supports **CO5** — *Evaluate prototypes through usability testing*.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wizard of Oz Prototypes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECNT748 - PROTOTYPING INTERACTIVE SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Wizard of Oz Prototypes ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Wizard of Oz (WoZ) Prototyping** is a low-fidelity evaluation and design technique in Human-Computer Interaction (HCI) where a participant interacts with what they believe to be a fully autonomous interactive system, while a hidden human operator (the "wizard") simulates or controls part (or all) of the system's responses from behind the scenes. The method was formally introduced by **John F. Kelley** in his seminal 1984 MIT paper *"An iterative design methodology for user-friendly natural language office information applications."* The core idea is to **simulate the future capabilities of a system that does not yet exist (or is too expensive to build)**, allowing designers to gather realistic user data, test interaction paradigms, and validate design assumptions early in the development lifecycle. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Man Behind the Curtain Imagine a small child watching a puppet show. The child sees a magnificent talking dragon on stage, roaring, breathing smoke, and answering questions from the audience. The child fully believes the dragon is a real, magical creature. In reality, a puppeteer is hidden behind a curtain, operating levers, voicing the dragon, and controlling the smoke machine. > [!NOTE] > **The Magic of Misattribution:** The user (child) attributes intelligence and capability to the system (dragon) that is actually being provided by a human (puppeteer). In a Wizard of Oz prototype, the user attributes intelligence to the computer, but the "intelligence" is the human operator. In a real WoZ prototype, the **user sits in front of a screen or device**, types or speaks commands, and receives responses that *appear* to come from an intelligent machine. Unbeknownst to the user, a researcher in another room is reading the input and manually generating the response. ### 1.3 Core Terminology | Term | Meaning | |---|---| | **The Wizard** | The hidden human operator who simulates the system's intelligence or responses. | | **The User / Participant** | The person interacting with the prototype, unaware of the human behind it. | | **The Veil of Ignorance** | The deliberate deception (or withheld information) that maintains the illusion of automation. | | **Wozzer / Wizard** | Synonymous terms for the operator. | | **Mock-up Interface** | The visible surface the user interacts with. | | **Back-End Simulation** | The hidden manual control mechanism. | > [!TIP] > KTU students should remember the **three pillars** of a WoZ study: **(1) User**, **(2) Wizard**, and **(3) Illusion of Autonomy**. If any pillar collapses (e.g., the user discovers the wizard), the experiment loses validity. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework ### 2.1 The Operational Theory — Why It Works The WoZ technique rests on a critical HCI principle: **the user's mental model is shaped by behavior, not by internal architecture.** Users do not care *how* a system produces a response; they care only that the response is **coherent, timely, and contextually appropriate**. This means a prototype does not need real AI, real parsers, or real databases to feel "real" to a user. It only needs to *behave* as if it were intelligent. By leveraging this, designers can: - **Test interaction models** without investing in unproven technology. - **Gather authentic user data** in a controlled environment. - **Validate AI/NLP assumptions** before expensive implementation. - **Explore "magical" or futuristic UX ideas** that current tech cannot support. ### 2.2 Taxonomy of Wizard of Oz Prototypes Not all WoZ studies are the same. The wizard's role can vary in granularity: 1. **Full Wizard Control (Complete Simulation):** The wizard generates *every* system response from scratch. Used when no part of the system is technically feasible yet. 2. **Partial Wizard Control (Hybrid Simulation):** Some components (e.g., a working database) are real, but the "intelligent" layer (e.g., natural language understanding) is simulated by the wizard. 3. **Wizard as Fallback (Reactive Simulation):** The system tries to handle input automatically, but the wizard intervenes when the system fails, ensuring the user never sees a system error. 4. **Wizard as Initiator (Proactive Simulation):** The system runs autonomously, but the wizard occasionally triggers unexpected events (e.g., a notification) to test how users react. ### 2.3 When to Use Wizard of Oz Prototyping — The Decision Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > WoZ prototyping is **most valuable in early-to-mid stage design** when the technology is unproven, expensive, or unavailable, but designers need to understand user behavior and expectations. | Use Case | Why WoZ Fits | |---|---| | **Natural Language Interfaces (chatbots, voice assistants)** | NLP is hard to build; a human can simulate conversation realistically. | | **Embodied Conversational Agents (ECAs)** | Facial expressions, gestures, and emotional responses are easier to act than to animate. | | **Adaptive / Personalized Systems** | The wizard can manually apply "personalization" rules to study user reactions. | | **Futuristic / Speculative UIs** | Enables testing of concepts like brain-computer interfaces or AR overlays. | | **Search & Recommendation Engines** | Wizard pretends to be a smart recommender. | | **Robotics & Autonomous Vehicles** | Human tele-operates the robot to test user trust and interaction patterns. | ### 2.4 Advantages vs. Disadvantages — KTU Exam Favorite | **Advantages** | **Disadvantages** | |---|---| | **Low cost & fast:** No need to build real AI/ML pipelines. | **Wizard latency:** Humans are slower than computers; this can break the illusion. | | **High fidelity of interaction:** Users behave naturally because they believe it's real. | **Ethical concerns:** Deliberate deception requires careful debriefing and informed consent. | | **Validates design assumptions early:** Saves years of misdirected engineering. | **Limited scalability:** One wizard per user; cannot test 10,000 users simultaneously. | | **Enables research into "impossible" systems:** Test future tech today. | **Wizard bias:** The wizard's own intuitions can leak into responses, polluting data. | | **Rich qualitative data:** Researchers observe real user strategies, errors, and expectations. | **Single-session only:** The illusion cannot be maintained across multiple sessions without sophisticated infrastructure. | ### 2.5 The "Cheat Sheet" — KTU High-Yield Facts to Memorize > [!NOTE] > The following table is the **minimum expected knowledge** for a PECNT748 exam question on WoZ prototyping. | **Aspect** | **Key Fact** | |---|---| | **Origin / Year** | John F. Kelley, **1984**, MIT. | | **Name origin** | The 1939 MGM film *The Wizard of Oz*, where the "great Oz" is exposed as an ordinary man behind a curtain. | | **Core principle** | User attributes intelligence to the system; the intelligence is human. | | **Primary use** | Testing natural language, conversational, and intelligent systems before they are built. | | **Wizard's role** | Simulate system responses, often via a hidden terminal, keyboard, or remote control. | | **Ethical requirement** | **Informed consent** (in some protocols) and **mandatory post-experiment debriefing**. | | **Data type produced** | Qualitative (think-aloud protocols, observation logs) and quantitative (task completion times, error rates). | | **Limitations** | Wizard latency, scalability, potential bias, single-session validity. | ## 3. Step-by-Step Process — Conducting a WoZ Study The Wizard of Oz methodology is not ad-hoc. It follows a **structured six-phase process** that KTU examiners frequently ask students to describe in full. ### Phase 1: Define the Research Question and Scope Before any code or interface is built, the design team must clearly state: - **What system are we simulating?** (e.g., a voice-based travel booking assistant) - **What do we want to learn?** (e.g., *What vocabulary do users naturally use when booking flights?*) - **What is the boundary of the wizard's role?** (e.g., The wizard handles intent recognition and confirmation; flight data comes from a real database.) > [!TIP] > A common student pitfall is **vague scoping**. KTU examiners reward answers that explicitly state *"The wizard simulates X, while Y is automated."* ### Phase 2: Design the User-Facing Interface The interface must be **just functional enough** to support realistic interaction but need not be polished. Common choices include: - **A chat window** (most common, easiest to fake) - **A voice interface** (more immersive, but requires audio routing) - **A mock GUI** (for visual tasks) - **A physical prop** (for AR, VR, or robotics studies) The interface should be on a **separate screen or device** from the wizard's control panel, so the user cannot see the wizard's actions. ### Phase 3: Design the Wizard's Control Interface This is the **hidden cockpit** from which the operator runs the show. A well-designed wizard interface should provide: - **Pre-canned response templates** for common scenarios (e.g., "Booking confirmed," "Sorry, I didn't catch that"). - **A live transcript** of the user's input. - **Quick action buttons** for sub-tasks (e.g., `Look up flight`, `Confirm booking`, `Ask for clarification`). - **Latency feedback** so the wizard knows if their delay is becoming suspicious (typical acceptable delay: **1–3 seconds**). ### Phase 4: Pilot Test (Dry Run) Before involving real participants, the team runs **2–3 pilot sessions** with internal volunteers. The goal is to: - Identify **broken interaction flows**. - Calibrate the **wizard's response time**. - Refine the **deception script** (the "cover story" given to participants). - Train the wizard to be **consistent, fast, and in-character**. > [!WARNING] > Skipping the pilot test is a **common KTU exam answer mistake**. Always mention the pilot phase. ### Phase 5: Run the Experiment with Real Participants The actual session proceeds as follows: 1. **Greeting & Briefing:** The participant is told they will use a new prototype. They are *not* told a human is involved.
