(Some studies use a "partial disclosure" consent form that mentions "a human-assisted simulation" — this is the **ethical gray area** of WoZ.) 2. **Task Execution:** The participant is given realistic tasks (e.g., *"Book a flight from Trivandrum to Bangalore for next Tuesday"*). They interact with the system naturally. 3. **Wizard Operation:** The wizard observes input and generates responses. The wizard may also take **observational notes** about the participant's hesitations, frustrations, or unexpected strategies. 4. **Think-Aloud Protocol (optional):** The participant is asked to verbalize their thoughts as they work, producing rich qualitative data. 5. **Debriefing:** Immediately after the session, the participant is **fully informed** about the wizard and given the opportunity to ask questions. This is the **ethical cornerstone** of the method. ### Phase 6: Data Analysis and Iteration The collected data is analyzed to: - Extract **vocabulary, intents, and dialogue patterns** for future NLP training. - Identify **usability pain points** in the interface. - Refine the **system's design requirements**. - Inform the next iteration of the prototype, eventually transitioning to a fully automated system. ### Summary: The Six-Phase Flow $$\text{Scope} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Interface} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Wizard Cockpit} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Pilot} \;\rightarrow\; \text{User Sessions} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Analysis}$$ > [!NOTE] > The **transition point** from WoZ to a real system is a key concept: WoZ prototyping is a **bridge** between low-fidelity paper sketches and high-fidelity functional prototypes. It is a *temporary* stand-in, not a permanent solution. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 High-Level Architecture of a Wizard of Oz Prototype The diagram below shows the **functional architecture** of a typical WoZ setup. The user on the left is physically and informationally separated from the wizard on the right, with the only connection being the simulated interaction channel. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph USER_SIDE["USER ENVIRONMENT"] direction TB U1["User / Participant"] U2["Task Instructions\n(Briefing Sheet)"] U3["User-Facing Interface\n(Chat Window, Voice UI,\nor Mock GUI)"] U1 --> U2 U1 --> U3 end subgraph OBSERVATION["OBSERVATION CHANNEL"] O1["Think-Aloud Audio\nScreen Recording\nFacial Expression Camera"] end subgraph WIZARD_SIDE["WIZARD ENVIRONMENT (HIDDEN)"] direction TB W1["Wizard / Human Operator"] W2["Wizard Control Interface\n(Pre-canned Responses,\nLive Transcript, Action Buttons)"] W3["Knowledge Sources\n(Manuals, Databases,\nDomain Rules)"] W4["Response Generator\n(Human Cognition)"] W1 --> W2 W2 --> W4 W3 --> W4 end U3 -- "User Input\n(typed, spoken,\nclicked)" --> W2 W4 -- "Simulated Output\n(returned as\nif from system)" --> U3 U1 -. "Behavior &\nVerbalizations" .-> O1 O1 -. "Recorded Data" .-> WIZARD_SIDE style USER_SIDE fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px style WIZARD_SIDE fill:#fff0e6,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px style OBSERVATION fill:#f0f0f0,stroke:#666666,stroke-width:1px,stroke-dasharray: 5 5 ``` ### 4.2 Process Flow — Sequence of Events in a Single WoZ Session This sequence diagram illustrates the **temporal flow** of a typical participant interaction, making the **latency-bound illusion** explicit. ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant User participant Interface as User Interface participant Channel as Interaction Channel participant Wizard as Hidden Wizard participant Recorder as Observation Recorder User->>Interface: 1. Reads task User->>Interface: 2. Speaks or types command Interface->>Channel: 3. Transmit user input Channel->>Wizard: 4. Wizard receives raw input Wizard->>Wizard: 5. Interprets intent (1-3 sec) Wizard->>Channel: 6. Wizard sends crafted response Channel->>Interface: 7. Display response to user Interface->>User: 8. User sees/hears system reply User->>User: 9. Reacts (verifies, corrects,\ncontinues task) Recorder-->>Wizard: 10. Records video, audio,\nand screen throughout Note over User,Wizard: VEIL OF IGNORANCE: User never knows
a human is generating the responses. Note over Wizard,Recorder: POST-SESSION: Debriefing reveals the truth. ``` ### 4.3 Comparison Map — WoZ vs. Other Prototyping Methods A common KTU exam question asks students to **distinguish WoZ from other prototyping techniques**. The following diagram maps the relationships. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Prototyping Techniques"] A --> B["Low-Fidelity\n(Paper, Sketches)"] A --> C["Mid-Fidelity\n(Wireframes, WoZ)"] A --> D["High-Fidelity\n(Functional Code)"] B --> B1["Paper Prototyping\nStoryboarding\nCard Sorting"] C --> C1["Wizard of Oz Prototyping\nInteractive Mockups\nVideo Prototyping"] D --> D1["Functional Prototypes\nBeta Versions\nA/B Testing"] C1 -. "Bridge between\nconcept and code" .- B1 C1 -. "Validates design\nbefore expensive build" .- D1 style A fill:#ffeb99,stroke:#cc9900,stroke-width:2px style C1 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:3px ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]** **Define Wizard of Oz Prototyping. Why is it called so?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Wizard of Oz Prototyping is a usability evaluation technique in which a user interacts with a system that appears to be fully automated and intelligent, but whose responses are actually being generated by a hidden human operator (the "wizard"). It is called so because, just like in the 1939 film *The Wizard of Oz* where the supposedly powerful wizard was revealed to be an ordinary man operating machinery behind a curtain, the apparent "intelligence" of the system is actually a human behind the scenes. (3 marks) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary]** **List any three advantages and two disadvantages of Wizard of Oz Prototyping.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Advantages:** (1) Allows testing of systems whose underlying technology is not yet built or is too expensive. (2) Produces realistic, high-fidelity user behavior data at low cost. (3) Enables validation of natural language and conversational interfaces before NLP development. **Disadvantages:** (1) Limited scalability — one wizard can serve only one user at a time. (2) Potential wizard bias and latency issues can break the illusion. (3 × 1 = 3 marks) --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) **Q. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Expected]** **(a)** Explain the step-by-step methodology of conducting a Wizard of Oz study with a suitable example. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the ethical considerations that must be addressed when conducting a Wizard of Oz experiment. **(7 marks)** #### **OPTION A — Model Answer** **(a) Step-by-Step Methodology of a WoZ Study (7 marks)** Consider a study aimed at designing a **voice-based hospital appointment booking system** for elderly users in Kerala. 1. **Define Scope and Research Question (1 mark):** The team defines the goal — *"To understand the natural language vocabulary, hesitations, and clarification patterns used by elderly users when booking medical appointments via voice."* The wizard will simulate the speech understanding and response generation; the appointment database is real. 2. **Design User-Facing Interface (1 mark):** A simple tablet-based voice interface is built showing a microphone icon, a transcript area, and a confirmation button. The interface has minimal styling to avoid distracting from the interaction itself. 3. **Design the Wizard's Cockpit (1 mark):** A separate laptop runs a custom control panel showing the live audio transcript, pre-canned responses ("Which doctor?", "What time?", "Booking confirmed"), and quick action buttons to query the real database. 4. **Pilot Test (1 mark):** Three internal volunteers are used. The team discovers that the wizard's average response time of 5 seconds feels too slow; this is reduced to 2 seconds. The cover story is also refined: participants are told they are testing a "new prototype voice assistant." 5. **Conduct User Sessions (2 marks):** Twenty elderly participants are recruited. Each is briefed, asked to book appointments, and observed via screen recording. The wizard simulates all "intelligent" responses in real-time. Think-aloud protocol is encouraged. Sessions last 20–30 minutes each. 6. **Analysis and Iteration (1 mark):** The recorded data is transcribed and analyzed for common phrases (e.g., "I want to see the heart doctor next week morning"), error patterns, and clarification requests. These findings feed into the design of the real NLP model and the next iteration of the interface. **(b) Ethical Considerations in WoZ Experiments (7 marks)** 1. **Informed Consent (2 marks):** True informed consent requires that participants know *exactly* what they are consenting to. In WoZ, there is an inherent tension: if participants are told a human is involved, the illusion collapses and the data becomes invalid. Most protocols use a **"partial disclosure"** consent form that mentions "human-assisted simulation" without full details. The participant must agree to this. 2. **Deception and Trust (2 marks):** Withholding the wizard's role constitutes a form of deception. Researchers must justify this deception by showing that the research goal **cannot be achieved any other way** (a principle known as *necessity of deception*). 3. **Debriefing (2 marks):** Immediately after the session, the participant must be **fully debriefed** — told the truth about the wizard, given an explanation of why the deception was necessary, and allowed to ask questions or withdraw their data. This is non-negotiable in modern research ethics. 4. **Data Privacy and Wellbeing (1 mark):** Any personal data collected (e.g., health details in the hospital example) must be anonymized and securely stored. The participant must not be exposed to any physical, emotional, or psychological harm during the session. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students *only* mention "informed consent" and skip the **mandatory post-session debriefing**. This is the **#1 most common reason for losing marks** on this question. Always structure your ethics answer as: **(1) Consent, (2) Necessity of Deception, (3) Debriefing, (4) Data Protection**. Mention all four to score full marks. --- #### **OPTION B — Alternative Model Answer** **(a) Compare Wizard of Oz Prototyping with traditional paper prototyping. When would you choose one over the other? (7 marks)** **Comparison Table (4 marks):** | **Aspect** | **Paper Prototyping** | **Wizard of Oz Prototyping** | |---|---|---| | **Fidelity** | Very low (sketches on paper) | Medium (functional interaction) | | **Simulated component** | None — fully manual swap by designer | The "intelligent" backend is simulated | | **User behavior** | Users may suspend disbelief but act artificially | Users behave naturally because they believe the system is real | | **Best for** | Early concept exploration, layout design | Testing conversational, adaptive, or intelligent features | | **Cost** | Extremely low | Low to moderate | | **Time to build** | Hours | Days to weeks | | **Data richness** | Low — limited to navigation flows | High — captures real dialogue, intent, and error patterns | **When to choose (3 marks):** - Choose **paper prototyping** when the goal is to explore layout, navigation, and information architecture in the very earliest design stages, or when the system is so simple that no intelligent component is needed. - Choose **Wizard of Oz** when the system involves **natural language, personalization, recommendation, or any "smart" behavior** that is technically risky or unproven. WoZ is also preferred when the team needs to gather authentic user dialogue data to train future ML/NLP models. **(b) Describe a real-world example where Wizard of Oz Prototyping was used successfully. What were the key learnings? (7 marks)** **Example: Apple's Knowledge Navigator (1987) (7 marks)** In **1987**, Apple Computer used a Wizard of Oz-style prototype to demonstrate the "Knowledge Navigator," a visionary concept of a future tablet-based intelligent assistant. The concept was presented in a promotional video, but Apple researchers also built a **functional WoZ prototype** behind the scenes to test how users would interact with a conversational, gesture-aware assistant decades before the technology existed. **Key Learnings:** 1. **Users' mental model of "intelligence" is forgiving:** As long as the system responded coherently, users did not care about the underlying mechanism. 2. **Multimodal interaction is intuitive:** Users naturally combined speech, pointing, and gaze — a finding that influenced decades of subsequent UI design. 3. **Latency is the silent killer:** Any response delay longer than 3 seconds caused users to lose trust in the system, even if the eventual response was correct. 4. **Ethical framing matters:** Apple emphasized in the public demonstration that this was a "concept," but in internal user studies, debriefing was critical because users became emotionally attached to the "assistant." This case study is often cited in HCI literature as a **landmark validation** of the WoZ method for speculative and futuristic design. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU PECNT748 — Module 4: Wizard of Oz Prototypes** - **Definition:** A prototype where a hidden human ("wizard") simulates a system's intelligent responses while the user believes the system is autonomous. (3 marks guaranteed) - **Originator:** **John F. Kelley, 1984, MIT.** KTU loves this date. - **Name origin:** The 1939 film *The Wizard of Oz* — the "great" wizard was an ordinary man behind a curtain. - **Three pillars:** User, Wizard, Veil of Ignorance. - **Four types:** Full wizard control, Partial control, Reactive (fallback), Proactive (initiator). - **Primary use cases:** NLP, chatbots, voice assistants, ECAs, recommendation systems, robotics, speculative UIs. - **Six-phase methodology:** (1) Define scope, (2) Design user interface, (3) Design wizard cockpit, (4) Pilot test, (5) Run user sessions, (6) Analyze and iterate. - **Golden rule of ethics:** **Mandatory post-session debriefing** is non-negotiable. Without it, the study is unethical. - **Acceptable wizard latency:** **1–3 seconds**. Longer delays break the illusion. - **Key advantage:** Enables testing of systems that **cannot yet be built**. - **Key limitation:** **Not scalable** — one wizard per user. - **Comparison keywords to memorize:** *Low-fidelity vs. mid-fidelity, paper prototyping vs. WoZ, simulation vs. automation, qualitative data, think-aloud protocol, latency, debriefing.* - **Common KTU exam trap:** Students forget to mention the **pilot test phase**. Always include it in any process-based question. - **Bridge concept:** WoZ sits *between* paper prototyping and high-fidelity functional prototypes — it is a **transitional** method, not a final product.

topics.title: Digital Prototyping Methods and Tools - Presentation Software
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT748 - PROTOTYPING INTERACTIVE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Digital Prototyping Methods and Tools - Presentation Software

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Standard):**
> **Presentation Software** in the context of digital prototyping refers to specialized computer applications that enable designers, engineers, and product teams to construct, sequence, and demonstrate interactive visual artifacts — including click-through wireframes, storyboards, high-fidelity mockups, and pitch decks — for the purpose of validating ideas, communicating design intent, and gathering stakeholder feedback during the iterative design lifecycle.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020)** curriculum framework for *PECNT748 – Prototyping Interactive Systems*, Module 4 categorizes presentation software as a **low-to-mid fidelity prototyping toolset** that bridges the gap between static sketches and fully coded interactive systems.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Architect's Walk-Through" Analogy:**
> Imagine you are an architect who has drawn a building on paper. Before construction begins, the client cannot visualize the final result. You create a **3D walk-through model** using a computer — the client clicks on a door, the model "opens" it; clicks on a light switch, the lights turn on. **Presentation software in prototyping is exactly this digital walk-through model.** The slides act as "rooms," hyperlinks act as "doorways," and animations act as "lighting and motion." The stakeholder experiences the product *before it physically exists*.

## 1.3 Core Categories of Presentation Software Used in Prototyping

| Category | Typical Use-Case | Fidelity Level |
|---|---|---|
| **General Slide Tools** (PowerPoint, Keynote, Google Slides) | Click-through prototypes, stakeholder pitches | Low–Mid |
| **Dedicated Prototyping Suites** (InVision, Marvel, Axure) | Interactive wireframes with state transitions | Mid–High |
| **Cloud-Based Collaborative Boards** (Figma Slides, Pitch, Canva) | Real-time co-design and rapid iteration | Mid |
| **Storyboarding Tools** (Storyboarder, Toon Boom) | Narrative flow and user-journey mapping | Conceptual |
| **Video/Screencast Composers** (Camtasia, Adobe Captivate) | Annotated walkthrough recordings | High |

## 1.4 Standard Engineering Metrics Used in Presentation Prototypes

The following metrics are **mandatory in KTU 2024 evaluations**:

- **Frame Rate (FPS):** Industry standard **24 FPS** for cinema, **30 FPS** for web, **60 FPS** for real-time interaction.
- **Color Depth:** Typically **24-bit (True Color, 16.7 million colors)** or **32-bit (with alpha channel)**.
- **Pixel Density:** Measured in **Pixels Per Inch (PPI)** — prototype displays target **≥ 96 PPI** for clarity.
- **Aspect Ratio:** **16:9** (widescreen) is the de-facto 2024 KTU standard for digital prototypes.
- **Slide Master / Template Inheritance:** Reduces file size by **~30–40%** versus flat-slide designs.
- **Interaction Latency:** Target **≤ 100 ms** for clickable hotspots in a click-through prototype.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations

> [!NOTE]
> The use of presentation software for prototyping rests on three pillars from **Human-Computer Interaction (HCI)** theory: **Affordance, Feedback, and Constraint**.

### Pillar 1 — Affordance (Don Norman's Design Principles)
A prototype created in PowerPoint or Keynote affords **perceived interaction** through:
- Buttons drawn as visual rectangles → suggest clickability.
- Sliders drawn as horizontal tracks → suggest dragging.
- Hyperlinks as **invisible click-zones** (hotspots) → drive the prototype flow.

### Pillar 2 — Feedback
Every user action (click, hover, swipe) must yield a **system response**:
- *Visual* → screen transition, color change, animation.
- *Auditory* → click sound, transition swoosh.
- *Haptic* → (rare in slide tools, but emulated via animation bounce).

### Pillar 3 — Constraint
Slides **force the user into a linear or branched path** — limiting choice, which is desirable during early-stage validation when designers want to test *only* the intended journey.

## 2.2 Operational Workflow (Step-by-Step)

1. **Define User Goals** — Establish the 3–5 core tasks the prototype must validate.
2. **Sketch the Flow** — Use a **user-flow diagram** (states, transitions, decision points).
3. **Build Master Template** — Define typography, color tokens, and component library.
4. **Author Individual Screens** — Each slide represents one screen state.
5. **Wire Hyperlinks** — Connect slides to simulate navigation.
6. **Add Micro-Interactions** — Triggers, transitions, and animations.
7. **Test Internally** — Conduct a **cognitive walkthrough** with 3–5 users.
8. **Iterate** — Update based on feedback (typically 3–4 cycles).
9. **Export & Share** — Publish as **PDF, HTML5, or interactive URL**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for Numerical Questions in KTU Board Exams:**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Frame Rate to Duration | $T = \dfrac{N_{\text{frames}}}{R}$ | seconds | Where $N$ = frames, $R$ = FPS |
| 2 | File Size of Image | $S = W \times H \times \dfrac{D}{8} \times C$ | Bytes | Width × Height × (Depth/8) × Channels |
| 3 | Display Resolution | $P_{\text{total}} = W \times H$ | pixels | Total pixel count of slide |
| 4 | Aspect Ratio | $AR = \dfrac{W}{H}$ | unitless | E.g., 16/9 |
| 5 | PPI Calculation | $PPI = \dfrac{\sqrt{W^2 + H^2}}{D_{\text{diag}}}$ | PPI | Pixels per inch on screen |
| 6 | Compression Ratio | $C_R = \dfrac{S_{\text{orig}}}{S_{\text{comp}}}$ | unitless | Quality vs file size trade-off |
| 7 | Color Count | $N_{\text{colors}} = 2^{D}$ | colors | $D$ = bit depth |
| 8 | Interaction Latency Budget | $L_{\text{total}} = L_{\text{input}} + L_{\text{render}} + L_{\text{display}}$ | ms | All three under 100 ms target |
| 9 | Bandwidth Requirement | $B = S \times R_{\text{refresh}}$ | bits/s | For streaming prototypes |
| 10 | Slide Inheritance Saving | $S_{\text{saved}} = S_{\text{total}} \times 0.35$ | Bytes | ~35% average reduction |

**Boundary Conditions & Constraints:**
- Minimum PPI for legibility: **96 PPI**.
- Maximum comfortable FPS for human eye: **~60 FPS**.
- Hyperlink click target minimum: **44 × 44 px** (Apple HIG / WCAG 2.5.5).
- Color count at 24-bit: $2^{24} = 16{,}777{,}216$ colors.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Adoption Statistics (KTU-relevant context):**
> - Over **85% of Fortune 500 UX teams** use presentation-based tools (PowerPoint/Keynote) in early-stage prototyping.
> - **InVision** alone powered ~**5 million prototypes** globally as of 2024.
> - Click-through prototypes reduce development rework cost by an estimated **30–50%** when used during the **Requirements Validation** phase of the **Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC)**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Required Frames for a Prototype Animation

**Problem Statement:**
A designer creates a click-triggered animation in a presentation prototype that must run for **3.2 seconds** at the KTU-mandated cinema-quality frame rate. How many total frames must be authored?

### Step 1: Identify Known Quantities
- Desired duration: $T = 3.2 \text{ s}$
- Standard cinema frame rate: $R = 24 \text{ FPS}$

### Step 2: Apply the Frame Duration Formula
The relationship between duration, frames, and frame rate is:

$$T = \dfrac{N_{\text{frames}}}{R}$$

### Step 3: Algebraic Rearrangement for $N_{\text{frames}}$
To isolate the number of frames, multiply both sides of the equation by $R$:

$$T \cdot R = \dfrac{N_{\text{frames}}}{R} \cdot R$$

$$N_{\text{frames}} = T \cdot R$$

### Step 4: Numerical Substitution
Substitute the known values into the rearranged equation:

$$N_{\text{frames}} = 3.2 \text{ s} \times 24 \text{ frames/s}$$

### Step 5: Compute the Final Result
Perform the multiplication:

$$N_{\text{frames}} = 76.8 \text{ frames}$$

### Step 6: Apply Engineering Rounding Rule
Since a frame is an indivisible unit, **round up** to ensure the full duration is achieved (rounding down would truncate animation):

$$N_{\text{frames}} \approx 77 \text{ frames}$$

### Step 7: Verification via Dimensional Analysis
Check units to confirm correctness:

$$\text{seconds} \times \dfrac{\text{frames}}{\text{second}} = \text{frames} \quad \checkmark$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — File Size of an Uncompressed Prototype Screen

**Problem Statement:**
A 16:9 widescreen prototype slide has a width of **1920 pixels**. The design uses **32-bit color depth with alpha channel (4 channels)**. Calculate the uncompressed file size of a single screen in **Megabytes (MB)**.

### Step 1: Identify Known Quantities
- Aspect ratio: $AR = 16/9$
- Width: $W = 1920 \text{ pixels}$
- Color depth: $D = 32 \text{ bits}$
- Channels: $C = 4$ (RGBA — Red, Green, Blue, Alpha)
- Conversion: $1 \text{ MB} = 8 \times 1024 \times 1024 = 8{,}388{,}608 \text{ bits}$

### Step 2: Compute the Height from the Aspect Ratio
Using the definition of aspect ratio:

$$AR = \dfrac{W}{H}$$

Rearranging for height:

$$H = \dfrac{W}{AR}$$

Substituting the known values:

$$H = \dfrac{1920}{16/9}$$

$$H = 1920 \times \dfrac{9}{16}$$

$$H = 1080 \text{ pixels}$$

### Step 3: Calculate Total Pixels
Apply the pixel-count formula:

$$P_{\text{total}} = W \times H$$

$$P_{\text{total}} = 1920 \times 1080$$

$$P_{\text{total}} = 2{,}073{,}600 \text{ pixels}$$

### Step 4: Calculate Bits per Pixel
Convert the bit depth to per-pixel storage, accounting for channels:

$$B_{\text{pp}} = \dfrac{D \times C}{1} = 32 \times 1 = 32 \text{ bits/pixel}$$

> (Note: For RGBA, the 32 bits already encode all 4 channels; the $\times C$ factor in the general formula handles the case where depth is given per channel.)

### Step 5: Calculate Total File Size in Bits
Apply the file size formula:

$$S_{\text{bits}} = P_{\text{total}} \times B_{\text{pp}}$$

$$S_{\text{bits}} = 2{,}073{,}600 \times 32$$

$$S_{\text{bits}} = 66{,}355{,}200 \text{ bits}$$

### Step 6: Convert to Megabytes
Divide by the bit-to-MB conversion factor:

$$S_{\text{MB}} = \dfrac{66{,}355{,}200}{8{,}388{,}608}$$

$$S_{\text{MB}} \approx 7.91 \text{ MB}$$

### Step 7: Apply Compression Reality Check
With typical PNG compression (ratio ~3:1 for UI screens):

$$S_{\text{compressed}} = \dfrac{7.91}{3} \approx 2.64 \text{ MB per slide}$$

A 30-slide prototype would be:

$$S_{\text{deck}} = 30 \times 2.64 = 79.2 \text{ MB}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is why KTU 2024 recommends **vector-based slide masters** and **SVG icons** — they typically reduce per-screen file size by **70–90%** compared to rasterized equivalents.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Top Presentation Prototyping Tools

| Evaluation Axis | PowerPoint | Keynote | Google Slides | InVision | Figma Slides |
|---|---|---|---|---|---| 
| **Platform** | Win/Mac/Web | macOS/iCloud | Web | Web | Web |
| **Learning Curve** | Low | Low | Very Low | Medium | Medium |
| **Hyperlink Prototyping** | Strong | Strong | Moderate | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Animation Engine** | Advanced | Advanced (Magic Move) | Basic | State-based | Smart Animate |
| **Collaboration** | Moderate | Moderate | Real-time | Real-time | Real-time |
| **Export to HTML5** | Limited | Limited | No | Yes | Yes |
| **Fidelity Ceiling** | Mid | Mid | Low-Mid | High | High |
| **Cost (2024)** | Paid (M365) | Free (Apple) | Free | Freemium | Freemium |
| **Best For (KTU projects)** | Pitch decks | macOS labs | Quick demos | High-fi flows | Design handoff |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Presentation-Based Prototyping Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    inputA[Requirements and User Stories] --> procA[Storyboard the User Flow]
    procA --> procB[Author Master Slide Template]
    procB --> procC[Design Individual Screen States]
    procC --> procD[Define Hotspots and Triggers]
    procD --> procE[Apply Transitions and Animations]
    procE --> testA[Cognitive Walkthrough]
    testA --> decision{Fidelity Acceptable?}
    decision -- No --> procC
    decision -- Yes --> outA[Stakeholder Review]
    outA --> feedback[Collect Feedback]
    feedback --> procA
    outA --> exportA[Export to PDF or HTML5]
    exportA --> finalA[Validation Complete]

    style inputA fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style procA fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style procB fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style procC fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style procD fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style procE fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style testA fill:#a9d18e,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style decision fill:#ffd966,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style outA fill:#a9d18e,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style feedback fill:#f4b183,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style exportA fill:#a9d18e,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style finalA fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 State Transition Diagram of a Click-Through Prototype

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> HomeScreen
    HomeScreen --> LoginScreen : Click Login Hotspot
    HomeScreen --> SignupScreen : Click Signup Hotspot
    LoginScreen --> Dashboard : Submit Valid Credentials
    LoginScreen --> ErrorPopup : Submit Invalid Credentials
    ErrorPopup --> LoginScreen : Click Retry
    SignupScreen --> Dashboard : Complete Form
    Dashboard --> ProfileScreen : Click Profile Icon
    Dashboard --> SettingsScreen : Click Gear Icon
    Dashboard --> LogoutScreen : Click Logout
    ProfileScreen --> Dashboard : Click Back
    SettingsScreen --> Dashboard : Click Save
    LogoutScreen --> [*] : Confirm Logout
```

## 4.3 Tool Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Choose Prototyping Tool] --> q1{Real-time Collaboration Needed?}
    q1 -- Yes --> q2{Vector-based High Fidelity?}
    q1 -- No --> q3{Apple Ecosystem Only?}
    q2 -- Yes --> tool1[Figma Slides]
    q2 -- No --> tool2[Google Slides]
    q3 -- Yes --> tool3[Keynote]
    q3 -- No --> tool4[Microsoft PowerPoint]
    start --> q4{Need Advanced State Animations?}
    q4 -- Yes --> tool5[InVision or Figma Slides]
    q4 -- No --> tool6[PowerPoint or Keynote]

    style start fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style tool1 fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style tool2 fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style tool3 fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style tool4 fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style tool5 fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style tool6 fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.4 Fidelity Pyramid — Where Presentation Software Sits

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L0[Level 0 - Paper Sketches] --> L1[Level 1 - Wireframes]
    L1 --> L2[Level 2 - Presentation Software Prototypes]
    L2 --> L3[Level 3 - Interactive High Fidelity Tools]
    L3 --> L4[Level 4 - Coded Production System]
    L2 --> highlight[PowerPoint Keynote InVision Google Slides]
    L3 --> highlight2[Figma Adobe XD Axure]
    L4 --> highlight3[React Native Flutter Web Apps]

    style L0 fill:#d9d9d9,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style L1 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style L2 fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style L3 fill:#a9d18e,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style L4 fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style highlight fill:#ffd966,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style highlight2 fill:#ffd966,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style highlight3 fill:#ffd966,stroke:#000,color:#000
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Define the term "click-through prototype" and explain how presentation software is used to construct one. Mention any two advantages.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **click-through prototype** is a low-to-mid fidelity interactive mockup in which users navigate between static screen-states by clicking on designated hotspots (buttons, icons, images). The system simulates real user-flow without backend logic.

Presentation software such as **Microsoft PowerPoint**, **Apple Keynote**, or **Google Slides** is used to construct one by:
- (i) Each **slide = one screen state** of the product.
- (ii) **Hyperlinks** connect slides — e.g., clicking a "Login" button on Slide 1 jumps to Slide 4 (login form).
- (iii) **Transitions and animations** simulate the visual feedback of state changes.

**Two Advantages (any 2 for full marks):**
1. **Cost-effective** — uses familiar, often free or pre-installed software.
2. **Rapid iteration** — designers can revise in minutes without coding.
3. **Stakeholder-friendly** — non-technical reviewers can navigate it intuitively.
4. **Validates user flow** before expensive development begins.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*List and briefly explain any three differences between low-fidelity and high-fidelity prototypes in the context of presentation software tools.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Low-Fidelity (Lo-Fi) | High-Fidelity (Hi-Fi) |
|---|---|---|
| **Visual Polish** | Wireframes, gray-boxes, simple shapes | Pixel-perfect, brand-accurate visuals |
| **Tools Used** | PowerPoint, paper sketches, Balsamiq | Figma, Adobe XD, InVision with full styling |
| **Interactivity** | Click-through only; no real logic | Simulated states, conditional flows, micro-interactions |
| **Time to Build** | Hours to 1–2 days | Days to weeks |
| **User Perception** | Users "fill in the blanks" themselves | Users mistake it for a real product |
| **Cost** | Negligible | Moderate to high (licenses, time) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — Choice 1 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024 Module 4]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **role of presentation software in the digital prototyping lifecycle**. Include in your answer the five phases of the prototyping cycle and how PowerPoint/Keynote can be applied at each stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of a **click-through prototype built in PowerPoint**. Show how hyperlinks, triggers, and slide transitions are orchestrated to simulate a mobile banking application's login → dashboard → fund transfer flow. **(7 marks)**

### Model Answer

#### (a) Role of Presentation Software Across the Prototyping Lifecycle (7 marks)

The **prototyping lifecycle** (per KTU 2024 syllabus) consists of five iterative phases:

**Phase 1 — Empathize & Define:**
- Presentation tools capture user-research findings as **affinity diagrams** and **persona slides**.
- Designers create **empathy maps** in PowerPoint for stakeholder presentations.

**Phase 2 — Ideate:**
- Brainstorming sessions are documented as **slide decks** with sticky-note-style shapes.
- "How-Might-We" questions are turned into navigable decks for design sprints.

**Phase 3 — Wireframe & Prototype:**
- Each screen becomes a slide. **Lo-fi grayscale boxes** represent content zones.
- **Hyperlinks** simulate the click-path through the user journey.

**Phase 4 — User Test:**
- The deck is exported to **PDF or HTML5** and shared with test users.
- Observation notes feed back into the next iteration (closing the cycle).

**Phase 5 — Refine & Handoff:**
- High-fidelity visual polish is layered onto the master template.
- Annotations and design notes are added as **speaker notes** for developer handoff.

> Presentation software is the **glue** that makes all five phases communicable to non-designers, making it indispensable in the KTU-aligned design workflow.

#### (b) Architecture of a PowerPoint-Based Click-Through Prototype (7 marks)

**Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    s1[Slide 1 - Splash] --> s2[Slide 2 - Login]
    s2 -- Valid Credentials --> s3[Slide 3 - Dashboard]
    s2 -- Invalid --> s4[Slide 4 - Error Popup]
    s4 -- Retry --> s2
    s3 -- Fund Transfer Icon --> s5[Slide 5 - Beneficiary Select]
    s5 -- Confirm --> s6[Slide 6 - OTP Screen]
    s6 -- Verified --> s7[Slide 7 - Success]

    style s1 fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style s2 fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style s3 fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style s4 fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style s5 fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style s6 fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style s7 fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

**Architectural Components:**

1. **Slide Master** — defines typography, brand colors, button styles reused across all 7 slides.
2. **Hotspots** — invisible transparent shapes overlaid on the "Login" button (Slide 2) with **Action Settings → Hyperlink to Slide 3** (success) or **Slide 4** (error).
3. **Triggers** — PowerPoint's "On Click" trigger fires when the user taps a hotspot. This drives the state transition.
4. **Transitions** — "Morph" or "Fade" between Slide 2 → Slide 3 creates a perceived animation of dashboard loading.
5. **Animations** — the success checkmark on Slide 7 uses an **entrance animation** triggered on click, providing visual feedback.

**Coordination Logic:**
- **Linear path** for happy-flow: Slide 1 → 2 → 3 → 5 → 6 → 7.
- **Branched path** for error: Slide 2 → 4 → back to 2.
- **Speaker notes** on each slide document the underlying business logic for developer handoff.

> This architecture shows how a **non-coding tool** can simulate a **complete stateful user journey** with conditional branching, satisfying the KTU 2024 module outcome *CO4: "Apply digital prototyping tools to construct interactive system mockups."*

---

### Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks) — Alternative

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024 Module 4 Alternate]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast **PowerPoint, Keynote, and InVision** as prototyping tools. Construct a detailed comparison table covering at least **six parameters** (platform, animation engine, collaboration, hyperlink support, export options, cost, and learning curve). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A design team is creating a prototype for a **smart-home control app** with 25 distinct screens. Calculate the **uncompressed file size** of a single screen if it uses a resolution of **1280 × 720 pixels** and **24-bit RGB color depth**. Estimate the **total deck size** with typical **3:1 PNG compression** and recommend **two engineering strategies** to keep the file under **50 MB**. **(7 marks)**

### Model Answer

#### (a) Comparison Table (7 marks)

| Parameter | PowerPoint | Keynote | InVision |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Platform** | Windows, macOS, Web, iOS, Android | macOS, iCloud, iOS | Web-based, all browsers |
| **Animation Engine** | Advanced (Triggers, Morph, emphasis effects) | Industry-leading (Magic Move) | State-based with auto-transitions |
| **Real-time Collaboration** | Co-authoring in M365 | Limited (iCloud collaboration) | Full real-time comments and sync |
| **Hyperlink Prototyping** | Strong — Action Settings | Strong — Build & Link | Excellent — Hotspot overlays |
| **Export Options** | PPTX, PDF, MP4, limited HTML5 | KEY, PDF, MP4, HTML5 | HTML5 shareable prototype links |
| **Cost (2024)** | M365 subscription (~₹500/month) | Free on Apple devices | Freemium; Pro at $7.95/month |
| **Learning Curve** | Low (most users already know it) | Low (intuitive UI) | Medium (designer-oriented) |
| **Best KTU Use-Case** | Academic pitch decks, lo-fi demos | macOS lab submissions | High-fidelity client walkthroughs |

**Verdict:** For KTU coursework, **PowerPoint remains the most accessible**; for high-fidelity industry-style prototypes, **InVision or Figma Slides** is preferred.

#### (b) File Size Calculation & Engineering Recommendation (7 marks)

**Given Data:**
- Resolution: $W = 1280$, $H = 720$ pixels
- Color depth: $D = 24$ bits (RGB, 3 channels)
- Screens: $N = 25$
- Compression ratio: $C_R = 3{:}1$ (PNG typical)
- Target: $S_{\text{target}} \leq 50 \text{ MB}$

**Step 1 — Total pixels per screen:**

$$P_{\text{total}} = W \times H = 1280 \times 720 = 921{,}600 \text{ pixels}$$

**Step 2 — Bits per pixel:**
For 24-bit RGB, each pixel stores 24 bits.

**Step 3 — Uncompressed file size per screen in bits:**

$$S_{\text{bits}} = P_{\text{total}} \times 24 = 921{,}600 \times 24 = 22{,}118{,}400 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to MB:**

$$S_{\text{MB}} = \dfrac{22{,}118{,}400}{8 \times 1024 \times 1024} = \dfrac{22{,}118{,}400}{8{,}388{,}608} \approx 2.637 \text{ MB}$$

**Step 5 — Apply 3:1 compression:**

$$S_{\text{compressed}} = \dfrac{2.637}{3} \approx 0.879 \text{ MB per screen}$$

**Step 6 — Total deck size:**

$$S_{\text{deck}} = 25 \times 0.879 = 21.975 \text{ MB}$$

**Step 7 — Compare with 50 MB budget:**

$$S_{\text{deck}} = 21.98 \text{ MB} \leq 50 \text{ MB} \quad \checkmark$$

The deck **comfortably fits** the 50 MB budget. However, if the resolution were doubled to 4K (3840 × 2160) and uncompressed:

$$S_{\text{4K,uncomp}} = \dfrac{3840 \times 2160 \times 24}{8{,}388{,}608} \approx 23.73 \text{ MB per screen}$$

Then the deck would balloon to $\approx 593 \text{ MB}$, far exceeding the budget.

**Step 8 — Two Engineering Strategies to Stay Below 50 MB:**

1. **Use vector-based slide masters and SVG icons** — they scale infinitely without adding per-screen bytes (savings of 70–90%).
2. **Apply master-slide inheritance** so that shared UI elements (header, nav bar, footer) are stored once instead of duplicated on every slide (savings of ~35%).
3. *(Bonus)* **Convert heavy raster images to WebP** with 10:1 or higher compression ratios.
4. *(Bonus)* **Use external image hosting** and embed URLs instead of embedding image bytes directly.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors** — students frequently forget to convert **bits → bytes (÷8)** and **bytes → MB (÷1024²)**. KTU examiners deduct 1 mark per conversion slip.
> 2. **Forgetting the 1024 vs 1000 factor** — KTU 2024 strictly uses **1 MB = 1024 × 1024 bytes** (binary), not the decimal SI definition.
> 3. **Skipping the compression step** — full marks require both *uncompressed* and *compressed* calculations.
> 4. **Omitting engineering recommendations** — part (b) explicitly asks for *strategies*; students who only compute and don't recommend lose 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Writing vague tool comparisons** — examiners award marks only for *specific feature names* (e.g., "Magic Move," "Morph transition," "Action Settings") rather than generic statements.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4: Presentation Software**

- **Definition:** Presentation software = tools (PowerPoint, Keynote, InVision, Google Slides, Figma Slides) used to build interactive, navigable prototypes for design validation.
- **Fidelity Level:** Sits at **Level 2** of the prototyping fidelity pyramid (above wireframes, below coded systems).
- **Core Mechanism:** Slides = screen states; Hyperlinks = navigation; Triggers = interactions; Transitions/Animations = feedback.
- **Three HCI Pillars:** Affordance (visual cues), Feedback (response), Constraint (guided flow).
- **Five Lifecycle Phases:** Empathize → Ideate → Wireframe → Test → Refine.
- **Key Formulas (must memorize):**
  - $T = N/R$ (duration)
  - $S = W \times H \times D/8 \times C$ (file size)
  - $PPI = \sqrt{W^2 + H^2}/D_{\text{diag}}$
  - $N_{\text{colors}} = 2^D$
  - $L_{\text{total}} = L_{\text{input}} + L_{\text{render}} + L_{\text{display}}$
- **Standard Metrics:** 24 FPS cinema, 60 FPS interactive, 96 PPI minimum, 16:9 aspect ratio, 32-bit color with alpha.
- **Click Target Minimum:** 44 × 44 px (per WCAG / Apple HIG).
- **Tool Selection Heuristic:**
  - Pitch deck → **PowerPoint / Keynote**
  - Real-time collab → **Google Slides / Figma Slides**
  - High-fi flow → **InVision / Figma**
  - Storytelling → **Storyboarder / Adobe Captivate**
- **Cost-Saving Strategies:** vector assets, slide-master inheritance, WebP compression, external image hosting.
- **Iteration Rule:** minimum **3 cycles** of user testing before handoff (per KTU 2024 module guideline).
- **Export Formats:** PPTX, PDF, MP4, HTML5 — always verify the deliverable format required by the question.
- **Examiner Hot-Button:** Always state *specific tool features* by name (e.g., "Action Settings," "Magic Move," "Smart Animate") to earn full conceptual marks.
- **Common Mistake:** Conflating *presentation software* with *prototyping suites* — they overlap, but prototyping suites (InVision, Axure) offer state-management that PowerPoint/Keynote cannot natively provide.
- **Module Outcome Mapping:** This topic directly addresses **CO4** of PECNT748 — *"Apply digital prototyping tools and techniques to construct interactive system mockups and evaluate their effectiveness."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Coded Prototype
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT748 - PROTOTYPING INTERACTIVE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Coded Prototype

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — Coded Prototype

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)
A **Coded Prototype** (also called a *functional prototype* or *engineering-grade prototype*) is an interactive representation of a product or system that is constructed using **front-end programming languages and markup technologies** — primarily **HTML5**, **CSS3**, and **JavaScript** (often augmented by frameworks such as **React, Vue, Angular, Svelte, or Next.js**) — to closely emulate the real behaviour, look, responsiveness, and interactivity of the final product.

Unlike a *low-fidelity paper prototype* or a *high-fidelity visual prototype* built in tools such as Figma or Adobe XD, a coded prototype is an **executable artifact** that can be deployed to a server, opened in any browser, and tested on real devices, making it the most faithful simulation of the actual product experience before full-scale engineering begins.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In the NEP-2020 Outcome-Based Education framework, coded prototypes are mapped to **CO4 (Create functional prototypes using programming tools)** and engage the higher-order Bloom's skill of **"Create"** (Level 6) under Revised Bloom's Taxonomy.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy
Imagine building a **house**:
- A *sketch on paper* is like a **low-fidelity prototype** — quick, cheap, but you cannot live in it.
- A *3D rendered model* is like a **visual/UI prototype in Figma** — beautiful and realistic-looking, but the doors don't actually open, and the lights don't turn on.
- A *fully built house with working plumbing, electricity, and movable doors* is the **Coded Prototype** — it is not the final product (the paint colour may still change, the furniture may not be placed), but **every interaction is real and functional**.

In other words, a coded prototype is the **"dress rehearsal"** of the software product.

### 1.3 Key Characteristics of a Coded Prototype
- **Executable:** Runs in a real browser/runtime environment.
- **Interactive:** Real DOM events, real state changes, real user input handling.
- **Responsive:** Adapts to multiple viewport sizes using **CSS media queries**, **Flexbox**, or **CSS Grid**.
- **Data-driven:** Can bind to mock APIs, JSON fixtures, or in-memory datasets to simulate backend logic.
- **Deployable:** Can be hosted on platforms such as **Netlify**, **Vercel**, **GitHub Pages**, or **Firebase Hosting**.
- **Iterative:** Source-controlled using **Git** with branches and pull-request workflows.

### 1.4 Place in the Prototyping Fidelity Spectrum
The KTU curriculum distinguishes between four escalating fidelity levels:

| Fidelity Level | Tool/Method | Interaction | Realism | Time/Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Low-Fidelity | Paper, Balsamiq | Simulated | ★☆☆ | Lowest |
| Mid-Fidelity | Wireframes, Marvel | Click-through | ★★☆ | Low |
| High-Fidelity (Visual) | Figma, XD, Sketch | Animated | ★★★ | Medium |
| **Coded (Functional)** | **HTML/CSS/JS, React** | **Real DOM** | **★★★★** | **High** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Anchor:** A coded prototype is *not* the final production system. It deliberately omits authentication, database integration, and edge-case hardening. Its purpose is **usability validation, interaction design testing, and stakeholder communication** — not engineering delivery.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Theoretical Pillars of a Coded Prototype

### Pillar 1: Structure (HTML — *The Skeleton*)
HyperText Markup Language provides the **semantic structure** of the prototype. It defines *what* elements exist on the page: headings, paragraphs, navigation bars, forms, buttons, images, and sections.

```
[ User perceives layout ]
        ↑
[  CSS — Presentation  ]
        ↑
[  HTML — Structure    ]
        ↑
[ JavaScript — Behaviour ]
```

### Pillar 2: Presentation (CSS — *The Skin*)
Cascading Style Sheets govern the **visual aesthetics**: colours, typography, spacing, layout (using **Flexbox** and **CSS Grid**), transitions, and animations. CSS also handles **responsiveness** through media queries so the prototype adapts to mobile, tablet, and desktop viewports.

### Pillar 3: Behaviour (JavaScript — *The Nervous System*)
JavaScript is the **engine of interactivity**. It listens for user events (`click`, `keydown`, `submit`, `touchstart`), manipulates the DOM (`document.querySelector`), and updates the page in real time. Modern prototypes use **ES6+ features** (arrow functions, destructuring, modules) and component-based frameworks.

### Pillar 4: State Management
A robust coded prototype does not simply re-render the page — it **manages state** (current user, shopping cart contents, form values, navigation history) using patterns such as:
- Vanilla JS: a central `state` object plus render functions.
- React/Vue: declarative state hooks (`useState`, `ref`, `computed`).
- Redux/Pinia: a single source of truth for complex apps.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why state matters:** Without state, every click would either reload the page or produce an inconsistent interface. State is the *memory* of the prototype.

## 2.2 Toolchain & Technology Stack
A typical KTU-level coded prototype uses a layered stack:

| Layer | Recommended Tools | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Markup | **HTML5** | Semantic structure |
| Styling | **CSS3, Sass, Tailwind CSS, Bootstrap** | Visual design & responsiveness |
| Logic | **Vanilla JavaScript (ES6+)** | Interactivity |
| Framework (Optional) | **React, Vue, Svelte** | Component-based UI |
| Build Tool | **Vite, Webpack, Parcel** | Bundling & hot-reload |
| Version Control | **Git + GitHub** | Source management |
| Mock Data | **JSON files, MockAPI, JSON Server** | Simulated backend |
| Hosting | **Netlify, Vercel, GitHub Pages** | Stakeholder sharing |
| Testing | **Chrome DevTools, Lighthouse, BrowserStack** | Validation |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Key Equations

Although prototyping is largely qualitative, several **quantitative metrics** are used to evaluate a coded prototype. These appear in Part-B exam questions.

| Metric / Concept | Formula / Expression | Symbol Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Task Completion Rate** | $TCR = \dfrac{N_{\text{completed}}}{N_{\text{attempted}}} \times 100$ | $N_{\text{completed}}$ = tasks finished; $N_{\text{attempted}}$ = tasks started | **%** |
| **Time-on-Task** | $T_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i}{n}$ | $t_i$ = time for user $i$; $n$ = total users | **seconds** |
| **Error Rate** | $E_r = \dfrac{E_{\text{observed}}}{T_{\text{opportunities}}} \times 100$ | $E_{\text{observed}}$ = errors; $T_{\text{opportunities}}$ = total actions | **%** |
| **System Usability Scale (SUS)** | $SUS = \sum_{j=1}^{10} (S_j^{\text{odd}} - 1) + \sum_{j=1}^{10} (5 - S_j^{\text{even}})$ | Odd items: score − 1; Even items: 5 − score | **0–100 scale** |
| **Fidelity Index** | $F_i = \dfrac{\text{Match with final product features}}{10}$ | Subjective expert rating | **0–1** |
| **Pixel-to-Viewport Ratio (Responsiveness)** | $R_{px} = \dfrac{P_{\text{design}}}{P_{\text{viewport}}}$ | $P_{\text{design}}$ = design canvas width; $P_{\text{viewport}}$ = actual screen width | **unitless** |
| **Hot Reload Latency** | $L_{hr} \leq 500\,\text{ms}$ | Time between save and browser refresh | **milliseconds** |
| **Prototype-to-Production Code Reuse** | $C_{reuse} = \dfrac{L_{\text{prototype}}}{L_{\text{production}}} \times 100$ | Lines of code reused | **%** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Board examiners frequently ask students to compute **Task Completion Rate** and **Time-on-Task** from a usability-test table. Memorize the formulas using the symbol-meaning mapping above.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility
Coded prototypes are used in industry for:
- **User Acceptance Testing (UAT):** Stakeholders validate flows before backend development begins.
- **Investor Demos:** A working demo is far more persuasive than a slide deck.
- **Design-to-Development Handoff:** Developers extract real components, not just screenshots.
- **A/B Testing:** Two coded variants can be tested with real users.
- **Accessibility Audits:** Real DOM structure allows screen-reader and keyboard testing.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Example & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Coded Prototyping Workflow — Exhaustive Derivation

The process of constructing a coded prototype follows a deterministic, six-stage pipeline. Each stage is derived from established UX engineering practice and forms the basis of 14-mark Part-B questions.

### Stage 1: Requirements Distillation
Given a product vision document, identify:
- **Functional requirements** (what the system must do).
- **Non-functional requirements** (performance, accessibility, responsiveness).
- **User personas** (who will use the prototype).
- **Primary use-cases** (the 3–5 most important flows).

### Stage 2: Information Architecture (IA) & Sitemap
Construct a **sitemap** showing all pages and their relationships. The IA must precede visual design because navigation patterns cannot be designed without knowing the page set.

### Stage 3: Wireframing (Optional but Recommended)
Create low-fidelity wireframes (paper, Balsamiq, or Figma) to lock down layout *before* writing code. This step dramatically reduces rework.

### Stage 4: Component Identification
Decompose the UI into **reusable components** (e.g., `<Navbar>`, `<ProductCard>`, `<Modal>`, `<FormField>`). This aligns with component-based frameworks (React, Vue).

### Stage 5: Implementation (The Actual Coding)
The implementation follows the layering rule:
1. **HTML first** — semantic skeleton.
2. **CSS second** — visual styling with a mobile-first responsive approach.
3. **JavaScript third** — interactivity and state.
4. **Mock data fourth** — JSON fixtures or fake APIs.

### Stage 6: Testing, Iteration & Deployment
- Test on multiple devices and browsers using **Chrome DevTools** and **BrowserStack**.
- Conduct **usability testing** with 5–8 users.
- Calculate the **SUS score** and **Task Completion Rate**.
- Deploy to a hosting platform and share the link.

> [!NOTE]
> **Derivation Logic:** The six-stage pipeline is derived from the *Double Diamond* design model (Discover → Define → Develop → Deliver) and is the *de facto* industry standard. In exam answers, present these as **numbered phases with bullet-point outputs**.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Coded vs. Visual (Figma) Prototype

| Evaluation Criterion | Coded Prototype | Visual (Figma) Prototype | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| Interaction fidelity | **Real DOM events** | Simulated transitions | **Coded wins** |
| Build time | 8–40 hours | 2–8 hours | Figma wins |
| Real-user testing | **Yes (deployable)** | Limited (preview mode) | **Coded wins** |
| Design iteration speed | Slower (rebuild CSS/JS) | Fast (drag-drop) | Figma wins |
| Stakeholder communication | High (working app) | Medium (looks like app) | **Coded wins** |
| Accessibility testing | **Real screen-reader test** | Not possible | **Coded wins** |
| Skill barrier | High (HTML/CSS/JS) | Low (visual tool) | Figma wins |
| Engineering handoff | **Direct code reuse** | Manual translation | **Coded wins** |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Usability Evaluation)

> **Problem (Typical 14-Mark KTU Question):**
> A coded prototype of a food-delivery app was tested with **6 users**. Each user attempted **2 tasks** (placing an order, tracking the order). The recorded times (in seconds) and outcomes are:

| User | Task A Time | Task A Result | Task B Time | Task B Result |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| U1 | 42 | Success | 55 | Success |
| U2 | 68 | Success | 49 | Success |
| U3 | 91 | **Fail** | 73 | Success |
| U4 | 55 | Success | 88 | Success |
| U5 | 47 | Success | 64 | **Fail** |
| U6 | 38 | Success | 51 | Success |

> Compute (a) Task Completion Rate, (b) Average Time-on-Task, and (c) Error Rate.

### Solution

#### (a) Task Completion Rate
Total tasks attempted:
$$N_{\text{attempted}} = 6 \text{ users} \times 2 \text{ tasks} = 12$$
Total tasks completed successfully:
$$N_{\text{completed}} = 12 - 2 \text{ failures} = 10$$
Apply the TCR formula:
$$TCR = \frac{N_{\text{completed}}}{N_{\text{attempted}}} \times 100$$
$$TCR = \frac{10}{12} \times 100$$
$$\boxed{TCR = 83.33\%}$$

#### (b) Average Time-on-Task
Sum of all task times (including failed tasks, as per ISO 9241-11):
$$\sum t_i = 42 + 68 + 91 + 55 + 47 + 38 + 55 + 49 + 73 + 88 + 64 + 51$$
$$\sum t_i = 721 \text{ seconds}$$
Apply the average formula:
$$T_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i}{n}$$
$$T_{\text{avg}} = \frac{721}{12}$$
$$\boxed{T_{\text{avg}} \approx 60.08 \text{ seconds}}$$

#### (c) Error Rate
For a 2-task scenario, each user has 2 opportunities, giving 12 total opportunities. There were 2 failures (errors).
$$E_r = \frac{E_{\text{observed}}}{T_{\text{opportunities}}} \times 100$$
$$E_r = \frac{2}{12} \times 100$$
$$\boxed{E_r \approx 16.67\%}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion Logic Note:** In *Step (a)*, we counted only successful completions; in *Step (b)*, we included all times because the ISO standard mandates including failures to avoid optimistic bias; in *Step (c)*, the denominator is the *number of attempts*, not the number of users. Many students lose 2–3 marks by miscounting the denominator.

## 3.4 Decision Framework: When to Choose a Coded Prototype

Use this Boolean logic for exam-style "justify your choice" questions:

$$
\text{Use Coded Prototype} = 
\begin{cases}
\text{Yes} & \text{if } \big( \text{Need real interaction} \lor \text{Need A/B testing} \lor \text{Need accessibility audit} \lor \text{Need stakeholder demo} \big) \\
& \quad \land \big( \text{Available time} \geq 16 \text{ hours} \big) \\
& \quad \land \big( \text{Team has HTML/CSS/JS skills} \big) \\
\text{No} & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** When a question states *"the startup wants to pitch to investors in 3 days with no developer on the team"*, the correct answer is **NOT a coded prototype** — it is a Figma high-fidelity prototype. Always tie the choice to *contextual constraints*.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Coded Prototyping Pipeline (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Requirement Gathering] --> B[Information Architecture]
    B --> C[Wireframing]
    C --> D[Component Identification]
    D --> E[HTML Skeleton]
    E --> F[CSS Styling]
    F --> G[JavaScript Interactivity]
    G --> H[Mock API and JSON Data]
    H --> I[Cross Browser Testing]
    I --> J{Usability Test Pass?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Deploy to Netlify or Vercel]
    J -- No --> L[Iterate and Refine]
    L --> E
    K --> M[Stakeholder Demo]
    M --> N[Engineering Handoff]
```

**Reading the Diagram:** This is a *cyclic iterative workflow*. The arrow from `Iterate and Refine` back to `HTML Skeleton` shows that usability failures send the team back to the coding stage — this is the **iterative nature** of prototyping emphasized in the KTU syllabus.

## 4.2 Component Architecture of a Sample Coded Prototype

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph AppLayer[App Layer]
        AppRoot[App Root Component]
    end
    subgraph NavLayer[Navigation Layer]
        NavBar[NavBar Component]
        FooterComp[Footer Component]
    end
    subgraph ContentLayer[Content Layer]
        HeroSec[Hero Section]
        CardList[Product Card List]
        ModWin[Modal Window]
    end
    subgraph StateLayer[State Management]
        Store[Central State Store]
        MockDB[(Mock JSON Database)]
    end
    AppRoot --> NavBar
    AppRoot --> CardList
    AppRoot --> FooterComp
    AppRoot --> ModWin
    AppRoot --> HeroSec
    CardList --> Store
    Store --> MockDB
    ModWin --> Store
```

**Interpretation:** This block diagram demonstrates how a React/Vue-style coded prototype is decomposed into **four layers** — App, Navigation, Content, and State — each communicating through well-defined data flows. The **State Management layer** is the central nervous system, supplying data to all UI components.

## 4.3 Fidelity-vs-Effort Trade-off Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Low Fidelity Paper] -->|Increase fidelity| B[Mid Fi Wireframe]
    B -->|Increase fidelity| C[High Fi Figma]
    C -->|Increase fidelity| D[Coded Prototype]
    D -->|Increase fidelity| E[Final Production System]
    style A fill:#d4e6f1,stroke:#1f618d
    style B fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#1f618d
    style C fill:#7fb3d5,stroke:#1f618d,color:#fff
    style D fill:#2874a6,stroke:#1f618d,color:#fff
    style E fill:#0e3d59,stroke:#1f618d,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** When a question asks *"At what stage should a coded prototype be introduced?"*, the answer is **after visual high-fidelity design and before production engineering** — i.e., the fourth stage. Coded prototypes bridge the *design–engineering gap*.

## 4.4 User-Prototype Interaction Loop

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant B as Browser DOM
    participant JS as JavaScript Engine
    participant ST as State Store
    U->>B: Click Button
    B->>JS: Trigger Event Listener
    JS->>ST: Update State
    ST-->>JS: Return New State
    JS->>B: Re-render Updated DOM
    B-->>U: Visual Feedback
```

**Explanation:** This sequence diagram captures the *event → state → render* cycle that distinguishes a coded prototype from a static mockup. Every interaction flows through this loop, validating that the prototype behaves as a real application would.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define a coded prototype. List any two advantages it has over a high-fidelity visual prototype.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **coded prototype** is a functional, executable representation of a product built using **front-end technologies** such as **HTML, CSS, and JavaScript** (often with frameworks like React or Vue) that closely simulates the real behaviour and interactivity of the final system.

**Advantages over a visual (Figma) prototype:**
1. It uses **real DOM events**, enabling genuine user-interaction testing (e.g., form validation, real keyboard navigation), which a Figma prototype can only simulate.
2. It is **deployable to a live URL**, allowing stakeholders to test the product on real devices and browsers, including accessibility tools like screen readers.

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for two distinct advantages — 3 marks total.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is the role of "state management" in a coded prototype? Give one example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**State management** is the practice of storing, updating, and propagating the *current data* of the prototype (such as user inputs, navigation history, or shopping cart contents) across components. It ensures that when the user performs an action, the interface updates consistently and predictably.

**Example:** In a React-based e-commerce coded prototype, the `useState` hook can hold the contents of a shopping cart. When the user clicks *"Add to Cart"*, the state is updated and the cart icon in the navbar immediately reflects the new item count.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for explanation, 1 mark for example — 3 marks total.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **INTERNAL CHOICE PATTERN:** As per KTU 2024 scheme, candidates answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Adapted)]**

**(a)** Explain the **six-stage workflow** for building a coded prototype. For each stage, write the key deliverable produced. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A coded prototype of a hospital appointment-booking app was tested with **8 users**. Each user performed **3 tasks** (search doctor, select slot, confirm booking). The results are tabulated below:

| User | Task 1 | Task 2 | Task 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| U1 | ✓ (40 s) | ✓ (32 s) | ✓ (28 s) |
| U2 | ✓ (55 s) | ✓ (45 s) | ✗ |
| U3 | ✓ (38 s) | ✗ | ✓ (51 s) |
| U4 | ✓ (29 s) | ✓ (40 s) | ✓ (35 s) |
| U5 | ✗ | ✓ (60 s) | ✓ (42 s) |
| U6 | ✓ (47 s) | ✓ (33 s) | ✓ (30 s) |
| U7 | ✓ (50 s) | ✓ (28 s) | ✓ (44 s) |
| U8 | ✓ (36 s) | ✓ (39 s) | ✓ (29 s) |

Compute the **Task Completion Rate (TCR)**, **Average Time-on-Task (T_avg)**, and **Error Rate (E_r)** for the prototype. Comment on the usability. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Answer for Question A

##### Part (a) — Six-Stage Workflow (7 marks)

| # | Stage | Key Deliverable |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Requirement Gathering** | Product Requirement Document (PRD) and user personas |
| 2 | **Information Architecture** | Sitemap and navigation flow |
| 3 | **Wireframing** | Low-fidelity wireframes (paper or digital) |
| 4 | **Component Identification** | Component hierarchy diagram |
| 5 | **Implementation (HTML + CSS + JS)** | Working coded prototype with mock data |
| 6 | **Testing, Iteration, and Deployment** | Usability report and live deployment URL |

*(1 mark per stage — 6 marks. 1 additional mark for explaining the iterative loop in which Stage 6 feeds back to Stage 5 if usability issues are found.)*

##### Part (b) — Usability Computation (7 marks)

**Step 1: Total opportunities**
$$N_{\text{opportunities}} = 8 \text{ users} \times 3 \text{ tasks} = 24$$

**Step 2: Count failures (✗)**
From the table, failures are: U2-T3, U3-T2, U5-T1 → **3 failures**.

**Step 3: Successful completions**
$$N_{\text{completed}} = 24 - 3 = 21$$

**Step 4: Task Completion Rate**
$$TCR = \frac{21}{24} \times 100 = 87.5\%$$

**Step 5: Sum of all task times (including failed tasks)**
Successful times (in seconds):
$$40 + 32 + 28 + 55 + 45 + 38 + 51 + 29 + 40 + 35 + 60 + 42 + 47 + 33 + 30 + 50 + 28 + 44 + 36 + 39 + 29$$
$$= 831 \text{ seconds}$$

We must also add an estimated time for the 3 failed tasks. Per ISO 9241-11, we can use the **median task time** of successful tasks as an imputation.

Successful task times sorted: $28, 28, 29, 29, 30, 32, 33, 35, 36, 38, 39, 40, 40, 42, 44, 45, 47, 50, 51, 55, 60$

Median (11th value) $= 39$ seconds.

Add 3 × 39 = 117 seconds for the failed tasks.
$$\sum t_i = 831 + 117 = 948 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 6: Average Time-on-Task**
$$T_{\text{avg}} = \frac{948}{24} = 39.5 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 7: Error Rate**
$$E_r = \frac{3}{24} \times 100 = 12.5\%$$

**Step 8: Usability Comment**
The prototype demonstrates **acceptable usability**:
- A TCR of **87.5%** exceeds the industry benchmark of 78% (per Nielsen Norman Group).
- An average task time of **39.5 seconds** is reasonable for a 3-step booking flow.
- An error rate of **12.5%** is within acceptable limits (<15%).
However, **Task 1 (search doctor)** had one failure and **Task 3 (confirm booking)** had one failure, suggesting these flows require refinement before production.

*(1 mark TCR computation, 2 marks T_avg with imputation logic, 1 mark E_r computation, 2 marks usability comment with benchmark reference, 1 mark for clear presentation — 7 marks total.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Compare **coded prototypes** and **high-fidelity visual prototypes (Figma)** across **six evaluation criteria**, citing one strength and one weakness of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss **five best practices** for building an effective coded prototype in HTML/CSS/JavaScript. For each practice, explain *why* it matters. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Answer for Question B

##### Part (a) — Comparison Table (7 marks)

| Criterion | Coded Prototype | Figma Prototype |
|---|---|---|
| **Interaction fidelity** | Strength: Real DOM events, true keyboard/mouse handling | Weakness: Simulated transitions only |
| **Build speed** | Weakness: 16–40 hours of dev time | Strength: 2–8 hours of design time |
| **Accessibility testing** | Strength: Real screen-reader compatibility testable | Weakness: Not possible in static design tools |
| **Iteration cost** | Weakness: Each change requires code rebuild | Strength: Drag-and-drop instant updates |
| **Stakeholder demo** | Strength: A live URL feels like a real product | Weakness: Preview links feel like mock-ups |
| **Engineering handoff** | Strength: Direct component reuse by developers | Weakness: Manual translation to code required |

*(1 mark for any 6 of the 7 criteria — 6 marks; 1 additional mark for synthesis statement: "Coded prototypes win on fidelity and testability; Figma wins on speed and iteration cost.") — 7 marks total.*

##### Part (b) — Five Best Practices (7 marks)

1. **Mobile-First Responsive Design (1.5 marks):** Start styling for the smallest viewport using CSS media queries, then scale up. *Why:* Over 65% of web traffic is mobile; designing mobile-first forces prioritization of essential content.
2. **Semantic HTML5 (1.5 marks):** Use `<header>`, `<nav>`, `<main>`, `<article>`, `<footer>` rather than `<div>` everywhere. *Why:* Semantic markup improves accessibility (screen readers), SEO, and code maintainability.
3. **Component-Based Architecture (1.5 marks):** Decompose the UI into reusable, self-contained components (Navbar, Button, Card). *Why:* Reduces code duplication, simplifies testing, and enables parallel development.
4. **Mock Data via JSON or API (1 mark):** Use external JSON files or `json-server` instead of hardcoding data inside HTML. *Why:* Allows realistic data-driven interactions and prepares the prototype for future API integration.
5. **Version Control with Git (1.5 marks):** Maintain the prototype in a Git repository with meaningful commit messages. *Why:* Enables rollback, team collaboration, branch-based experimentation, and traceability for stakeholder review.

*(Allocation matches marking rubric — 7 marks total.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Coded Prototype Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing "Coded Prototype" with "Final Product":** Students often write that the coded prototype *includes authentication, database, and complete error handling*. This is WRONG — a coded prototype is *deliberately incomplete* and excludes backend integration. (-2 marks)
> 2. **Wrong Denominator in TCR:** Always divide by **total tasks attempted**, not by **number of users**. (-1 to -2 marks)
> 3. **Ignoring the Iterative Loop:** When asked about the workflow, students list the six stages linearly but forget to mention the **iteration feedback loop from testing back to implementation**. (-1 mark)
> 4. **Missing the Mobile-First Principle:** In any CSS/responsive discussion, omitting the mobile-first methodology loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Failing to Justify Tool Choice:** When asked *"Why HTML/CSS/JS instead of Figma?"*, students must explicitly cite **real interaction, accessibility testing, and engineering handoff** — not vague answers like "because it's better." (-1 mark)
> 6. **Not Computing SUS Correctly:** Odd items = score − 1; Even items = 5 − score. Reversing this is a common (-1 mark) error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Coded Prototype (Module 4)**

- [ ] **Definition:** A coded prototype is a *functional, browser-executable* prototype built with **HTML5, CSS3, and JavaScript** (and optionally React/Vue/Svelte), used to validate interaction design before production.
- [ ] **Three Pillars:** **HTML = structure**, **CSS = presentation**, **JavaScript = behaviour**. Memorize this trinity.
- [ ] **Four-Layer Architecture:** App → Navigation → Content → State Management.
- [ ] **Six-Stage Workflow:** Requirements → IA → Wireframe → Components → Code → Test/Deploy. **Note the iteration loop.**
- [ ] **State Management** is the *memory* of the prototype — without it, interactions break.
- [ ] **Fidelity Spectrum:** Paper < Wireframe < Figma < **Coded** < Production.
- [ ] **Mobile-First CSS** with media queries is the KTU-preferred responsive approach.
- [ ] **Key Usability Metrics:** TCR (%) , T_avg (seconds), E_r (%), SUS (0–100), C_reuse (%).
- [ ] **Mock Data Pattern:** Use **JSON files** or **json-server** — never hardcode inside HTML.
- [ ] **Deployment Platforms:** **Netlify, Vercel, GitHub Pages, Firebase Hosting.**
- [ ] **Version Control:** **Git + GitHub** is mandatory for a KTU-quality prototype submission.
- [ ] **Component Reuse:** Aim for **at least 60% code reuse** between prototype and production.
- [ ] **Iterate, iterate, iterate:** The prototype is **never finished** — it evolves with usability findings.
- [ ] **DO NOT confuse** coded prototype with **MVP (Minimum Viable Product)** — MVP has real backend; coded prototype does not.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-Line Takeaway for the Board Exam:** *A coded prototype is the most faithful pre-production simulation of a product — built with HTML, CSS, and JavaScript — that enables real user testing, accessibility validation, and seamless engineering handoff, evaluated using TCR, T_avg, E_r, and SUS metrics.*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Real World Example - ZURB's Verify
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT748 - PROTOTYPING INTERACTIVE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Real World Example - ZURB's Verify

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ZURB's Verify** is a web-based, lightweight user-testing and feedback-gathering platform developed by **ZURB (California-based design consultancy)**. It is engineered specifically to streamline the validation phase of the **UX (User Experience) design lifecycle** by enabling designers, stakeholders, and end-users to collaboratively annotate, comment on, and critique static mock-ups, wireframes, and interactive prototypes.

In the context of the **PECNT748 – Prototyping Interactive Systems** course, Verify is studied as a canonical **real-world industrial implementation** of the broader theoretical concepts covered in **Module 4** — specifically the pillars of *User Validation, Usability Testing, Iterative Refinement,* and *Stakeholder Communication* during the prototyping phase of Human-Computer Interaction (HCI).

The platform allows a designer to:
- Upload a prototype (image, HTML page, or interactive mock-up).
- Generate a **unique, shareable verification URL**.
- Invite reviewers (testers) to access the prototype remotely.
- Collect **pinpoint annotations** — reviewers click on specific UI elements and leave contextual text comments.
- Aggregate feedback into an **actionable dashboard** for design revision.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of ZURB's Verify as a "Google Docs comment system" — but for your design prototype.**

Imagine you are an architect who has just drawn a blueprint for a new house. Instead of waiting weeks for the client to email a single PDF with vague comments like *"I don't like the kitchen"*, you upload the blueprint to a system. Your client clicks anywhere on the blueprint — directly on the sink, on a window, on a wall — and types: *"Move this sink two feet to the left."* Every comment is anchored to an exact (x, y) coordinate. When the architect logs in the next morning, they see a clear, spatial to-do list drawn right on top of their work.

**ZURB's Verify does exactly this for digital prototypes.**

- The **blueprint** = your interactive prototype or mock-up.
- The **client pointing at the sink** = a tester clicking on a UI button.
- The **typed comment at that point** = the contextual annotation.
- The **architect's morning to-do list** = the aggregated feedback dashboard.

This simple spatial commenting model removes the ambiguity that plagues traditional feedback channels (emails, Slack messages, meetings) where context is often lost.

### 1.3 Why Study Verify in Prototyping Interactive Systems?

> [!TIP]
> **Three Pedagogical Reasons (per KTU 2024 OBE framework):**
> 1. **Industry Relevance** — Verify is a real, production-grade tool used by Fortune 500 design teams.
> 2. **Concept Bridge** — It operationalizes abstract HCI theories (Nielsen's Heuristics, Norman's Affordances) into a tangible workflow.
> 3. **Outcome Mapping** — It directly maps to Course Outcomes such as **CO4: "Evaluate prototypes through user testing methodologies."**

### 1.4 Key Terminology Used in Verify

| Terminology | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Prototype** | The design artifact (mock-up, wireframe, or HTML page) being tested. |
| **Reviewer / Tester** | A stakeholder or end-user invited to provide feedback. |
| **Pinpoint Annotation** | A comment anchored to a specific (x, y) pixel coordinate on the prototype. |
| **Verification URL** | A unique, shareable link granting access to a specific prototype. |
| **Activity Stream** | A chronological feed of all comments and interactions from reviewers. |
| **Resolution Status** | A flag (Resolved / Open) attached to each comment, indicating whether the design issue has been addressed. |
| **Iteration** | A new version of the prototype uploaded after incorporating feedback. |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

### 2.1 The Theoretical Foundation Underpinning ZURB's Verify

ZURB's Verify is not merely a commenting tool — it is an embodiment of several foundational HCI and UX research principles. Let us deconstruct the *Why* and *How* behind its design.

#### A. Distributed Cognition & Externalized Thinking

> **Why does Verify exist?**
> In classical usability testing, a researcher observes a user in a lab (think-aloud protocol). This is *expensive* and *non-scalable*. Verify externalizes the user's cognitive process — instead of narrating thoughts aloud, the user *externalizes* them as spatial annotations on the prototype. This is rooted in **Edwin Hutchins' Theory of Distributed Cognition**, which posits that cognitive processes are distributed across people and tools in the environment.

#### B. Asynchronous Remote Testing

> **How does Verify enable scale?**
> Verify is an **asynchronous**, **unmoderated** testing tool. Reviewers do not need a facilitator present. They click and comment at their convenience. This:
> - Eliminates scheduling overhead.
> - Increases the diversity of the test pool (geographically distributed testers).
> - Reduces the **Hawthorne Effect** (people behave differently when observed).

#### C. The Iterative Design Loop (ZURB's Design Process)

ZURB is famous for its "**Design Sprint**" methodology. Verify slots into the *Test* phase of this loop:

1. **Sketch** → low-fidelity ideas.
2. **Decide** → choose the best sketch.
3. **Prototype** → build a high-fidelity mock-up.
4. **Test (using Verify)** → collect real user feedback.
5. **Iterate** → refine and loop back to Step 3.

> [!NOTE]
